question: A small disc of mass $1\,g$ slides down a smooth hill of height $10\,m$ from rest and gets on to a plank of mass $100\,g$. Due to friction between the disc and the plank. The disc slows down and moves as one piece with the plank. The work done by the frictional force is approximately (Use, $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
The disc is at rest at the top of the hill.
$\therefore $ Potential energy of the disc, $U = mgh$
Let the velocity of the sliding disc before the friction is $v$.
The kinetic energy, $K = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$
From energy conservation,
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = mgh \Rightarrow v = \sqrt {2gh} $
The disc now gets onto the plank and slows down and moves as one piece with the plank. Let the velocity of the plank is $v'.$
From conservation of momentum,
$mv = (m + M)v' \Rightarrow v' = \frac{{m\sqrt {2gh} }}{{(m + M)}}$
$\therefore $ The kinetic energy of the plank,
${K_2} = \frac{1}{2}(m + M)v{'^2} = \frac{{{m^2} \times 2gh}}{{2(M + m)}}$
$ \Rightarrow {K_2} = \frac{{{m^2}gh}}{{(m + M)}}$
$\therefore $The total work done by the frictional force is
${W_f} = {K_1} - {K_2} = mgh - \frac{{{m^2}gh}}{{m + M}} = \frac{{Mmgh}}{{m + M}}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {\rm{ }}{W_f} = \frac{{100 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 1 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 10 \times 10}}{{(100 + 1) \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {W_f} = 0.1\,J$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A ball of mass $1.2\,kg$ moving with a velocity of $12\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ makes one dimensional collision with another stationary ball of mass $1.2\,kg$. If the coefficient of restitution is $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, then the ratio of the total kinetic energy of the balls after collision and the initial kinetic energy is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956350/preptracker/questions/q_108606_explanation_1764956350304.png $m = 1.2\,kg,{u_1} = 12\,m{s^{ - 1}},e = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}$
${m_2} = 1.2\,kg,{u_2} = 0$.
By momentum conservation,
$m\left( {{u_1} + {u_2}} \right) = m\left( {{v_1} + {v_2}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow {v_1} + {v_2} = 12\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Also, $e=\dfrac{v_{2}-v_{1}}{u_{1}-u_{2}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{v_{2}-v_{1}}{12}$
$\therefore \quad {v_2} - {v_1} = 6\sqrt 2 {\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From eq (1) and (2), we get
$\therefore {v_1} = (6 - 3\sqrt 2 )m/s,{v_2} = (6 + 3\sqrt 2 )m/s$
$\therefore {(K \cdot E)_f} = \frac{1}{2}m\left( {v_1^2 + v_2^2} \right)$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {{{(6 - 3\sqrt 2 )}^2} + {{(6 + 3\sqrt 2 )}^2}} \right] \times m$
$ = \frac{1}{2}m(36 + 18) \times 2 = 54m$
Also, ${(K.E)_i} = \frac{1}{2}mu_i^2 = \frac{1}{2}m{(12)^2} = 72\,m$
$\therefore \dfrac{(K E)_{f}}{(K E)_{i}}=\dfrac{54 m}{72 m}=3: 4$

question: A ball '$A$' of mass $1.2\,kg$ moving with a velocity of $8.4\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ makes one dimensional elastic collision with a ball '$B$' of mass $3.6\,kg$ at rest. The percentage of kinetic energy transferred by ball '$A$' to ball '$B$' is
explanation:
${m_1} = 1.2\,kg,{u_1} = 8.4\,m{s^{ - 1}},$ ${m_2} = 3.6\,kg,{u_2} = 0$,
$\therefore$ In elastic head on collision, percentage of kinetic energy transferred from $m_{1}$ to $m_{2}$ is
$\Delta K\% = \frac{{\Delta K}}{K} \times 100$
$ = \left[ {1 - {{\left( {\frac{{{m_1} - {m_2}}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}} \right)}^2}} \right] \times 100$
$=\left[1-\left(\dfrac{1.2-3.6}{1.2+3.6}\right)^{2}\right] \times 100=75 \%$

question: A ball falls freely from a height $h$ on a rigid horizontal plane. If the coefficient of restitution is $e$, then the total distance travelled by the ball before hitting the plane second time is
explanation:
Given, height $=h$,
Coefficient of restitution $=e$
The ball acquires the velocity $u=\sqrt{2 g h}$ when dropped from height $h$, just before the first impact. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956281/preptracker/questions/q_108522_explanation_1764956281158.png If collision on the floor is elastic, the ball will rebound with same velocity. But here coefficient of restitution is $e$; the velocity $v$ (less than $u$ ) is given by
$v = eu\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
In above case ball will rebound to a height $h_{1}$.
${h_1} = \frac{{{v^2}}}{{2g}} \Rightarrow {h_1} = \frac{{{e^2}{u^2}}}{{2g}}{\text{ }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( 2 \right)$
Initial height, $h=\dfrac{u^{2}}{2 g}$Put in $2^{\text {nd }}$ equation, $h_{1}=e^{2} h$
So, total distance travel by ball before $2^{\text {nd }}$ drop is $h+e^{2} h+e^{2} h=h\left(1+2 e^{2}\right)$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Partial Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A moving particle collides with a stationary particle of mass $\dfrac{1}{n}$ times the mass of moving particle. The fraction of its kinetic energy transferred to the stationary particle is
explanation:
Let the mass of the moving particle $=m$
$\therefore$ The mass of the stationary particle$=\dfrac{1}{n} \times m=\dfrac{m}{n}$
Also, let the speed of moving particle $=v$
Now, by the law of conservation of linear momentum https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956275/preptracker/questions/q_108520_explanation_1764956275385.png $\therefore \quad m v+\left(\dfrac{m}{n} \times 0\right)=m v^{\prime}+\dfrac{m}{n} v^{\prime \prime}$
$ \Rightarrow mv = m\left( {{v^\prime } + \frac{{{v^{\prime \prime }}}}{n}} \right) \Rightarrow v = {v^\prime } + \frac{{{v^{\prime \prime }}}}{n}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Also, coefficient of restitution,
$e=\dfrac{-\left(v^{\prime \prime}-v^{\prime}\right)}{(0-v)}$
Since, it is elastic collision,
$\therefore \quad e=1$
$\Rightarrow 1=\dfrac{-\left(v^{\prime \prime}-v^{\prime}\right)}{-v} \Rightarrow v^{\prime \prime}=v+v^{\prime}$
$ \Rightarrow {v^\prime } = {v^{\prime \prime }} - v\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
${v^{\prime \prime }} = \frac{{2nv}}{{(n + 1)}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
Now, kinetic energy transferred to the stationary particle
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{m}{n} \times {v^{\prime \prime 2}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 4 \right)$
Also, initial kinetic energy of mass $m$
$ = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 5 \right)$
$\therefore$ The dfraction of $K E$ that gets transferred to the stationary particle from the moving particle
$=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{m}{n} \times v^{\prime \prime 2}}{\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}}=\dfrac{v^{\prime \prime 2}}{n v^{2}}=\dfrac{1}{n}\left(\dfrac{v^{\prime \prime}}{v}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{4 n}{(n+1)^{2}}$
[From equ (3)]
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question:
Assertion: In an elastic collision of two billiard balls, both kinetic energy and linear momentum remain conserved.

Reason: During the collision of the balls, as the collision is elastic there is no exchange of energy. Therefore, both energy and momentum are conserved. The correct option among the following is
explanation:
In an elastic collision of two billiard balls, both kinetic energy and linear momentum remain conserved.In elastic collision, momentum is conserved but total energy of any one of ball is not conserved i.e. exchange of energy takes place.
Hence, Assertion is true but Reason is false.
So, the correct option is (3).

Assertion is true, Reason is true but Reason is not the correct explanation for Assertion.

question: An object $A$ of mass $20\,kg$ and travelling at $20 {~m} / {s}$ crashes into another object $B$ of mass $200\,kg$ and travelling at $10 {~m} / {s}$, in the same direction. After the collision, object $A$ bounces back in opposite direction at a speed of $10 {~m} / {s}$. The speed of the object $B$ after the collision is
explanation:
Given, $m_{A}=20 {~kg}, m_{B}=200 {~kg}$
$u_{A}=20 {~m} / {s}, u_{B}=10 {~m} / {s}$
After collision, final speed of $A$ and $B$ are $v_{A}$ and $v_{B}$ where,
$v_{A}=-10 {~m} / {s}$ 。
By using law of conservation of momentum,
$m_{A} u_{A}+m_{B} u_{B}=m_{A} v_{A}+m_{B} v_{B}$
$\Rightarrow 20 \times 20+200 \times 10=20(-10)+200 v_{B}$
$\Rightarrow 200 v_{B}=2600 \Rightarrow v_{B}=13 {~ms}^{-1}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Partial Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: Ball $P$ of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ collides head on with another ball $Q$ of mass $2 m$, at rest. If $v_{P}$ and $v_{Q}$ are the final velocities of $P$ and $Q$ respectively, after collision, then (Assume the coefficient of restitution is $1 / 3$ )
explanation:
Given, mass of ball $P$ and $Q$ be $m_{P}$ and $m_{Q}$ where, $m_{p}=m$ and $m_{Q}=2 m$
Initial velocities of $P$ and $Q$ are $u_{P}, u_{Q}$ i.e.$u_{p}=v$ and $u_{Q}=0$ and final velocities of $P$ and $Q$ are $v_{P}$ and $v_{Q}$
Coefficient of restitution, $e=1 / 3$
As we know that
$e=\dfrac{v_{P}-v_{Q}}{u_{Q}-u_{P}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{1}{3}=\dfrac{v_{P}-v_{Q}}{0-v} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{3}=\dfrac{v_{P}-v_{Q}}{-v}$
$ \Rightarrow {v_P} - {v_Q} = - \frac{v}{3}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
By using law of conservation of momentum, $m_{P} u_{P}+m_{Q} u_{Q}=m_{p} v_{P}+m_{Q} v_{Q}$
$\Rightarrow m v+2 m_{Q} \times 0=m v_{P}+2 m v_{Q}$
$ \Rightarrow v = {v_P} + 2{v_Q}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Now, solving we get,$v_{p}=\dfrac{v}{9}$ and $\Rightarrow v_{Q}=\dfrac{4 v}{9}$
Now, $\dfrac{v_{P}}{v_{Q}}=\dfrac{v / 9}{4 v / 9}=\dfrac{1}{4} \quad$ or $\quad \dfrac{v_{Q}}{v_{P}}=4$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Partial Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A bullet of mass $m$ moving horizontally with speed $v_{0}$ hits a wooden block of mass $M$ that is suspended from a massless string. The bullet gets lodged into the block and comes into halt. If the block-bullet combine swings to a maximum height $h$, then how much of the initial kinetic energy of the bullet is lost in the collision?
explanation:
The given situation is as shown in figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952415/preptracker/questions/q_108499_explanation_1764952415268.png Bullet hits block and gets lodged into the block. After this, block + bullet system moves to rise upto height $h$. Let, $v=$ common velocity of block and bullet with which mass system begins to rise.
By using conservation of linear momentum,
$\Rightarrow m v_{0}=(m+M) v$
So, $v=\dfrac{m v_{0}}{(m+M)}$
$\therefore$ Loss of $K E$
$=(K . E)_{i}-(K . E)_{f}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2}(m+M) v^{2}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2}(m+M) \dfrac{m^{2} v_{0}^{2}}{(m+M)^{2}}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}\left(1-\dfrac{m}{m+M}\right)=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}\left(\dfrac{M}{m+M}\right)$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Inelastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A moving body with a mass $m_{1}$ and velocity $u$ strikes a stationary body of mass $m_{2}$. The masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ should be in the ratio $\dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}$, so as to decrease the velocity of the first body to $\dfrac{2 u}{3}$ and giving a velocity of $v$ to $m_{2}$ assuming a perfectly elastic impact. Then, the ratio $m_{1} / m_{2}$ is
explanation:
The given situation is shown in the following figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956183/preptracker/questions/q_108486_explanation_1764956183262.png By using the law of conservation of linear momentum, We have,
$\Rightarrow m_{1}(u)+m_{2}(0)=m_{1}\left(\dfrac{2 u}{3}\right)+m_{2}(v)$
$\Rightarrow m_{1} u+0=\dfrac{2}{3} m_{1} u+m_{2} v$
$\Rightarrow m_{1} u-\dfrac{2}{3} m_{1} u=m_{2} v \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{3} m_{1} u=m_{2} v$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{m_1}}}{{{m_2}}} = \frac{{3v}}{u}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Since, the collision is perfectly elastic, then coefficient of restitution $=1$
$e=1$
$\left|\dfrac{v_{2}-v_{1}}{u_{1}-u_{2}}\right|=1 \Rightarrow \dfrac{v-\dfrac{2 u}{3}}{u-0}=1 \Rightarrow \dfrac{v-\dfrac{2 u}{3}}{u}=1$
$\Rightarrow v=\dfrac{5 u}{3}$
$\therefore$ From Eq. (1), we get
$\dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}=\dfrac{3 \times 5 u / 3}{u}=5$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: Ball $A$ of mass $50\,gm$ and speed $10 {~m} / {s}$ collides with other ball $B$ of mass $10\,gm$ and speed $15 {~m} / {s}$ travelling in opposite direction with each other. Determine the final speed of ball $B$ after collision, if the coefficient of restitution is $2 / 5$.
explanation:
Given, $m_{A}=50 {gm}, u_{A}=10 {~m} / {s}$, $m_{B}=10 {gm}, u_{B}=-15 {~m} / {s}$ and coefficient of restitution, $e=\dfrac{2}{5}$
The collision of ball $A$ and $B$ is shown as below, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957981/preptracker/questions/q_108480_explanation_1764957980634.png Velocity of second ball $B$ is given by the relation,
$v_{B}=\dfrac{m_{A}(1+e)}{m_{A}+m_{B}} u_{A}+\dfrac{m_{B}-e m_{A}}{m_{A}+m_{B}} u_{B}$
Putting the given values in above relation, we get
$\Rightarrow v_{B}=\dfrac{50\left(1+\dfrac{2}{5}\right)}{50+10} \times 10+\dfrac{10-\dfrac{2}{5} \times 50}{50+10}(-15)$
$\Rightarrow v_{B}=\dfrac{50 \times 7 \times 10}{60 \times 5}+\dfrac{10 \times 15}{60} {~m} / {s}$
$\Rightarrow v_{B}=\dfrac{70}{6}+\dfrac{15}{6}=\dfrac{85}{6} {~m} / {s}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Partial Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A bouncing ball of mass $200\,g$ falls from the height of $5\,m$ on a horizontal ground. After every impact with the ground, the velocity of the ball decreases by $\dfrac{1}{2}$ times. The total momentum, it imparts on the ground after 3 impacts is (Let $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
Given, $m_{\text {ball }}=200 {gm}=0.2 {~kg}$
$h=5 m$
$\therefore$ Velocity of ball just before the first impact,
$v_{1}=\sqrt{2 g h}=\sqrt{2 \times 10 \times 5}=10 {~m} / {s}$
The recoil velocity of the ball just after first impact,
$v_{1}^{\prime}=\dfrac{v_{1}}{2}=\dfrac{10}{2}=5 {~m} / {s}$
$\therefore$ Momentum imparted by the ball in first impact,
$p_{1}=m v_{1}-m\left(-v_{1}^{\prime}\right)$
$=m v_{1}+m v_{1}^{\prime}=0.2 \times 10+0.2 \times 5 {~kg}-{m} / {s}$
$p_{1}=3 {~kg} {~m} / {s}$
Similarly, velocity of the ball at the time of second impact and its recoil velocity will be
$5 {~m} / {s}$ and $\dfrac{5}{2} {~m} / {s}$ respectively.
Hence, momentum imparted by ball in second impact,
$p_{2}=m \times 5-m\left(-\dfrac{5}{2}\right)$
$=0.2 \times 5+0.2 \times \dfrac{5}{2}=1.5=\dfrac{3}{2} {~kg} {~m} / {s}$
Again, velocity of ball at the time of third impact and its recoil velocity will be $\dfrac{5}{2} {~m} / {s}$ and
$\dfrac{5}{4} {~m} / {s}$ respectively.
Hence, momentum imparted by ball in third impact,
$p_{3}=m\left(\dfrac{5}{2}\right)-m\left(-\dfrac{5}{4}\right)$
$p_{3}=0.2 \times \dfrac{5}{2}+0.2 \times \dfrac{5}{4}=\dfrac{3}{4} {~kg}-{m} / {s}$
$\therefore$ Total momentum imparted by the ball to the ground,
$p=p_{1}+p_{2}+p_{3}=3+\dfrac{3}{2}+\dfrac{3}{4}=\dfrac{21}{4} {~kg} {~m} / {s}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Partial Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A ball of mass $1\,kg$ moving along $X$-direction collides elastically with a stationary ball of mass $m$. The first ball (mass $=1 {~kg}$ ) recoils at right angle to its original direction of motion. If the second ball starts moving at an angle $30^{\circ}$ with the $X$-axis, then the value of $m$ must be
explanation:
Motion of the balls before and after impact are shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952401/preptracker/questions/q_108474_explanation_1764952401316.png According to the law of conservation of momentum,
$1 \times {u_1} = m{v_2}\cos 30^\circ $ ($X$-axis)
$ \Rightarrow {u_1} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}m{v_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
and $\quad 1 \times v_{1}=m v_{2} \sin 30^{\circ} \quad(Y-$ axis $)$
${v_1} = \frac{1}{2}m{v_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From kinetic energy conservation,
$\frac{1}{2}u_1^2 = \frac{1}{2}v_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}mv_2^2 \Rightarrow u_1^2 - v_1^2 = mv_2^2\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
On putting the values of $u_{1}$ and $v_{1}$, we get
$\dfrac{3}{4} m^{2} v_{2}^{2}-\dfrac{1}{4} m^{2} v_{2}^{2}=m v_{2}^{2} \Rightarrow m=2 {~kg}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Elastic Collision in Two Dimensions

question: A ball moving with a velocity $v$, collides head on with a stationary second ball of same mass. After the collision, the velocity of the first ball is reduced to $0.15 v$. The kinetic energy of the system is decreased nearly by
explanation:
Motion of the balls before and after the collision is as shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956155/preptracker/questions/q_108472_explanation_1764956155483.png $\Rightarrow$ Momentum is conserved,
$m_{1} u_{1}+m_{2} u_{2}=m_{1} v_{1}+m_{1} v_{2}$
Given, $m_{1}=m_{2}=m ; u_{1}=v$ and $u_{2}=0$,
also, $v_{1}=0.15 v$
$m v=m(0.15 v)+m v_{2}$
$v-0.15 v=v_{2}$
$ \Rightarrow 0.85v = {v_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Here, ${(KE)_i} = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}{\text{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
$(K E)_{f}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{1}^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{2}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m(0.15 v)^{2}$
$ + \frac{1}{2}m{(0.85v)^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
The decrement in kinetic energy is
$\Delta K E=(K E)_{i}-(K E)_{f}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \triangle K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
$-\left[\dfrac{1}{2} m(0 \cdot 15 v)^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} m(0.85 v)^{2}\right]$
$\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}\left[ {1 - {{(0.15)}^2} + {{0.85}^2}} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 4 \right)$
% change in kinetic energy $=\dfrac{\Delta K E}{(K E)_{i}} \times 100$
$=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}\left[1-(0.15)^{2}-(0.85)^{2}\right]}{\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}} \times 100$
$\Rightarrow \%$ decrease in $KE = 25.5\% \approx 25\% $
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Partial Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question:
A block of mass $4 m$ travelling at a velocity $v_{1}$ in
$X$-direction on a frictionless horizontal plane makes a head-on collision with another block of mass $2 m$ travelling in opposite direction with a velocity $v_{2}$. After collision, both the blocks travel as a single block along positive $X$-direction with a final velocity $5 v_{2}$. The ratio of velocities $v_{1} / v_{2}$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952408/preptracker/questions/q_108471_explanation_1764952408222.png Momentum before collision,
$p_{1}=4 m v_{1}-2 m v_{2}$
Momentum after collision,
$p_{2}=6 m\left(5 V_{2}\right)=30 m v_{2}$
By applying conservation of momentum, we have
$4 m v_{1}-2 m v_{2}=30 m v_{2}$
$4 m v_{1}=32 m v_{2}$
$\dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\dfrac{32}{4}=8$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

After collision $m_{2}$ starts moving in a direction which makes an angle $30^{\circ}$ to the $X$-axis as shown in the figure. If the kinetic energy of the combined system of these two particles reduces by $50 \%$, then the ratio of the masses $m_{2} / m_{1}$ is

question: Head on collision takes place between two solid spheres denoted as 1 and 2 . The initial velocities of the spheres are $u_{1}=3 {~m} / {s}$ and $u_{2}$ $=1.5 {~m} / {s}$ and the final velocities are $v_{1}=2.5$ ${m} / {s}$ and $v_{2}=3.5 {~m} / {s}$. The coefficient of restitution between the materials of the spheres is nearly
explanation:
Coefficient of restitution,
$e=\dfrac{\text { Relative velocity of separation }}{\text { Relative velocity of approach }}$
$\Rightarrow e=\dfrac{v_{2}-v_{1}}{u_{1}-u_{2}}=\dfrac{3.5-2.5}{3-1.5}=\dfrac{1}{1.5}=\dfrac{2}{3}=0.67$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Partial Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A ball of mass $0.5\,kg$ is dropped from a height of $40\,m$. The ball hits the ground and rises to a height of $10\,m.$ The impulse imparted to the ball during its collision with the ground is (Take $g = 9.8\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957966/preptracker/questions/q_108465_explanation_1764957965429.png ${v_1} = \sqrt {2g{h_1}} = \sqrt {2 \times 9.8 \times 40} = 28\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
and ${v_2} = \sqrt {2g{h_2}} = \sqrt {2 \times 9.8 \times 10} = 14\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Impulse $=\Delta \vec{p}=m\left(\vec{v}_{f}-\vec{v}_{i}\right)=m\left(\vec{v}_{2}-\vec{v}_{1}\right)$
$ = \frac{1}{2}(14 - ( - 28)) = 21\,Ns$
subject: Physics

question:
During an elastic collision between two bodies, which of the following statements are correct?
I. The initial kinetic energy is equal to the final kinetic energy of the system.
II. The linear momentum is conserved.
III. The kinetic energy during $\Delta t$ (the collision time) is not conserved.
explanation:
I, II and III
During the course of collision, a part of the kinetic energy of the system is converted into elastic potential energy which is recovered completely at the end of collision. So correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A simple pendulum of length $1 m$ has a wooden bob of mass $1\,kg$ . It is struck by a bullet of mass  ${10^{ - 2}}\,kg$  moving with a speed of  $2 \times {10^2}\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. The bullet gets embedded into the bob. The height to which the bob rises before swinging back is (use $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$ )
explanation:
Mass of wooden bob, $m = 1\,kg$
Mass of bullet, $m' = {10^{ - 2}}\,kg$
$u = 2 \times {10^2}\,m/s$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951616/preptracker/questions/q_36259_explanation_1764951615560.png
Let the height raised is $h$.
Let the velocity of bob and bullet system after striking is $v$.
Using conservation of momentum,
$m^{\prime} u=\left(m+m^{\prime}\right) v$ and $h=\dfrac{v^{2}}{2 g}$
$v=\dfrac{m^{\prime} u}{m+m^{\prime}}$
$h=\dfrac{1}{2 g} \times\left(\dfrac{m^{\prime} u}{m+m^{\prime}}\right)^{2}$
$h=\dfrac{1}{2 \times 10} \times\left(\dfrac{10^{-2} \times 2 \times 10^{2}}{1+10^{-2}}\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow h \simeq 0.2 m$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Collisions

question: An elevator platform is going up at a speed $20 {~ms}^{-1}$ and during its upward motion a small ball of 50 $g$ mass falling in downward direction strikes the platform elastically at a speed $5 {~ms}^{-1}$. Find the speed   with which the ball rebounds.
explanation:
The situation is analysed in the figure.
We can consider mass of platform to be very large compared to that of ball, so we have https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951653/preptracker/questions/q_36208_explanation_1764951652769.png ${\vec v_1} = {\vec u_1}e = \left( {\frac{{{v_2} - {v_1}}}{{{u_2} - {u_1}}}} \right) = \left( {\frac{{{v_2} - {u_1}}}{{{u_1} - \left( { - {u_2}} \right)}}} \right)$
$\vec{v}_{2}=2 \vec{u}_{1}-\vec{u}_{2}$
Thus rebound velocity of ball is
$\vec{v}_{2}=2 \times 20-(-5)=45 {~ms}^{-1} \text { upward }$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question:
A ball of mass 1 $kg$ moving with a velocity of $5 {~m} / {s}$ collides elastically with rough ground at an angle $\theta$ with the vertical as shown in figure. What can be the minimum coefficient of friction if ball rebounds vertically after collision?
(${\rm{Given}}\,\,\tan \theta = 2$)
explanation:
From impulse-momentum theorem.
$\begin{align*}& \int N d t=m(v+5 \cos \theta) \tag{1}\\& \int f d t=m 5 \sin \theta\end{align*}$
$\int N dt = m5\sin \theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mu m(1+5 \cos \theta)=m 5 \sin \theta$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951651/preptracker/questions/q_36191_explanation_1764951651101.png According to Newton's law of restitution,$\quad v = e\,\,5\cos \theta $
Solve to get $\mu=1$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Partial Elastic Collision in Two Dimensions

question: Disc $A$ of mass $m$ collides with stationary disc $B$ of mass $2\,\,m$ as shown in figure. Find the value of coefficient of restitution for which the two discs move in perpendicular direction after collision.
explanation:
Velocity of disc '$A$' in tangent direction
$\begin{equation*}v_{1, t}=u \sin 45^{\circ}=\dfrac{u}{\sqrt{2}} \tag{1}\end{equation*}$
The disc ' $B$ ' will move along common normal direction only. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951649/preptracker/questions/q_36183_explanation_1764951649513.png And according to equation both the discs move perpendicular to each other. It means disc ' $A$ ' should move along common tangent direction.
Hence $v_{1}=\dfrac{u}{\sqrt{2}}$
along common tangent direction.
Now conserving linear momentum along common normal direction $m \cdot \frac{u}{{\sqrt 2 }} + 0 = m \times 0 + 2m{v_{2,n}}$
or ${v_{2,n}} = \frac{u}{{\sqrt 2 }}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\quad {\rm{(2)}}$
or velocity of disc $B^{\prime}$ is $\frac{u}{{2\sqrt 2 }}$ along common normal.
Now coefficient of restitution
$e=\dfrac{\text { Relative velocity of separation }}{\text { Relative velocity of approach }}$$=\dfrac{v_{2, n}-0}{u \cos 45^{\circ}}=\dfrac{u / 2 \sqrt{2}}{u / \sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Partial Elastic Collision in Two Dimensions

question: A particle is moving with velocity $u=2 {~m} / {s}$ towards a heavy wall moving towards the particle with constant speed $v_{0}=1 {~m} / {s}$ as shown in the figure. Assuming collision to be elastic, find the velocity of the particle  immediately after the collision.
explanation:
Let the velocity of the particle immediately after the collision be $v_{1}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951647/preptracker/questions/q_36167_explanation_1764951646547.png For calculating velocity of the ball just after collision, we use coefficient of restitution equation
$e = \left( {\frac{{{v_2} - {v_1}}}{{{u_1} - {u_2}}}} \right)$
$ = \frac{{{\text{ relative velocity of separation }}}}{{{\text{ relative velocity of approach }}}}$
This equation is applicable along the direction of impulsive force.
Here $u_{1}=u, u_{2}=-v_{0}$
$v_{1}=-v$ and
$v_{2}=-v_{0}$
Here $v_{2}=-v_{0}$.
since the wall being very heavy (infinite mass).
moves with constant velocity.
Hence $e = \frac{{ - {v_0} - ( - v)}}{{u - \left( { - {v_0}} \right)}} = \frac{{v - {v_0}}}{{u + {v_0}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
As collision is elastic.Putting the value of $e = 1$,we obtain
$1=\dfrac{v-v_{0}}{u+v_{0}} \Rightarrow v=u+2 v_{0}$
Putting $u=2 {~m} / {s}, v_{0}=1 {~m} / {s}$,we get
$v=2+2(1)=4 {~m} / {sec}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A boy throws a ball with speed $u$ in a well of depth 14 $m$ as shown. On bounce with bottom of the well the speed of the ball gets halved. What should be the minimum value of   $u$  such that the ball may be able to reach his hand again? Assume that his hands are at 1 $m$ height from top of the well while throwing and catching.
explanation:
Ball hits ground with velocity
$v=\sqrt{u^{2}+2 g(15)}=\sqrt{u^{2}+300}$
Ball bounces back with velocity $\dfrac{v}{2}=\dfrac{\sqrt{u^{2}+300}}{2}$
If the ball just rises by 15 m then by conservation of energy:
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{2} m\left(\dfrac{v}{2}\right)^{2}=m g(15) \\\Rightarrow & \dfrac{1}{2} m\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{u^{2}+300}}{2}\right)^{2}=m g(15) \Rightarrow u=30 {~ms}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: For shown situation, if collision between block ${A}$ and ${B}$ is perfectly elastic, then  find  the maximum energy stored in spring  is.
explanation:
At maximum compression of spring, velocities of block ${B}$ and${C}$ are same (say ${v_{0}}$ ) then by conservation of linear momentum
${3(2)=(3+6) v_{0} \Rightarrow v_{0}=\dfrac{2}{3} {~m} / {s}}$
At this instant energy stored in spring$=\dfrac{1}{2}(3)(2)^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2}(3+6)\left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)^{2}=6-2=4 {~J}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: The friction coefficient between the horizontal surface and blocks ${A}$ and ${B}$ are ${\dfrac{1}{15}}$ and ${\dfrac{2}{15}}$ respectively. The collision between the blocks is perfectly elastic. Find the separation  between the two blocks when they come to rest.
explanation:
Velocity of block ${A}$ just before collision
${v_A} = \sqrt {u_A^2 - 2\mu gx} $
$ = \sqrt {16 - 2\left( {\frac{1}{{15}}} \right)(10)(2)} = \sqrt {\frac{{40}}{3}} $
Velocity of Block ${B}$ just after collision ${v_{B}=v_{A}=\sqrt{\dfrac{40}{3}}}$
Velocity of Block ${A}$ just after collision ${=0}$
Total distance travelled by block
$B=\dfrac{v_{B}^{2}}{2 \mu g}=\dfrac{40 / 3}{2\left(\dfrac{2}{15}\right)(10)}=5 {~m}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A small ball moves towards right with a velocity ${v}$ starting from ${A}$. It collides with the wall and returns back and continues to and fro motion. If the average speed for the first trip is ${(2 / 3) v}$, the coefficient of restitution of impact  is
explanation:
The ball moves from ${A}$ to ${B}$ with a speed ${v_{1}=v}$ and returns from ${B}$ to ${A}$ with speed ${v_{2} \Rightarrow v_{2}=e v_{1}}$
$v_{a v}=\dfrac{A B+B A}{\dfrac{A B}{v_{1}}+\dfrac{B A}{v_{2}}}=\dfrac{2 v}{3}$
$\left( {AB = BA} \right)$
Solve to get ${e=0.5=5 \times 10^{-1}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Partial Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: Two particles $A$ and $B$ of masses $3\,kg$ and $6\,kg$ respectively, are incident making angles $45^{\circ}$ and $120^{\circ}$ with a same normal. These particles moving with speeds $8\,m{\rm{/}}s$ and $6\,m{\rm{/}}s,$ collide elastically at the origin. After the collision, particle $B$ moves with a speed of   (Take $\sqrt{2}=1.41$ and $\sqrt{3}=1.73$ )
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951610/preptracker/questions/q_35925_explanation_1764951609566.png Applying law of conservation of linear momentum in $\mathrm{X}$ and $\mathrm{Y}$ direction of the system,
$3\left( {8\cos 45^\circ } \right) + 6\left( {6\cos 30^\circ } \right) = 6\left( {{v_1}\cos 45^\circ } \right)$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ + 3\left( {{v_2}\cos 30^\circ } \right)$
$12\sqrt 2 + 18\sqrt 3 = {v_1}3\sqrt 2 + {v_2}\frac{{3\sqrt 3 }}{2}$
$\therefore \quad 3\sqrt 2 {v_1} + \frac{{3\sqrt 3 }}{2}{v_2} = 48.06\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Also,
$6\left( {6\sin 30^\circ } \right) - 3\left( {8\sin 45^\circ } \right) = 6\left( {{v_1}\sin 45^\circ } \right)$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, - 3\left( {{v_2}\sin 30^\circ } \right)$
$\therefore \quad 18 - 12\sqrt 2 = 3\sqrt 2 {v_1} - \frac{3}{2}{v_2}$
$\therefore 3\sqrt 2 {v_1} - \frac{3}{2}{v_2} = 1.08$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
On solving equation (1) and (2), we get,
$\frac{3}{2}(\sqrt 3 + 1){v_2} = 46.98$
${v_2} = \frac{{46.98 \times 2}}{{3 \times 2.73}}$
$\therefore \quad {v_2} = 11.47\,m/s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Elastic Collision in Two Dimensions

question: A particle of mass ' ${m}$ ' is moving horizontally ${w i}$ th a speed ' ${v}$ ' towards a rigid wall which is moving in the opposite direction with a constant velocity ${u}$. Assuming elastic impact between the wall and the particle, the work done by the wall in reflecting the particle is :
explanation:
Since the collision between the particle and the wall is elastic,
Velocity of separation ${=}$ velocity of approach$v^{\prime}-u=v+u$ $\left[v^{\prime}=\text { velocity of particle after collision }\right]$
Work done ${(W)}$ by the wall in reflecting the particle will be given by change in the $K.E$. of the particle
$ - W = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} - \frac{1}{2}m{v^{\prime 2}} = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} - \frac{1}{2}m{(v + 2u)^2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} - \frac{1}{2}m\left( {{v^2} + 4{u^2} + 4uv} \right)$$ = \frac{1}{2}m\left( { - 4{u^2} - 4uv} \right)$
$ = \frac{{ - 4}}{2}mu(u + v) = - 2mu(u + v)$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} W = 2mu(u + v)$.
So correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: An object ${A}$ of mass 2$kg$ is moving with a velocity of ${3 {~m} {~s}^{-1}}$ and collides head on with an object ${B}$ of mass 1 $kg$ moving in the opposite direction with a velocity of ${4 {~m} {~s}^{-1}}$. After collision both objects coalesce so that they move with a common velocity ${v}$ equal to
explanation:
Given : ${m_{A}=2 {~kg}, v_{A}=3 {~m} / {s}}$,$m_{B}=1 {~kg}, v_{B}=4 {~m} / {s}$
Applying momentum conservation$m_{A} v_{A}-m_{B} v_{B}=\left(m_{A}+m_{B}\right) v$${[\because A}$ and ${B}$ have opposite velocities]
${\Rightarrow 2(3)-1(4)=(2+1) v \Rightarrow v=\dfrac{2}{3} {~m} / {s}}$.
So correct option is(1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Inelastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A mass $m_{1}$ moves with a great velocity. It strikes another mass $m_{2}$ at rest in a head on collision. It comes back along its path with low speed, after collision. Then
explanation: $v_{1}=\dfrac{\left(m_{1}-m_{2}\right) u_{1}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}$ As $v_{1}$ is negative and less than $u_{1}$, therefore, ${m_1} < {m_2}.$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Partial Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A sphere of mass $m$, moving with velocity $V$, enters a hanging bag of sand and stops. If the mass of the bag is $M$ and it is raised by height $h$, then the velocity of the sphere was -
explanation:
By the conservation of linear momentum Initial momentum of sphere $=$ Final momentum of system
$mV = (m + M){v_{sys}},\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
If the system rises up to height $h$ then by the conservation of energy
$\frac{1}{2}(m + M)v_{{\rm{sys}}{\rm{.}}}^2 = (m + M)gh$
$ \Rightarrow {v_{{\rm{sys}}{\rm{. }}}} = \sqrt {2gh} $
Substituting this value in equation (1)
$V = \left( {\frac{{m + M}}{m}} \right)\sqrt {2gh} $
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Inelastic Collision in One Dimension

question: When two bodies stick together after collision, the collision is said to be
explanation: When the two bodies stick together after collision, then it is perfectly inelastic collision and in this case, the coefficient of restitution e is equal to zero.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Inelastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A ball is allowed to fall from a height of $10\,m$. If there is $40 \%$ loss of energy due to impact, then after one impact ball will go up to
explanation:
Kinetic energy of ball when reaching the ground
$ = mgh = mg \times 10$
Kinetic energy after the impact
$ = \frac{{60}}{{100}} \times mg \times 10 = 6\,mg$
If the ball rises to a height $h$, then $mgh = 6\,mg$.
Hence, $h = 6\,m.$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Partial Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A particle of mass $10\,kg$ moving eastwards with a speed $5\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ collides with another particle of the same mass moving north-wards with the same speed $5\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. The two particles coalesce on collision. The new particle of mass $20\,kg$ will move in the north-east direction with velocity
explanation:
Here $\hat imv + \hat jmv = 2\,m\vec V$
That is $\vec V = \frac{v}{2}(\hat i + \hat j)$
Hence $V = \frac{v}{2} \times \sqrt 2 = \frac{v}{{\sqrt 2 }} \cdot \left[ {} \right.$Here $\left. {v = 5\,m{s^{ - 1}}} \right]$
So, $V = \frac{5}{{\sqrt 2 }}\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Inelastic Collision in Two Dimensions

question: A ball falling freely from a height of $4.9\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ hits a horizontal surface. If $e=3 / 4$, then the ball will hit the surface second time after
explanation:
From equation of motion,
$v^{2}=u^{2}+2 g h$
where, $v$ is final velocity, $u$ is initial velocity and $h$ is height,
Given, $h = 4.9\,m,g = 9.8\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
and $u=0$
So, $v=\sqrt{2 g h}$
$ = \sqrt {2 \times 9.8 \times 4.9} = 9.8\,\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Coefficient of restitution $(e)$ of an object is a unique dfractional value representing the ratio of relative velocities before and after an impact.
$\therefore \quad v^{\prime}=\dfrac{3}{4} \times 9.8$
Time taken from first bounce to second bounce
$t = \frac{{2v'}}{g} \Rightarrow t = 2 \times \frac{3}{4} \times 9.8 \times \frac{1}{{9.8}} = 1.5\,s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Partial Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A body of mass $m$ is moving towards east and another body of equal mass and speed is moving towards the north. If after collision both stick together, then their speed after collision would be
explanation:
In inelastic collision, most of the kinetic energy of the body is lost in the form heat. Thus, in inelastic collision kinetic energy is not conserved but total energy and momentum are still conserved. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955921/preptracker/questions/q_35726_explanation_1764955921257.png Hence, we have $m v \hat{i}+m v \hat{j}=2 m v$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & \left|2 m v^{\prime}\right|=m|v \hat{i}+v \hat{j}| \\\Rightarrow & 2 m v^{\prime}=m \sqrt{v^{2}+v^{2}} \\\Rightarrow & v^{\prime}=\dfrac{v \sqrt{2}}{2}=\dfrac{v}{\sqrt{2}}\end{array}$
subject: Physics

question: A bullet of mass $20\,g$ and moving with $600\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ collides with a block of mass $4\,kg$ hanging with the string. What is velocity of bullet when it comes out of block, if block rises to height $0.2 \mathrm{~m}$ after collision?
explanation:
According to the law of conservation of linear momentum,
$m_{1} v=m_{1} v_{1}+m_{2} v_{2}$
where, $v$ is the velocity of bullet before the collision, $v_{1}$ is velocity of the bullet after the collision and $v_{2}$ is the velocity of block.
Given, ${m_1} = 20g = 0.02\,kg,$
$v = 600\,m{s^{ - 1}},\,\,{m_2} = 4\,kg$
and $h = 0.2\,m$
$\therefore \quad 0.02 \times 600 = 0.02{v_1} + 4{v_2}$
$\left( {\because {v_2} = \sqrt {2gh} = \sqrt {2 \times 10 \times 0.2} = 2\,m{s^{ - 1}}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \quad 0.02\,\,{v_1} = 12 - 8$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {v_1} = \frac{4}{{0.02}} = 200\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Partial Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: Which of the following is not an example of perfectly inelastic collision?
explanation: Whenever there is a collision between two bodies, the total momentum of the bodies remains conserved. If after the collision of two bodies, the total kinetic energy of the bodies remains the same as it was before collision, then it a perfectly elastic collision. A ball bearing striking another ball bearing is an example of elastic collision. If two bodies stick together after the collision, then the collision is said to be perfectly inelastic collision. Options (a), (b) and (c) are examples of perfectly inelastic collisions.

options.text: A bullet fired into a block if bullet gets embedded into block
Capture of electrons by an atom
A man jumping on to a moving boat
A ball bearing striking another ball bearing

subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Inelastic Collision in Two Dimensions

question:
For a system to follow the law of conservation of linear momentum during a collision, the condition is
(i) total external force acting on the system is zero.
(ii) total external force acting on the system is finite and time of collision is negligible.
(iii) total internal force acting on the system is zero.
explanation:
From Newton's second law,
$F=\dfrac{d p}{d t}$
If $F=0$, then $\dfrac{d p}{d t}=0$
$\Rightarrow p=$ constant
Thus, if total external force acting on the system is zero during collision,then the linear momentum of the system remains conserved.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Collisions

question: A body of mass $2\,kg$ makes an elastic collision with another body at rest and continues to move in the original direction with one-fourth its original speed. The mass of the second body which collides with the first body is
explanation:
Let initial velocity of first body be $u$.
For second body, initial velocity,
$u_{2}=0$ (at rest),
Mass of first body, ${m_1} = 2\,kg,$
Mass of second body $=m_{2}$ and $v_{1}=\dfrac{u}{4}$
Law of conservation of linear momentum gives$\begin{align*}& & m_{1} u_{1}+m_{2} u_{2} & =m_{1} v_{1}+m_{2} v_{2} \\\Rightarrow & & 2 u+0 & =2\left(\dfrac{u}{4}\right)+m_{2} v_{2} \\\Rightarrow & & m_{2} v_{2} & =\dfrac{3 u}{2} \tag{1}\end{align*}
$Conservation of kinetic energy gives
$\dfrac{1}{2} m_{1} u_{1}^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} m_{2} u_{2}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m_{1} v_{1}^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} m_{2} v_{2}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow$
$\dfrac{1}{2} m_{1} u+\dfrac{1}{2} m_{2}(0)=\dfrac{1}{2} m_{1}\left(\dfrac{u}{4}\right)^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} m_{2} v_{2}^{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {m_2}v_2^2 = \frac{{15{u^2}}}{8}$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
${m_2} = 1.2\,kg.$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A ball falls from a height of $20\,m$ on the floor and rebounds to a height of  $5\,m.$ Time of contact is $0.02 s$. Find the acceleration during impact
explanation:
According to Newton's second law of motion, the force acting on a body is equal to the rate of change of momentum during the impact
$F=\dfrac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}$
Also, $F=m a \Rightarrow m a=\dfrac{p_{2}-p_{1}}{\Delta t}$
$\Rightarrow a=\dfrac{m v_{2}-\left(-m v_{1}\right)}{m \Delta t}=\dfrac{v_{2}+v_{1}}{\Delta t}$
Given, ${h_2} = 20\,m,\,\,{h_1} = 5\,m$
and $\Delta t=0.02 s$
$a=\sqrt{2 g \times h_{2}}+\sqrt{2 g \times \dot{n}_{1}}$
So, $\quad a=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 \times 10 \times 20}+\sqrt{2 \times 10 \times 5}}{0.02}$
$\because \left( {v = \sqrt {2gh} } \right)$
$ = \frac{{20 + 10}}{{0.02}} = 1500\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Partial Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A particle of mass $m_{1}$ moves with velocity $v_{1}$ and collides with another particle at rest of equal mass. The velocity of the second particle after the elastic collision is
explanation:
Given, mass $m_{1}=m_{2}$,
velocity, $u_{1}=v_{1}$ and $u_{2}=0$
For elastic collision,
$v_{2}=\left(\dfrac{m_{2}-m_{1}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\right) u_{2}+\dfrac{2 m_{1} u_{1}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}$
After putting the given values, we get
$\begin{aligned}v_{2} & =\dfrac{2 m_{1} v_{1}}{2 m_{1}} \\\Rightarrow v_{2} & =v_{1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A block $C$ of mass $m$ is moving with velocity $v_{0}$ and collides elastically with block $A$ of mass $m$ and connected to another block $B$ of mass $2 m$ through spring of spring constant $k$. What is the value of $k$, if $x_{0}$ is compression of spring when, velocity of $A$ and $B$ is same?
explanation:
Using the law of conservation of linear momentum, we have
$m v_{0}=m v+2 m v \Rightarrow v=v_{0} / 3$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957249/preptracker/questions/q_35698_explanation_1764957249248.png Using law of conservation of energy, we have
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} k x_{0}^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2}(3 m) v^{2}$
where, $x_{0}$ is compression in the string.
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & m v_{0}^{2}=k x_{0}^{2}+(3 m) \dfrac{v_{0}^{2}}{9} \\\Rightarrow & k x_{0}^{2}=m v_{0}^{2}-\dfrac{m v_{0}^{2}}{3} \\\Rightarrow & k x_{0}^{2}=\dfrac{2 m v_{0}^{2}}{3} \\\therefore & k=\dfrac{2 m v_{0}^{2}}{3 x_{0}^{2}}\end{array}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A ball of $0.5\,kg$ collided with wall at $30^{\circ}$ and bounced back elastically. The speed of ball was $12\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$ The contact remained for $1\,s.$ What is the force applied by wall on ball?
explanation:
Given, $m = 0.5\,kg,\,\,v = 12\,m{s^{ - 1}},$
$\Delta t=1 s$ and $\theta=30^{\circ}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951566/preptracker/questions/q_35694_explanation_1764951566115.png Force applied by wall on ball,
$F=\dfrac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}$ or $F=\dfrac{\left(p_{f}\right)_{H}-\left(p_{i}\right)_{H}}{\Delta t}$
$\because$ In this elastic collision, final and initial velocity will be same but direction change.
$\left(p_{f}\right)_{H}=m v \cos \theta$
and$\left(p_{i}\right)_{H}=-m v \cos \theta$
$\because F=\dfrac{m v \cos \theta+m v \cos \theta}{\Delta t}$
$F=\dfrac{2 m v \cos \theta}{\Delta t}$
$F=\dfrac{2 \times 0.5 \times 12 \times \cos 30^{\circ}}{1}$
$F=6 \sqrt{3} N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Elastic Collision in Two Dimensions

question: Two objects of mass $m$ each moving with speed  $u$ $\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ collide at $90^{\circ}$, then final momentum is (assume collision is inelastic)
explanation:
Speed of objects $ = u\,\,\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Since, both objects collide with $90^{\circ}$.Hence, by the law of conservation of momentum,Total moment before collision
$=$ Total momentum after collision
$|m u \hat{i}+m u \hat{j}|=p_{f}$
$\sqrt{m^{2} u^{2}+m^{2} u^{2}}=p_{f}$
$\Rightarrow \quad p_{f}=\sqrt{2} m u$

explanation: When object of mass $20\,kg$ is moving with speed $10\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ in west direction collide with object of mass $10\,kg$ and stick together, hence it is perfectly inelastic collision. In inelastic collision, only momentum is conserved.

options.text: There kinetic energy is conserved as it is inelastic collision.
There kinetic energy is conserved as it is elastic collision.
There momentum is conserved as it is inelastic collision.
There momentum is conserved as it is elastic collision.

question: A quarter horse power motor runs at a speed of $600\,rpm.$ Assuming $40 \%$ efficiency, the work done by the motor in one rotation will be
explanation:
Given, power
$=\dfrac{1}{4} H P=\dfrac{1}{4} \times 746$
$=186.5 \mathrm{~W}$
$(\because 1 H P=746 W)$
Power used to do work $=40 \% \times 186.5$
$ = \frac{{40}}{{100}} \times 186.5$
$ \Rightarrow \quad P = 74.6\,W$
${\rm{ Speed }} = 600\,\,rpm$
$ = \frac{{600}}{{60}} = 10\,\,rps$
$ = 2\pi (10)\,rad\,{s^{ - 1}}$
$ = 20\,\pi rad\,{s^{ - 1}}$
$40\,\% \,\, \times P = \frac{W}{t}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad 74.6 = \frac{W}{{2\pi \times \frac{1}{{20\pi }}{\rm{ rotations }}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad W = 7.46\,J$

question: Two particles of masses ${m_{1}}$ and ${m_{2}}$ move with initial velocities ${u_{1}}$ and ${u_{2}}$, respectively. On collision, one of the particles gets excited to a higher level, after absorbing energy ${\varepsilon}$. If the final velocities of particles are ${v_{1}}$ and ${v_{2}}$, then
explanation:
Total mechanics energy will always be conserved, so,
${K . E_{\cdot_{\text {initial }}}=K . E_{\cdot f \text { final }}+}$ Excitation energy
${\dfrac{1}{2} m_{1} u_{1}^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} m_{2} u_{2}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m_{1} v_{1}^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} m_{2} v_{2}^{2}+\varepsilon}$
So correct option is (2).

question: A particle of mass ${m}$ moves with ${a}$ velocity of ${v_{0}=20 {~m} / {s}}$ towards a wall, which is moving with velocity ${v=5 {~m} / {s}}$. If the particle collides with the wall elastically, the speed of the particle just after the collision is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955909/preptracker/questions/q_35552_explanation_1764955908973.png Velocity of seperation ${=}$ Velocity of approach
(${\because}$ collision is elastic)
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & v-5=20+5 \\\Rightarrow & v=30 {~m} / {s}\end{array}$

question: A ball moving with velocity ${2 {~m} / {s}}$ involves head-on collision with another stationary ball of double the mass. If the coefficient of restitution is 0.5 , then their velocities (in ${{m} / {s}}$ ) after the collision will be
explanation:
Here, ${m_{1}=m, m_{2}=2 m, u_{1}=2 m / s, u_{2}=0}$ and coefficient of restitution, ${e=0.5}$.
Let ${v_{1}}$ and ${v_{2}}$ be their respective velocities after collision. Applying the law of conservation of linear momentum, we get
${m_{1} u_{1}+m_{2} u_{2}=m_{1} v_{1}+m_{2} v_{2}}$
${\therefore m \times 2+2 m \times 0=m \times v_{1}+2 m \times v_{2}}$
or ${2=m v_{1}+2 m v_{2}}$
or $2 = \left( {{v_1} + 2{v_2}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
By definition of coefficient of restitution,
${e=\dfrac{v_{2}-v_{1}}{u_{1}-u_{2}}}$
or ${e\left(u_{1}-u_{2}\right)=v_{2}-v_{1}}$
${0.5(2-0)=v_{2}-v_{1}}$
$1 = {v_2} - {v_1}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Solving Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
${v_{1}=0 {~m} / {s}, v_{2}=1 {~m} / {s}}$

question: Two bodies ${A}$ and ${B}$ of same mass undergo completely inelastic one dimensional collision. The body ${A}$ moves with velocity ${v_{1}}$ while body ${B}$ is at rest before collision. The velocity of the system after collision is ${v_{2}}$. The ratio ${v_{1}: v_{2}}$ is
explanation:
As the collision is completely inelastic so the bodies move with same velocity after collision Using conservation of linear momentum Initial momentum ${=}$ Final momentum
${\Rightarrow m v_{1}=m v_{2}+m v_{2}}$
${\Rightarrow m v_{1}=2 m v_{2}}$
${\Rightarrow \dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\dfrac{2}{1}}$

question: A particle of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ collides with a stationary particle of mass $2\,m$. After collision, they stick together and continue to move together with velocity
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952924/preptracker/questions/q_35453_explanation_1764952924165.png After collision, let the combined mass move with velocity $v^{\prime}$.
Using conservation of momentum, $\vec{P}_{i}=\vec{P}_{f}$
$m v+2 m \times 0=(m+2 m) v$
$\Rightarrow m v=3 m v^{\prime}$ or $v^{\prime}=\dfrac{v}{3}$

question: As per the given figure, a small ball $P$ slides down the quadrant of a circle and hits the other ball $Q$ of equal mass which is initially at rest. Neglecting the effect of friction and assume the collision to be elastic, the velocity of ball $Q$ after collision will be ($g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
Since the balls are of equal mass and the collision is elastic, after collision they will exchange their velocities and the first ball will come to rest. Therefore total potential energy of first ball will be converted to total kinetic energy of second ball.
Mathematically, $m g h=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
$m(10)(0.2)=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \Rightarrow v=2 m / s$

question: A ball of mass $200\,g$ rests on a vertical post of height $20\,m$. A bullet of mass $10\,g$, travelling in horizontal direction, hits the centre of the ball. After collision both travels independently. The ball hits the ground at a distance $30\,m$ and the bullet at a distance of $120\,m$ from the foot of the post. The value of initial velocity of the bullet will be (if ${g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}}$)
explanation:
Using $H=\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}$
$t=\sqrt{\dfrac{20}{5}}=2 \sec$
So, velocities of ball and bullet just after the impact can be determined using $R=v t$
${v_{{\rm{ball }}}} = \frac{{30}}{2} = 15\,m/s$
${v_{{\rm{bullet }}}} = \frac{{120}}{2} = 60\,m/s$
Using law of conservation of momentum,
${m_{{\text{bullet}}}}\,{u_{{\text{bullet}}}} + {M_{{\text{ball}}}}\,{u_{{\text{ball}}}}$
$ = {m_{{\text{bullet}}}}\,{v_{{\text{bullet}}}} + {M_{{\text{ball}}}}\,{v_{{\text{ball}}}}$
$\left( {\frac{{10}}{{1000}}} \right){u_{bullet{\rm{ }}}} + 0$
$ = \left( {\frac{{10}}{{1000}}} \right)(60) + \left( {\frac{{200}}{{1000}}} \right)(15)$
$u_{\text {bullet }}=(0.6+3) 100$
$ = 360\,m/s$

question: A body of mass $1000\,kg$ is moving horizontally with velocity $6\,m{\rm{/}}s$, if $200\,kg$ extra mass is added, the final velocity (in $m{\rm{/}}s)$ is :
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956505/preptracker/questions/q_35362_explanation_1764956504796.png As there is no external force on the system
$\therefore {P_i} = {P_f}$
$ \Rightarrow 1000 \times 6 = (1000 + 200) \times v$
$ \Rightarrow v = 5\,m{\rm{/}}s$

Reason: Deformation of matter occurs at some instant of elastic collision.

Kinetic energy $\left( {K.E} \right)$ is not conserved continuously throughout an elastic collision due to losses caused by factors such as friction and deformation at some instants. However, overall elastic collision has conservation of energy .${K.E}$. gets converted into elastic potential energy at those instants. Assertion is wrong and Reason is correct. question:
Assertion: If two bodies of equal masses undergo elastic collision in one dimension, then after the colision, the bodies will exchange their velocities.

Reason: In elastic collision, velocity of approach is not equal and opposite of velocity of separation.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955868/preptracker/questions/q_35267_explanation_1764955867854.png As collision is elastic
${m_1}{u_1} + {m_2}{u_2} = {m_1}{v_1} + {m_2}{v_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\frac{1}{2}{m_1}u_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}{m_2}u_2^2 = \frac{1}{2}{m_1}v_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}{m_2}v_2^2\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
solving : $\left( {{u_1} - {u_2}} \right) = \left( {{v_2} - {v_1}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
$\Rightarrow$ Reason is false
Use $v_{2}$ from (3) in (1) with $m_{1}=m_{2}=m$
$\Rightarrow v_{1}=u_{2}$ and $v_{2}=u_{1}$
$\Rightarrow$ Assertion is correct.
Therefore, it is proved that if bodies of equal mass collides then their velocities arc exchanged. So correct option is (3)

velocity of separation $=-$ velocity of approach

question:
Assertion: In an elastic collision of two bodies, only linear momentum (but not energy) of body is conserved.

Reason: If two bodies stick to each other, after colliding, then the collision is said to be perfectly 'inelastic'.
explanation:
In an elastic collision, both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved. However, it's essential to note that this conservation principle applies to the system of bodies involved before and after the collision, not to individual bodies. Conversely, collisions where there is a loss of kinetic energy are termed inelastic collisions, which are common in everyday life. A perfectly inelastic collision occurs when two bodies stick together after the collision.
So correct option is (4)

question: Two balls of same mass each $m$ are moving with same velocities, $v$ on a smooth surface as shown in figure. If all collisions between the masses and with the wall are perfectly elastic, the possible number of collisions between the bodies and wall together is
explanation: The speeds of balls remain unchanged after collisions. Then, there are only three collisions between balls and wall together.

question: In the figure, pendulum bob on left side is pulled a side to a height $h$ from its initial position. After it is released it collides with the right pendulum bob at rest, which is of same mass. After the collision, the two bobs stick together and rise to a height.
explanation:
When bob $A$ strikes the bob $B$, then $m u=(m+n) v^{\prime}$
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2024_02_14_1c8494a226fb2c4787ebg-5.jpg?height=235&width=242&top_left_y=701&top_left_x=432
$\Rightarrow v^{\prime}=\dfrac{u}{2}$
The potential energy of $A$ at height $h$ converts into kinetic energy of this mass, at point $O$, i.e.
$m g h=\dfrac{1}{2} m u^{2} \text { or } u=\sqrt{2 g h}$
$\therefore v^{\prime}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 g h}}{2}=\dfrac{\sqrt{g h}}{2}$
Let combined mass moves to a height h', then
$2 m g h^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{2}(2 m) v^{\prime 2} \Rightarrow g h^{\prime}=\dfrac{g h}{4} \Rightarrow h^{\prime}=\dfrac{h}{4}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Inelastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A wagon of mass 10 tons moving at a speed of $12\,kmph$ collides with another wagon of mass 8 tons moving on the same track in the same direction at a speed of $10\,kmph$. If the speed of the first wagon decreases to $8\,kmph$. The speed of the other after collision is
explanation:
By applying law of conservation of momentum
${m_1}{u_1} + {m_2}{u_2} = {m_1}{v_1} + {m_2}{v_2}$
$10 \times 12 + 8 \times 10 = 10 \times 8 + 8 \times {v_2}$
$ \Rightarrow {v_2} = 15\,kmph$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Partial Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: Two astronauts, $A$ and $B$, both with mass of $60\,kg$, are moving along a straight line in the same direction in a "weightless" spaceship. Relative to the spaceship the speed of $A$ is $2\,m{\rm{/}}s$ and that of $B$ is $1\,m{\rm{/}}s$. A is carrying a bag of mass $5\,kg$ with him. To avoid collision with $B, A$ throws the bag with a speed $v$ relative to the spaceship towards $B$ and $B$ catches it. Find the minimum value of $v$.
explanation:
$65 \times 2=5 v+60 v^{1}$
$5 v+60=65 v^{11}$
for no collision to take place
${v^1} < {v^{11}} \Rightarrow \frac{{130 - 5v}}{{60}} < \frac{{5v + 60}}{{65}}$
$ \Rightarrow 1690 - 65v < 60v + 720 < 970 < 125\,v$
$v > \frac{{970}}{{125}} \Rightarrow v > 7.8$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Collisions

question: Two masses ' $m_{1}$ ' and ' $m_{2}$ ' moving with velocities ' $v_{1}$ ' and ' $v_{2}$ ' in opposite directions collide elastically and after collision masses ' $m_{1}{ }^{\prime}$ and ' $m_{2}$ ' move with velocity ' $v_{2}$ ' and ' $v_{1}{ }^{\prime}$ respectively. The ratio $\left(\dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}\right)$ is
explanation:
According to law of conservation of momentum for collisions https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955839/preptracker/questions/q_35068_explanation_1764955839035.png $m_{1} v_{1}+m_{2} v_{2}=m_{1} v_{2}+m_{2} v_{1}$
$m_{1} v_{1}-m_{1} v_{2}=m_{2} v_{1}-m_{2} v_{2}$
$m_{1}\left(v_{1}-v_{2}\right)=m_{2}\left(v_{1}-v_{2}\right)$
$m_{1}=m_{2}$
$\therefore \dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}=1$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: There is no loss in energy in elastic collision.

Reason: Linear momentum is conserved in elastic collision
explanation:
In elastic collision, total energy, kinetic energy and momentum remain conserved, therefore no loss in energy occurs in elastic collision.
Hence, both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A body of mass $5 \times {10^3}\,kg$ moving with speed $2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ collides with a body of mass $15 \times {10^3}\,kg$ inelastically and sticks to it. Then, loss in kinetic energy of the system will be
explanation:
Given, mass of body,
${m_1} = 5 \times {10^3}\,kg$
Velocity, ${v_1} = 2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ and mass of another body
${m_2} = 15 \times {10^3}\,kg$
For perfectly inelastic collision $(e=0)$,
Loss in kinetic energy of system,
$\Delta {E_K} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{{m_1}{m_2}}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}} \times v_1^2$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{5 \times {{10}^3} \times 15 \times {{10}^3}}}{{5 \times {{10}^3} + 15 \times {{10}^3}}} \times {2^2}$
$ = 7.5 \times {10^3}\,J = 7.5\,kJ$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Inelastic Collision in One Dimension

question:
Assertion: Two particles moving in the same direction do not lose all their energy in completely inelastic collision.

Reason: Principle of conservation of momentum holds true for all kinds of collisions.
explanation:
If two particles are initially moving in the same direction, then their resultant momentum will not be zero. Therefore, their resultant momentum cannot be zero after a completely inelastic collision.
As, kinetic energy is directly proportional to the square of the momentum, hence kinetic energy cannot be zero. This implies, not all the energy in inelastic collision is lost.
So, both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Inelastic Collision in One Dimension

question: Body of mass $M$ is much heavier than the other body of mass $m$. The heavier body with speed $v$ collides with the lighter body which was at rest initially elastically. The speed of lighter body after collision is
explanation:
If $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ collide elastically we know that
$\begin{aligned}& v_{2}=\dfrac{2 m_{1}}{m_{1}+m_{2}} v_{1}+\dfrac{m_{2}-m_{1}}{m_{1}+m_{2}} v_{2} \\& \therefore v_{2}=0 \\& v_{2}=\dfrac{2 m_{1}}{m_{1}+m_{2}} v_{1} \\& =\dfrac{2 M}{M+m} v_{1} \\& \approx 2 v_{1}(\because M>>>m)\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Perfectly Head on Elastic Collision in One Dimension

question: A body of mass $4\,kg$ moving with velocity $12\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ collides with another body of mass $6\,kg$ at rest. If two bodies stick together after collision, then the loss of kinetic energy of system is
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Quick collision between two bodies is more violent than a slow collision; even when the initial and final velocities are identical

Reason: The momentum is greater in first case.
explanation:
As momentum, $p=m v$ or $p \propto v$, i.e., momentum is directly proportional to its velocity, so the momentum is greater in a quicker collision between two bodies than in slower one.
Hence, due to greater momentum quicker collision between two bodies will be more violent even inital and final velocities are identical.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.
