question: A mass of  2 $kg$  falls from a height of 40 $cm$ on a spring with a force constant of 1960 $N/m$. The spring is compressed by ( take $g = 9.8\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
Decrease in gravitational potential energy= increase in elastic potential energy
or $mg(h + x) = \frac{1}{2}K{x^2}$
or $2 \times 9.8(4.0+x)=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 1960 \times x^{2}$
Solving this equation we get, $x = 0.1\,m\,$ or $10\,cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conservation of Mechanical Energy

question: A body of mass 5 $kg$ is thrown vertically up with a kinetic energy of 490 $J$. The height at which the kinetic energy of the body becomes half of the original value is
explanation:
Let the kinetic energy of the body become half of its original value at height $h$. According to law of conservation of mechanical energy,
$\frac{1}{2}m{u^2}\, = \,\frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{1}{2}m{u^2}} \right)\, + \,mgh$
$490 = 245 + 5 \times 9.8 \times h$
$h = \frac{{245}}{{5\, \times \,9.8}} = 5m$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conservation of Mechanical Energy

question: The bob of a pendulum is released from a horizontal position $A$ as shown in the figure. If the length of the pendulum is 1.5 $m$, then the speed with which the bob arrives at the lower most point $B$. Given that it dissipated $5 \%$ of its initial energy against air resistance?
explanation:
Length of the pendulum $=1.5 \mathrm{~m}$
Potential energy of the bob at position $\mathrm{A}=m g h$
As bob moves from position A towards position $B$ its potential energy converted into kinetic energy.
$\therefore \mathrm{KE}$ at positon $\mathrm{B}=95 \%$ of its $\mathrm{PE}$ at position A
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{95}{100} \times m g h \\\text { or } \quad v & =\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 95 \times g h}{100}} \\& =\sqrt{\dfrac{19}{10} \times 9.8 \times 15}=5.28 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \cong 5.3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conservation of Mechanical Energy

question:
In shotput event an athlete throws the shotput of mass 10 $kg$ with an initial speed of $1\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ at $45^{\circ}$ from a height 1.5 $m$ above a ground.
Assuming air resistance to be negligible and acceleration due to gravity to be $10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$, the kinetic energy of the shotput when it just reaches the ground will be
explanation:
As the shotput reaches the ground, its KE $=\mathrm{PE}$ of shotput when it is thrown $+\mathrm{KE}$ given
$\begin{aligned}& =m g h+\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=10 \times 10 \times 1.5+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10(1)^{2} \\& =150+5=155 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conservation of Mechanical Energy

question: A ring of mass $m$ can slide over a smooth vertical rod as shown in figure. The ring is connected to a spring of force constant $\dfrac{4 m g}{R}$, where $2 \mathrm{R}$ is the natural length of the spring. The other endof spring is fixed to the ground at a horizontal distance $2 R$ from the base of the rod. If the mass is released at a height $1.5 \mathrm{R}$, then the velocity of the ring as it reaches the ground is
explanation:
Initial length of spring
$=\sqrt{(2 R)^{2}+(1.5 R)^{2}}=2.5 R$
So, $\quad x=2.5 R-2 R=0.5 R$
From conservation of energy :
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=m g h+\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=m g 1.5 R+\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{4 m g}{R}(0.5 R)^{2} \\& \Rightarrow v=2 \sqrt{g R}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Conservation of Mechanical Energy

question: A ball of mass $m$ kept at top slide freely on frictionless hemispherical surface. At what minimum angle $\theta$ net force acting on ball would be equal to $mg$ ?
explanation:
When the net force is equal to mg then the particle looses contact with the surface. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955702/preptracker/questions/q_33986_explanation_1764955702481.png $i.e.,\,\,N + \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} = mg\cos \theta \,\,(N = 0)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
From conservation of energy
$mgr(1 - \cos \theta ) = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) & (2) we get
$\cos \theta=2 / 3$

question: The maximum value attained by the tension in the string of a swinging pendulum is four times the minimum value it attains. There is no slack in the string. The angular amplitude of the pendulum is
explanation:
Let $l$ is the length of the string. Tension is maximum at the point $A$ and minimum at point $B$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951132/preptracker/questions/q_33604_explanation_1764951131766.png At point $A$:
${T_1} - mg = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{l}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
At Point $B$:
${T_2} = mg\cos \theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Given that
${T_1} = 4{T_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
From the above eq's
$\Rightarrow m g+\dfrac{m v^{2}}{l}=4 m g \cos \theta$
$ \Rightarrow mg(4\cos \theta - 1) = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{l}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(4)$
Conserving energy between points $A$ and $B$
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} + 0 = 0 + mgl(1 - \cos \theta )\frac{{m{v^2}}}{l}$
$ = 2mg(1 - \cos \theta )\,\,\,(5)$
From equations (4) & (5)
$\begin{aligned}& m g(4 \cos \theta-1)=2 m g(1-\cos \theta) \\& \Rightarrow \theta=60^{\circ}\end{aligned}$

question: Match the types and definitions related to work given in Column I with their correct descriptions in Column II. Choose the correct option from the codes given below:

B. Non-conservative work is work done by forces where the work depends on the particular path taken, unlike conservative forces. (B-S)
C. The definition of work in physics is the product of the component of the force in the direction of the displacement and the magnitude of this displacement. (C-P)
D. Work is calculated by the formula $W=(F \cos \theta) d$ which simplifies to $F . d$ when force and displacement are in the same direction. (D-Q). Correct option is (2).

question:
A force $F(x)$ is conservative, if
I. It can be derived from a scalar quantity $V(x)$
II. It depends only on the end points.
III. Work done by $F(x)$ in a closed path is zero. Which of the above statement(s) is/are correct?
explanation:
A force $F(x)$ is conservative, if it can be derived from a scalar quantity $V(x)$ by the relation given by equation, $F(x)=\dfrac{-d V}{d x}$. The work done by the conservative force depends only on the end points. This can be seen from the relation,
$W=K_{f}-K_{i}=U\left(x_{i}\right)-U\left(x_{f}\right)$ which depends on the end points.
The work done by this force in a closed path is zero. This is once again apparent from equation,
$K_{i}+U\left(x_{i}\right)=K_{f}+U\left(x_{f}\right)$,
Since $x_{i}=x_{f}$
So, all statements are correct.

question: $F=2 x^{2}-3 x-2$. Choose correct option
explanation:
For equilibrium, $F=0$
So $0=2 x^{2}-3 x-2 \Rightarrow 0=2 x^{2}-4 x+x-2$
$0=2 x(x-2)+1(x-2) \Rightarrow x=2, \dfrac{-1}{2}$
For checking nature of equilibrium.
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{d^{2} U}{d x^{2}}=\dfrac{d}{d x}\left[\dfrac{d U}{d x}\right]=\dfrac{d}{d x}[-F]=\dfrac{d}{d x}\left[-2 x^{2}+3 x+2\right] \\& \dfrac{d^{2} U}{d x^{2}}=-4 x+3\end{aligned}$
At $x=2, \dfrac{d^{2} U}{d x^{2}}=-5$ Unstable equilibrium
At $\quad x=-\dfrac{1}{2}, \dfrac{d^{2} U}{d x^{2}}=+5$ Stable equilibrium

options.text: $x=-1 / 2$ is a position of stable equilibrium
$x=2$ is a position of stable equilibrium
$x=-1 / 2$ is a position of unstable equilibrium
$x=2$ is a position of natural equilibrium

$\begin{aligned}F_{x} & =x^{2} y^{3} \\\dfrac{J F_{x}}{J y} & =3 x^{2} y^{2} \\F_{y} & =y^{2} x^{3} \\\dfrac{J F_{y}}{J x} & =3 y^{2} x^{2} \\\Rightarrow \dfrac{J F_{x}}{J y} & =\dfrac{J F_{y}}{J x}\end{aligned}$

question: When a conservative force does positive work on a body:
explanation:
Positive work done by a conservative force always decreases the potential energy
$W = - \Delta U$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta U = - (W) = - ( + ve) = - ve$

question: The kinetic energy of a body of mass $4\,kg$ moving with a velocity of $(2 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) m s^{-1}$ is
explanation:
$m=4 k g, \vec{v}=(2 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) m s^{-1}$
$\therefore v = |2\hat i - 4\hat j - \hat k|$
$ = \sqrt {{{(2)}^2} + {{( - 4)}^2} + {{( - 1)}^2}} = \sqrt {21} $
$\therefore$ K.E. $ = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 21 = 42\,J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: Match the terms related to kinetic energy in Column I with their correct descriptions in Column II. Choose the correct option from the codes given below:
explanation:
A. The formula for kinetic energy is given by $K=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ where $m$ is mass and $v$ is velocity.
(A - Q)
B. Kinetic energy is a scalar quantity, meaning it has only magnitude and is not affected by direction. (B-R)
C. An example of kinetic energy is the energy of a fast flowing stream used to grind corn, illustrating how kinetic energy is employed in practical applications. (C-S)
D. Kinetic energy is defined as the energy an object possesses due to its motion, and it represents the work that the object can do by virtue of its motion. (D - P)
Correct option is (3).

options.text: A - S,       B - P,          C - Q,       D - R
A - P,  B - S,            C - R,    D - Q
A - Q,  B - R,          C - S,    D - P

question:
Read the ${\rm{Statement - A}}$ and ${\rm{Statement - B}}$ carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The kinetic energy of an object is always positive.
The work done by a force can be negative, positive, or zero depending on the direction of the force relative to the displacement.
explanation: Kinetic energy, being a scalar quantity that is a function of the square of velocity, is always positive, making Statement A true. Work done, which is the dot product of force and displacement, can indeed have different signs based on their relative directions; hence, Statement B is also true. Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

Read the ${\rm{Statement - A}}$ and ${\rm{Statement - B}}$ carefully to mark the correct option given below:
If an object's speed doubles, its kinetic energy quadruples.
Kinetic energy is a vector quantity that depends on the direction of the object's velocity.
explanation: Statement A is true because kinetic energy is proportional to the square of the speed, so if the speed doubles, the kinetic energy increases by a factor of four . Statement B is false because kinetic energy is a scalar quantity, not a vector, and does not depend on the direction of velocity. Correct option is (2).

explanation: Statement A is false because even though the speed is constant in uniform circular motion, the kinetic energy also remains constant since it only depends on the magnitude of the velocity, not the direction. Statement B is true as it correctly defines the relationship between kinetic energy, mass, and speed. Correct option is (3).

question: Two bodies of masses of $1 g$ and $4 g$ are moving with equal kinetic energies. The ratio of the magnitudes of their linear momenta is
explanation:
Given, $m_{1}=1 g, m_{2}=4 g$
Kinetic energy of both bodies are equal.
From the formula of linear momentum
$p = \sqrt {2m(KE)} $
For 1st body ${p_1} = \sqrt {2(1){{(KE)}_1}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
For 2 nd body ${P_2} = \sqrt {2(4){{(KE)}_2}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Divide Eqs. (1) by (2)
$\dfrac{p_{1}}{p_{2}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2(K E)}_{1}}{\sqrt{8(K E)_{2}}}$
As $K E$ of both are same, $\dfrac{p_{1}}{p_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{4}}=\dfrac{1}{2}=1: 2$
Hence, the ratio is $1: 2$.
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: A particle having kinetic energy $K$ is projected at $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The kinetic energy at the highest point is
explanation:
The motion of the projectile is as shown in figure, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957989/preptracker/questions/q_108498_explanation_1764957988614.png $K{E_i} = \frac{1}{2}m{u^2} = K{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
At highest point,
$K E_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m(u \cos \theta)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m u^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta$
$=K E_{1} \cos ^{2} 60^{\circ}=\dfrac{K}{4}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: The sum of the magnitudes of two forces acting at a point is $8\,N.$ The resultant of these forces is perpendicular to the smaller force and has a magnitude of  $4\,N.$ If the smaller force is of magnitude $x$, then the value of $x$ is
explanation:
Given: $x + y = 8\,,$
$\therefore {y^2} = {4^2} + {x^2}$
$\therefore {(8 - x)^2} = {4^2} + {x^2}$
$(\because y = 8 - x)$
$\therefore {\rm{ }}64 - 16x + {x^2} = {4^2} + {x^2}$
$\therefore {\rm{ }}16x = 48$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951668/preptracker/questions/q_36311_explanation_1764951667882.png $\therefore {\rm{ }}x = 3N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: If $\vec{P}$ and $\vec{Q}$ are the nonzero positive vectors making angle of $30^{\circ}$ with each other, then the angle (in degrees) between additive inverse of $\vec{P}$ with $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{Q}}$ will be
explanation: If $\vec P$ is multiplied by negative real number, then we get a vector acting in the opposite direction of  $\vec P$ i.e., $-\vec P$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951643/preptracker/questions/q_36306_explanation_1764951642556.png  https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951643/preptracker/questions/q_36306_explanation_1764951643352.png
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: If momentum is decreased by ${20 \%}$, kinetic energy will decrease by
explanation:
Momentum ( ${p}$ ) is related to kinetic energy ${(K)}$ as
$p = \sqrt {2mK} {\text{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
New momentum,
$p' = \frac{{4p}}{5} = \sqrt {2mK'} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,$
$\quad \quad \quad $ ($K' = \,\,$new kinetic energy) (2)
Dividing eq. (1) by eq. (2), we get
${\dfrac{5}{4}=\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{K^{\prime}}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{K}{K^{\prime}}=\dfrac{25}{16}=K^{\prime}=\dfrac{16}{25} K}$
Decrease in kinetic energy (in %)
${=\dfrac{K-\dfrac{16}{25} K}{K} \times 100=\dfrac{9}{25} \times 100=36 \%}$.
So correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: An artillery piece of mass $M_{1}$ fires a shell of mass $M_{2}$ horizontally. Instantaneously after the firing, the ratio of kinetic energy of the artillery and that of the shell is
explanation:
Let $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ be the velocity of artillery piece of mass $M_{1}$ and shell of mass $M_{2}$ respectively.
Using conservation of momentum,
$M_{1} v_{1}=M_{2} v_{2}$
$\Rightarrow p_{1}=p_{2}$
As, $K E=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 M}$
$\Rightarrow K E \propto \dfrac{1}{M} \Rightarrow \dfrac{K E_{1}}{K E_{2}}=\dfrac{M_{2}}{M_{1}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: Three bodies $A, B$ and $C$ have equal kinetic energies and their masses are  $400\,g,$  $1.2\,kg$ and $1.6\,kg$ respectively. The ratio of their linear momenta is
explanation:
$K=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}$, so $p=\sqrt{2 m K}$
$K.E.$ is same, and hence $p \propto \sqrt{m}$
${m_1} = 0.4\,kg,$ ${m_2} = 1.2\,kg,$ ${m_3} = 1.6\,kg$
$\therefore {p_A}:{p_B}:{p_C} = \sqrt {0.4} :\sqrt {1.2} :\sqrt {1.6} $
$ = 1:\sqrt 3 :\sqrt 4 = 1:\sqrt 3 :2$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: A stationary particle breaks into two parts of masses $m_{A}$ and $m_{B}$ which move with velocities $v_{A}$ and $v_{B}$ respectively. The ratio of their kinetic energies $\left(K_{B}: K_{A}\right)$ is
explanation:
According the principle of conservation of momentum, they fly off in opposite directions with same momentum, as external force on the system is zero.
$\therefore m_{A} v_{A}=m_{B} v_{B}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{m_{A}}{m_{B}}=\dfrac{v_{B}}{v_{A}}$ $\quad \,(1)$
As $K=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}$
As $p=$ same and hence $K \propto \dfrac{1}{m}$
$\dfrac{K_{B}}{K_{A}}=\dfrac{m_{A}}{m_{B}}=\dfrac{v_{B}}{v_{A}} \quad$ (using (1))
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: When kinetic energy of a body becomes 36  times of its original value, the percentage increase in the momentum of the body will be
explanation:
$K E_{2}=36 K E_{1}$
Relation between $K E$ and momentum is,
$p=\sqrt{2 m K E} \Rightarrow p \propto \sqrt{K E}$
$\dfrac{p_{2}}{p_{1}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{K E_{2}}{K E_{1}}} \Rightarrow \sqrt{\dfrac{36 K E_{2}}{K E_{1}}}=6$
$\Rightarrow p_{2}=6 p_{1}$
So, $\dfrac{p_{2}-p_{1}}{p_{1}} \times 100 \%=\dfrac{6 p_{1}-p_{1}}{p_{1}} \times 100=500 \%$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: Four particles $A, B, C, D$ of mass $\dfrac{m}{2}, m, 2 m, 4 m$, have same momentum, respectively. The particle with maximum kinetic energy is
explanation:
$K E=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}$
For same momentum $(p), K E \propto \dfrac{1}{m}$
Therefore, the lightest particle will possess maximum kinetic energy.
$\therefore $ A possess maximum kinetic energy.
So, correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: A bullet of mass  $50\,g$  is fired with a speed  $100\,m/s$  on a plywood and emerges with $40\,m/s$. The percentage loss of kinetic energy is
explanation:
Here, $m = 50\,g = 0.05\,kg,$ ${v_1} = 100\,m/s$ and ${v_2} = 40\,m/s$
$K E \propto v^{2}$
$\dfrac{K E_{2}}{K E_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{v_{2}}{v_{1}}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{40}{100}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{4}{25}$
$\%$ loss of $K E$ is $=\dfrac{K E_{1}-K E_{2}}{K E_{1}} \times 100 \%$
$=\left(1-\dfrac{K E_{1}}{K E_{2}}\right) \times 100 \%$
$=\left(1-\dfrac{4}{25}\right) \times 100 \%$
$=84 \%$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: A particle of mass $m$ at rest is acted upon by a force $P$ for a time $t$. Its kinetic energy after an interval $t$ is
explanation:
Given, force, $P=m a$
As the kinetic energy, $K = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{{m^2}\left( {{v^2}} \right)}}{m} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{{m^2}{a^2}{t^2}}}{m}$
$(\because v = at,{\text{ as }}u = 0)$
$ = \frac{{{P^{\,2}}{t^2}}}{{2\,m}}\quad \quad (\because \,\,P = ma)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: An open water tight railway wagon of mass  $5 \times {10^3}\,kg$ coasts at an initial velocity of $1.2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$  without friction on a railway track. Rain falls vertically downwards into the wagon. What change occurs in kinetic energy of the wagon, when it has collected ${10^3}\,kg$ of water?
explanation:
If $v^{\prime}$ is the final velocity of the wagon, then applying the principle of conservation of linear momentum, we get
$\Rightarrow m v=m^{\prime} v$
where $m, v^{\prime}$ are final mass and velocity of wagon.
Given, $m = 5 \times {10^3}\,kg,v = 1.2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
and $m' = \left( {5 \times {{10}^3} + {{10}^3}} \right)kg$
$ \Rightarrow 5 \times {10^3} \times 1.2 = \left( {5 \times {{10}^3} + {{10}^3}} \right) \times v'$
$v' = 1\,\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Change in $K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m^{\prime} v^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\left( {6 \times {{10}^3}} \right) \times {1^2}$
$ - \frac{1}{2}\left( {5 \times {{10}^3}} \right) \times {(1.2)^2} = 600\,J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: A particle of mass $2\,mg$ moves with constant speed and is found to pass two points $5.0\,m$ apart in a time interval of $5\,ms.$ Find the kinetic energy of the particle.
explanation:
Given, mass $ = 2\,mg = 2 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,kg,$
Distance $ = 5.0\,m$
and time, $t = 5\,ms = 5 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,s$
Now, velocity $ = \frac{{{\rm{ distance }}}}{{{\rm{ time }}}} = \frac{5}{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}\,\,\,m/s$
$ = 1000\,m/s$
and $KE = \frac{1}{2}\,m{v^2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\, \times 2 \times {10^{ - 6}}{\left( {{{10}^3}} \right)^2} = 1\,J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: Kinetic energy of a particle is increased by 4 times. What will be the relation between initial and final momentum?
explanation:
Relation between kinetic energy and momentum is
$p_{1}=\sqrt{2 m K_{1}}$
$\because$ Kinetic energy is increased by 4 times, then$K_{2}=4 K_{1}$
Hence, $p_{2}=\sqrt{2 m K_{2}}=\sqrt{2 m\left(4 K_{1}\right)}$\begin{aligned}& p_{2}=2 \sqrt{2 m K_{1}} \\& \text { or } \\& p_{2}=2 p_{1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: A car accelerates from rest to a speed of ${10 {~m} / {s}}$. Let the energy spent be ${E}$. If we accelerate the car from ${10 {~m} / {s}}$ to ${20 {~m} / {s}}$, the energy spent will be
subject: Physics

question:
At time ${t=0}$, particle starts moving along the
${x}$-axis. If its kinetic energy increases uniformly with time ${t}$, the net force acting on it must be proportional to
explanation:
${E_{K . E} \propto t \Rightarrow u^{2} \propto t \Rightarrow u \propto \sqrt{t}}$
${a=\dfrac{d u}{d t} \Rightarrow a \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{t}} \Rightarrow F \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{t}}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: Two bodies are having kinetic energies in the ratio $16: 9$. If they have same linear momentum, the ratio of their masses respectively is
explanation:
Kinetic energy of a body is given by
$K=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}$
where, $p=$ momentum, $m=$ mass
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{p_{1}}{p_{2}}\right)^{2}\left(\dfrac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}\right)$
Given, $p_{1}=p_{2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}=\dfrac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}=\dfrac{K_{2}}{K_{1}}=\dfrac{9}{16}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question:
Assertion: If momentum of a body increases by $50 \%$, its kinetic energy will increase by $125 \%$.

Reason: Kinetic energy is proportional to square root of velocity (for constant mass).
explanation:
$p^{\prime}=p+0.5 p=1.5 p$
$\Rightarrow E^{\prime}{ }_{k}=\left(\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}\right)=\dfrac{\left(p^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{2 m}=(2.25) \dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}=2.25 E_{k}$
$\Rightarrow \Delta E_{k}=1.25 E_{k}$
$\Rightarrow$ Kinetic energy increases by $125 \%$
Assertion is correct. As $K . E \propto v^{2}$ Reason is wrong. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question:
Assertion: A kinetic energy of a body is quadrupled, when its velocity is doubled.

Reason: Kinetic energy is proportional to square of velocity.
explanation:
When the velocity of a body is doubled, its kinetic energy is indeed quadrupled, because kinetic energy is proportional to the square of velocity.
So correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: A particle is acted upon by a force \[F\] which varies with position \[x\] as shown in figure. If the particle at \(x=0\) has kinetic energy of 25 \[J\], then the kinetic energy of the particle at \[x = 16\,m\] is
explanation:
Work done $\mathrm{W}=$ Area under $\mathrm{F}$ - $\mathrm{x}$ graph. Area is $(+) v e$ if $F$ is $(+) v e$. Area is $(-) v e$ if $F$ is $(-)$ ve
$W=\left[\dfrac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 10\right]+4 \times[-5]+[4 \times 5]+[2 \times(-5)]$$\Rightarrow W=30-20+20-10=20 \mathrm{~J}$
According to work energy theorem,
$\begin{aligned}& K_{f}-K_{i}=W \\& K_{f}=25 J+20 J=45 J\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: Kinetic energy of a particle moving in a straight line varies with time $t$ as $K=4 \mathrm{t}^{2}$. The force acting on a particle
explanation:
$K = 4{t^2}\quad $ or ${v^2} \propto {t^2}$
$\therefore \mathrm{V} \propto \mathrm{t}$
$v$ varies linearly with time when acceleration or force is constant
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: If the linear momentum of a body is increased by $50 \%$, then the kinetic energy of that body increases by
explanation:
Kinetic energy of the body, $\mathrm{K}=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}$
where $p$ is the linear momentum and $m$ is the mass of the body.
Since mass remains constant
$\therefore \mathrm{K} \propto \mathrm{p}^{2}$
As ${p^\prime } = p + \frac{{50}}{{100}}p = \frac{3}{2}p$
$\therefore \dfrac{K^{\prime}}{K}=\left(\dfrac{p^{\prime}}{p}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{9}{4}$
$\%$ Increase in the kinetic energy
$=\dfrac{K^{\prime}-K}{K} \times 100 \%$
$=\left(\dfrac{K^{\prime}}{K}-1\right) \times 100 \%=\left(\dfrac{9}{4}-1\right) \times 100 \%=125 \%$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: Two particles of masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ have equal kinetic energies. The ratio of their momenta is
explanation:
The kinetic energy $K$ and momentum $p$ of a particle are related by the relation
$\mathrm{K}=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}$ where $m$ is the mass of the particle.
So $K_{1}=\dfrac{p_{1}^{2}}{2 m_{1}}$ and $K_{2} \dfrac{p_{2}^{2}}{2 m_{2}}$
But as per question $K_{1}=K_{2}$
$\therefore \dfrac{p_{1}^{2}}{2 m_{1}}=\dfrac{p_{2}^{2}}{2 m_{2}}$ or $\dfrac{p_{1}^{2}}{p_{2}^{2}}=\dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}$ or $\dfrac{p_{1}}{p_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: The kinetic energy of a body of mass 4 $kg$ and momentum 6 $Ns$ will be
explanation:
The kinetic energy $K$ and momentum $p$ of a body are related as
$K=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}$ where $\mathrm{m}$ is the mass of the body. Here,
$p = 6\,N\,s,\,m = 4\,kg$
$\therefore K = \frac{{{{(6Ns)}^2}}}{{2(4\,kg)}} = 4.5\,J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question:
A light body and a heavy body have same momentum. Then they also have same kinetic energy.
Kinetic energy does not depend on mass of the body

Kinetic energy of a system can be increased or decreased without applying any external force on the system.
$K . E .=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$, so it is independent of any external forces.
explanation: K.E. can be increased or decreased without applying any external force, as internal forces can do work e.g., explosion of a bomb.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question: A mass of 5 $kg$  is moving along a circular path of radius 1 $m$ . If the mass moves with 300 revolutions per minute, its kinetic energy would be
explanation:
Here, $m=5 \mathrm{~kg}, \mathrm{r}=1 \mathrm{~m}$
$\omega=\dfrac{300}{60} \mathrm{rps}=5 \mathrm{rps}=5 \times 2 \pi \mathrm{rad} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$
$K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m(r \omega)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 5(1 \times 10 \pi)^{2}=250 \pi^{2} J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: A projectile is fired with some velocity making certain angle with the horizontal. Which of the following graphs is the best representation for the kinetic energy of a projectile (\[KE\]) versus its horizontal displacement \[x\] ?
explanation: The $K.E$ decreases and it reaches minimum value at the top and then it. Starts increasing.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: An athlete in the olympic games covers a distance of 100 $m$ in 10 $s$. His kinetic energy can be estimated to be in the range
explanation:
The average speed of the athelete
$\begin{aligned}& v=\dfrac{100}{10}=10 m / s \\& \therefore K . E=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}\end{aligned}$
If mass is $40 \mathrm{~kg}$ then,
$K . E=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 40 \times(10)^{2}=2000 J$
If mass is $100 \mathrm{~kg}$ then,
$K . E=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 100 \times(10)^{2}=5000 J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: When a man increases his speed by $2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$, he finds that his kinetic energy is doubled, the original speed of the man is
explanation:
Man possesses kinetic energy, because of his velocity $(v)$. When $m$ is mass of man, then
$K=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
Let $v_{1}=v, m_{1}=m_{2}=m$
$v_{2}=(v+2) m s^{-1}$
Then $K_{2}=2 K_{1}$
Then, $\dfrac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}=\dfrac{v_{1}^{2}}{v_{2}^{2}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{K_{1}}{2 K_{1}}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{(v+2)^{2}}$
$\begin{gathered}\Rightarrow v^{2}-4 v-4=0 \\\Rightarrow v_{1}=\dfrac{4+\sqrt{16+16}}{2} \\v_{1}=2(\sqrt{2}+1) m s^{-1}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Energy

question: If the momentum of a body is increased by $40 \%$, then the percentage increase in its kinetic energy is
explanation:
Initial momentum $\left(p_{1}\right)=p$
Final momentum $\left(p_{2}\right)=1.4 p$
Kinetic energy $(K)=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m} \alpha p^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{p_{1}}{p_{2}}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{p}{1.4 p}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{1.96}$
$\begin{gathered}\Rightarrow K_{2}=1.96 K \\\Delta K=1.96 K-K=0.96 K \text { or } 96 \%\end{gathered}$

question: Two particles $A$ and $B$, move with constant velocities $\vec{v}_{1}$ and $\vec{v}_{2}$. At the initial moment their position vectors are $\vec{r}_{1}$ and $\vec{r}_{2}$ respectively. The condition for particles $A$ and $B$ for their collision is
explanation:
For two particles to collide, the direction of the relative velocity of one with respect to other should be directed towards the relative position of the other particle
i.e. $\dfrac{\vec{r}_{1}-\vec{r}_{2}}{\left|\vec{r}_{1}-\vec{r}_{2}\right|} \rightarrow$ direction of relative position of 1 w.r.t.2.& $\dfrac{\vec{v}_{1}-\vec{v}_{2}}{\left|\vec{v}_{1}-\vec{v}_{2}\right|} \rightarrow$ direction of velocity of 2 w.r..t.1
so for collision of A & B
$\dfrac{\vec{r}_{1}-\vec{r}_{2}}{\left|\vec{r}_{1}-\vec{r}_{2}\right|}=\dfrac{\vec{v}_{1}-\vec{v}_{2}}{\left|\vec{v}_{1}-\vec{v}_{2}\right|} \text { (Both are unit vectors) }$

question: A spring of force constant $k$ is cut into lengths of ratio 1:2:3. They are connected in series and the new force constant is  $k'$. Then they are connected in parallel and force constant is $k''$. Then ${k^\prime }:k''$ is
explanation:
Length of the spring segments $=\dfrac{\ell}{6}, \dfrac{\ell}{3}, \dfrac{\ell}{2}$
As we know $K \propto \dfrac{1}{\ell}$
So spring constants for spring segments will be
$K_{1}=6 K, K_{2}=3 K, K_{3}=2 K$
For parallel combination
$K^{\prime \prime}=K_{1}+K_{2}+K_{3}=11 K$
For series combination
$K^{\prime}=K$ (As it will become original spring)
so $K^{\prime}: K^{\prime \prime}=1: 11$

question: Two particles having position vectors $8 \hat{j},-8 \hat{i}$ metres are moving with velocities $\vec{v}_{1}=4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}$ and $\vec{v}_{2}=\alpha \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}$. If they collide after 2 seconds, the value of ' $\alpha$ ' is
explanation:
The displacement vector $\Delta \vec{r}$ between the particles is
$\Delta \,\vec r = {{\vec r}_2} - {{\vec r}_1} = - 8\hat i - 8\hat j$
$\left| {\Delta \,\vec r} \right| = \sqrt {{{( - 8)}^2} + {{( - 8)}^2}} = 8\sqrt 2 \,\,\,\,\,(1)$
The relative velocity is given by
${\overrightarrow v _{rel}} = {\overrightarrow v _2} - {\overrightarrow v _1} = (\alpha - 4)\hat i + 4\hat j$
$\left| {{{\overrightarrow v }_{rel}}} \right| = \sqrt {{{(\alpha - 4)}^2} + 16} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
When they collide in time $t$
$\left| {{{\overrightarrow v }_{rel}}} \right| = \frac{{\left| {\Delta \vec r} \right|}}{t} \Rightarrow \alpha = 8.$

$ = \frac{{6000 \times 10 \times 60}}{{1200}} = 3000\,W = 3\,kW$

question: An object is moving in a straight line under the influence of a source of constant power. If $v$ and $t$ are velocity and time respectively, then
explanation:
Power $=F \cdot v$ or $P=F \cdot v$ (for a body travelling in a straight line, force is parallel to velocity)
$P=\operatorname{mav} \quad(\because F=m a)$
$P=m \dfrac{d v}{d t} v$
$\Rightarrow v d v=\dfrac{P}{m} d t \Rightarrow \int_{0}^{v} v d v=\dfrac{P}{m} \int_{0}^{t} d t$
or $\dfrac{v^{2}}{2}=\dfrac{P t}{m} \Rightarrow v=\left(\dfrac{2 P}{m}\right)^{1 / 2} \cdot t^{1 / 2}$
or $v \propto t^{1 / 2}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A boat of mass $1000\,kg$ goes from rest to speed $20.0 {~m} / {s}$ in $5.0\,s$. The water exerts a constant drag force and the acceleration of the boat is constant. If the average power required by the boat is $45000\,W$, then the magnitude of the drag force is
explanation:
Net force on boat,
$F_{\text {net }}=F_{\text {boat }}-F_{\text {drag }}$
Average power of boat$=\dfrac{1}{2} F_{\text {net }} \times v$
so, $\dfrac{1}{2}\left(F_{\text {boat }}-F_{\text {drag }}\right) \times v=P$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left( {m \times {a_{{\text{boat }}}} - {F_{{\text{drag }}}}} \right) \times v = P\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Given, $m=1000 {~kg}$
$a_{\text {boat }}=\dfrac{20-0}{5}=4 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
$v=20 {~m} / {s}$ and $P=45000 {~W}$
Substituting given data, in Eq. (1), We get
$\dfrac{1}{2}\left(1000 \times 4-F_{\text {drag }}\right) \times 20=45000$
$\Rightarrow 4000-F_{\text {drag }}=4500$
or $F_{\text {drag }}=-500 {~N}$ (negative sign because $F_{\text {drag }}$ opposes velocity)
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: An engine is dragging a mass of $5000\,kg$ with a velocity of $5 {~ms}^{-1}$ along a smooth inclined plane of inclination 1 in 50. Then the power of the engine is
explanation:
Given,
$m=5000 {~kg}, v=5 {~m} / {s}$
and Inclination $=\dfrac{1}{50}$
Let $\theta$ be the angle of inclination
$\therefore \sin \theta=\dfrac{1}{50}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957996/preptracker/questions/q_108523_explanation_1764957995509.png Force acting on mass on inclined plane in a direction opposite to the direction of motion of the engine,
$F_{\text {net }}=m g \sin \theta+$ frictional force
Frictional force $=0$ [Smooth surface]
$F_{\text {net }}=F=5000 \times 10 \times \dfrac{1}{50} \quad\left[g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}\right]$
$\Rightarrow F_{\text {net }}=1000 {~N}$
Now power is given by
$P=F \cdot v=1000 \times 5=5000 {~W}=5 {~kW}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A pump on the ground floor of a building can pump up water to fill a tank of volume $36 m^{3}$ in 30 min . If the tank is $50\,m$ above the ground, and the electric power consumed by the pump is $40\,kW$, the efficiency of the pump is (use $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ and density of water$=1000 {~kg} / {m}^{3}$ )
explanation:
Work done by pump $=m g h=V . \rho . g . h$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952438/preptracker/questions/q_108516_explanation_1764952438242.png $V = $ volume, $\rho = $ density, $h = $ height
$={P}_{\text {out }}=\dfrac{V \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot h}{t}$
$\eta=\dfrac{\text { Power output }}{\text { Electrical power consumed }} \times 100$
$\eta=\dfrac{V \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot h \times 100}{P_{\text {electric }} \times t}$
$\Rightarrow \eta=\dfrac{36 \times 1000 \times 10 \times 50 \times 100}{40 \times 1000 \times 30 \times 60}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \eta=25 \%$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: Water is pumped steadily out of a flooded basement, at the speed of $10 {~m} / {s}$ through a hose (tube) of radius $1\,cm$, passing through a window $3 m$ above the water level. The power of the pump is (Assume $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$, density of water$=1000 {~kg} / {m}^{3}$ )
explanation:
Given, $v=10 {~ms}^{-1}$
As we know that,
Power $(P)=\dfrac{\text { Work }(W)}{\text { Time }(t)}$
and $\quad W=$ total energy $=m g h+\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
$\therefore \quad P=\dfrac{m g h+\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}}{t}=\dfrac{m}{t}\left(g h+\dfrac{v^{2}}{2}\right)$
$=\rho A v\left(g h+\dfrac{v^{2}}{2}\right)$
$=1000 \times \pi\left(\dfrac{1}{100}\right)^{2} \times 10\left(10 \times 3+\dfrac{100}{2}\right)$
$=1000 \times \dfrac{10}{10000} \times \pi 80=80 \pi {~W}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A constant force of $5 N$ accelerates a stationary particle of mass $500\,g$ through a displacement of $5\,m$. The average power delivered is
explanation:
Given, constant applied force, $F=5 N$,
$m=500 g=\dfrac{500}{1000}=0.5 {~kg}, s=5 m, u=0 {~ms}^{-1}$
$v_{f}=v=$ Final velocity.
As we know that,
$\operatorname{Average} \,\,\,\operatorname{power} \,\,(P) = \frac{{\operatorname{Work} \,\,(W)}}{{\operatorname{Time} \,\,(t)}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
According to work-energy theorem,
Work done, $W = \frac{1}{2}m\left( {{v^2} - {u^2}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
and $\quad F=m a$
where, $a$ is acceleration.
$\therefore \quad a=\dfrac{F}{m}=\dfrac{5}{0.5}=\dfrac{50}{5}=10 {~ms}^{-2}$
Now work done $W=F \cdot S=5 \times 5 {~J}$
$=25 {~J}$
According to first equation of motion,
$v=u+a t \Rightarrow t=\dfrac{v-u}{a}$
$ \Rightarrow t = \frac{{10 - 0}}{{10}} = 1\,s\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 4 \right)$
Now, from Eqs. (1), (3) and (4), we get
$P = \frac{W}{t} = \frac{{25}}{1} = 25\,W$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A water pump rated $600 W$ has an efficiency of $95 \%$. If it is used to raise water through vertical distance of $60\,m$. The volume of water drawn in 20 min , is [Use, density of water $=1000 {kgm}^{-3}, g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ ]
explanation:
Given, power $P=600 W, \eta=95 \%$
$h=60 {~m} \quad t=20 {~min}=20 \times 60 {~s}=1200 {~s}$
Density of water, $\rho=1000 {kgm}^{-3}$
Since, $\eta=\dfrac{P_{\text {output }}}{P_{\text {input }}}$
$\therefore \quad P_{\text {output }}=\dfrac{95}{100} P_{\text {input }}=0.95 P_{\text {input }}$
$=0.95 \times 600=570.00 {~W}$
$\operatorname{Output~power}\left(P_{\text {output }}\right)=\dfrac{\operatorname{Work}(W)}{\operatorname{Time}(t)}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {\rm{ Work }}(W) = P \times t{\rm{ }}$
$ = 570 \times 20 \times 60 = 684000\,J$
and $W = mgh\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
where, $m$ is mass.
$\because \quad \operatorname{Density}(\rho)=\dfrac{\operatorname{Mass}(m)}{\text { Volume }(V)}$
$\therefore \quad m=\rho V$
Substituting in Eq. (1), we get
$W=\rho V g h$
$ \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac{W}{{\rho gh}} = \frac{{684000}}{{1000 \times 10 \times 60}}{m^3} = 1.14\,{m^3}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A force acts on a body of mass $15\,kg$, initially at rest. If the instantaneous power due to the force at the end of the third second is $5\,W$, then the instantaneous power (in $W$ ) at the end of the $4 s$ will be
explanation:
Given, mass of the body, $m=15 {~kg}$
Acceleration, $a=\dfrac{F}{m}$
From kinematics, $v=u+a t$
$\Rightarrow v=a t \quad(\because u=0)$
$\Rightarrow \quad v=\dfrac{F}{m} t=\dfrac{3 F}{m} \quad(\because t=3 s)$
$\because$ Power $=5 W=F \cdot v=\dfrac{3 F^{2}}{m}$
$\Rightarrow F^{2}=\dfrac{5 m}{3}=\dfrac{5 \times 15}{3}=25 \Rightarrow F=5 {~N}$
Let the instantaneous power at the end of 4 th second be $P_{1}$ and velocity of body at the end of $4 s$ be $v^{\prime}$, then
$\therefore \quad \nu^{\prime}=u+a t=0+\dfrac{F}{m} t$
$v^{\prime}=\dfrac{5}{15} \times 4=\dfrac{4}{3} m / s \quad(\because t=4 s)$
$P_{1}=F \cdot v^{\prime}=5 \times \dfrac{4}{3}=\dfrac{20}{3} {~W}=6.67 {~W}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A block of mass $m$ placed on a rough horizontal plane is pulled by a constant power $P$. The coefficient of friction between the block and the surface is $\mu$. The maximum velocity of the block will be
explanation:
The given situation is as shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952423/preptracker/questions/q_108489_explanation_1764952423151.png Here, $N=m g$ and $f r_{k}=\mu N=\mu m g$
$\because$ Power, $P=F v_{\text {max }} \cos \left(0^{\circ}\right)=F v_{\text {max }} \times 1=F v$
When the block acquires maximum velocity $F-f_{r_{k}}=0$, where $F=$ applied force.
$\Rightarrow v_{\text {max }}=\dfrac{P}{F}=\dfrac{P}{f r_{k}} \Rightarrow v_{\text {max }}=\dfrac{P}{\mu m g}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: An object moves along the circle with normal acceleration proportional to $t^{a}$, where $t$ is the time and $\alpha$ is a positive constant. The power developed by all the forces acting on the object will have time dependence proportional to
explanation:
Given, $a_{c} \propto t^{\alpha}$ or $a_{c}=k t^{\alpha}$
Now, $a_{c}=\dfrac{v_{t}^{2}}{r}$
(where, $v_{t}=$ tangential component of velocity)
$\Rightarrow v_{t}=\sqrt{k r \cdot t^{\alpha}}$
and $a_{t}=\dfrac{d v_{t}}{d t}=\sqrt{(k r)} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha}{2} \cdot t^{\frac{\alpha}{2}-1}$
Now, power developed is
$P=F_{t} v_{t}=m a_{1} v_{t}=m \sqrt{k r} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha}{2} \cdot t^{\frac{\alpha}{2}-1} \sqrt{k r} \cdot t^{\alpha / 2}$
$\Rightarrow P \propto t^{\alpha-1}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A car of mass $1200\,kg$ (together with the driver) is moving with a constant acceleration of $2 {~m} / {s}^{2}$. How much power does the engine generate at the instance, when the speed reaches $20 {~m} / {s}$ ? (Assume that the coefficient of friction between the car and the road is 0.5).
explanation:
The forces acting on the car are as shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952413/preptracker/questions/q_108473_explanation_1764952413045.png Power of engine overcomes friction and provides necessary acceleration to the car. Now friction,
$f=\mu m g=0.5 \times 1200 \times 10=6000 {~N}$
and accelerating force,
$F=m a=1200 \times 2=2400 {~N}$
So, total force produced by engine
$F_{T}=8400 {~N}$
As $P=F_{T} v$
Then, $P=8400 \times 20=168000 {~W}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A small body of mass $500\,g$ moves on a rough horizontal surface before finally stops. The initial velocity of the body is $2 {~m} / {s}$ and coefficient of friction is 0.3 . Find absolute value of the average power developed by the frictional force during the time of motion. (Take, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
Initial and final positions of the block are shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957978/preptracker/questions/q_108466_explanation_1764957977626.png As, retardation is constant, so average velocity$v_{a v}=\dfrac{u+v}{2}=\dfrac{2+0}{2}=1 {~m} / {s}$
Now, friction force, $f=\mu N=\mu m g$
Given, coefficient of friction, $\mu=0.3$
Mass of body, $m=500 {~g}=0.5 {~kg}$
$\Rightarrow f=0.3 \times 0.5 \times 10 {~N}=1.5 {~N}$
So the absolute value of average power by friction,
$P_{a v}=f v_{a v}$
$\Rightarrow \quad P_{a v}=1.5 \times 1=1.5 {~W}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: Sand is being dropped from a stationary dropper at a rate of  $0.5\,kg\,{s^{ - 1}}$  on a convey  or belt moving with a velocity of  $5\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. The power needed to keep the belt moving with the same velocity will be
explanation:
Given, mass of sand dropped at a rate of $\frac{{dm}}{{dt}} = 0.5\,kg/s$
The power required to move the bell constant velocity is
$P = {v^2}\frac{{dm}}{{dt}} = {5^2} \times 0.5 = 12.5\,W$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A body of mass $2\,kg$ begins to move under the action of a time dependent force given by $\vec{F}=\left(6 t \hat{i}+6 t^{2} \hat{j}\right) N$. The power developed by the force at this time $t$ is given by
explanation:
Here, $\vec F = \left( {6t\hat i + 6{t^2}\hat j} \right)N;\,\,m = 2\,kg$
Acceleration of the body, $\vec{a}=\dfrac{\vec{F}}{m}$
$\vec a = \frac{{\left( {6t\hat i + 6{t^2}\hat j} \right)}}{2} = \left( {3t\hat i + 3{t^2}j} \right)m/{s^2}$
Velocity of the body at time $(t)$ is,
$\vec v = \int {\vec a} dt = \int {\left( {3t\hat i + 3{t^2}\hat j} \right)} \,dt$
$ = \left( {\frac{{3{t^2}}}{2}\hat i + \frac{{3{t^3}}}{3}\hat j} \right) = \left( {\frac{{3{t^2}}}{2}\hat i + {t^3}\hat j} \right)m/s$
Power developed by the force at time ' $t$ '
$P=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{v}=\left(6 t \hat{i}+6 t^{2} \hat{j}\right) \cdot\left(\dfrac{3 t^{2}}{2} \hat{i}+t^{3} \hat{j}\right)$
$P=\left(9 t^{3}+6 t^{5}\right) W$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A body is moving  unidirectionally  under the influence of a constant power source. Its displacement in time $t$ is proportional to
explanation:
Let the constant power be $P$.
Here, initial velocity, $u=0$
Instantaneous power of a body, $P=F v$
$\therefore \quad P = mav$
$\begin{aligned}& P=m\left(\dfrac{d v}{d t}\right) v \\& \Rightarrow v d v=\dfrac{P}{m} d t \\& \int_{0}^{v} d v=\dfrac{P}{m} \int_{0}^{t} d t \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{v^{2}}{2}=\dfrac{P}{m} t \\& v=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 P t}{m}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 P}{m}} t^{1 / 2} \\& v=\dfrac{d S}{d t}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 P}{m}} t^{1 / 2} \\& \Rightarrow d S=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 P}{m}} t^{1 / 2} d t \\& \int d S=\int \sqrt{\dfrac{2 P}{m}} t^{1 / 2} d t \\& S=\dfrac{2}{3} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 P}{m}} t^{3 / 2} \\& \therefore S \propto t^{3 / 2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: Two beads connected by a light inextensible string are placed over fixed vertical rings as shown in figure. The mass of each bead is $100\,g$ and all surface are frictionless. Find  the tension in the string   just after the beads are released from the shown position is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951622/preptracker/questions/q_36028_explanation_1764951621798.png ${N_{1}=\dfrac{T}{\sqrt{2}}+m g \dfrac{T}{\sqrt{2}}=m a}$
${\dfrac{T}{\sqrt{2}}=N_{2} m g-\dfrac{T}{\sqrt{2}}=m a}$
${m g-\dfrac{T}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{T}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow m g=T \sqrt{2}}$
${T=\dfrac{m g}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{\left(10^{-1}\right)(10)}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} {~N}=0.707 {~N}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Miscellaneous Problems on Work, Energy and Power
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Work, Energy and Power

question: A lift weighing $480\,kg$ is to be lifted upto a constant velocity of  $0.40\,m/s.$  The minimum horse power of the motor to be used is ____  $hp.$ (Take $g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$)
explanation:
$F = mg = 480 \times 10 = 4800\,N$
Now, $P = \overrightarrow F \cdot \overrightarrow V = Fv\cos \theta $
In this case, $\theta = 0^\circ $,
$\therefore \quad P = 4800 \times 0.4 = 1920\,W$
Now, $1hp = 746\,W$
$\therefore \quad P = \frac{{1920}}{{746}} = 2.57hp$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: An object of mass $m$ moves horizontally, increasing in speed from 0 to $v$ in a time $t$. The power necessary to accelerate the object during this time period is
explanation:
Power, $P=\dfrac{W}{t}$
Since, $\quad K_{i}=$ initial $K E=0$
$K_{f}=$ final $K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
From work-energy theorem,
Work done $=$ Change in $\mathrm{KE}$
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \quad P=\dfrac{K_{f}-K_{i}}{t}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}-0}{t} \\& \Rightarrow \quad P=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{2 t}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A car of mass $1000\,kg$ accelerates uniformly from rest to a velocity of $54\,km\,{h^{ - 1}}$ in $5\,s.$ The average power of the engine during this period in watt is (neglect friction)
explanation:
Given, $m = 100\,kg,\,\,v = 54\,km\,{h^{ - 1}}$
$ = \frac{{54 \times 5}}{{18}} = 15\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ and $t=5 \mathrm{~s}$
From equation of motion, velocity,
$v=u+a t$
$ \Rightarrow a = \frac{{15 - 0}}{5} = 3\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
Hence, $P_{a v}=\dfrac{1}{2} F v=\dfrac{1}{2} m a v$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 3 \times 15$
$ = 22500\,W$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A body of mass $10\,kg$ moves with a velocity $2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ along a circular path of radius $8\,m.$ The power produced by the body will be
explanation:
Given, $m = 10\,kg,v = 2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ and $R = 8\,m$
As, force, ${F_c} = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{R} = \frac{{10 \times {{(2)}^2}}}{8} = 5\,N$
Power is defined as the rate of change of energy in a system or the time rate of doing work.
$\Rightarrow \quad P=\dfrac{d E}{d t}=\dfrac{d W}{d t}$
Also, work $=$ force $\cdot$ displacement
$=F \cdot d=F \cdot d \cos \theta$
Since, for circular motion,
displacement is zero and $\theta=90^{\circ}$.
So, $\quad W=0$
Hence, power is also zero.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A tap supplies water at $22^\circ C,$ a man takes $1 L$ of water per min at $37^\circ C$ from the geyser. The power of the geyser is
explanation:
Given, ${T_1} = 22^\circ C,\,\,{T_2} = 37^\circ C,$
$\Delta T = {T_2} - {T_1} = (37 - 22)^\circ C$
and $m=1 L$ of water per $\min$,
i.e. $\quad t=60 \mathrm{~s}$
As, power, $P=\dfrac{W}{t}=\dfrac{m \times s \times \Delta T}{t}$
(where, $s=$ specific heat of water)
$=\dfrac{1 \times 4200 \times(37-22)}{60}$
$ = 1050\,W$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: The machine gun fires 240 bullets per minute. If the mass of each bullet is $10\,g$ and the velocity of the bullets is $600\,m{s^{ - 1}},$ the power (in $k W$ ) of the gun is
explanation:
Given, $n=240,=4$ bullets per second, $m = 10g = 10 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,kg,$
$t = 1\,s$ and $v = 600\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Work done by the gun
$=$ Total kinetic energy of the bullets
$=n \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=4 \times \dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \times(600)^{2}$
$=2 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \times 600 \times 600$
$\therefore \quad$ Power of gun $=\dfrac{\text { Work done }}{\text { Time taken }}$
$=\dfrac{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \times 600 \times 600}{1}$
$ = 7.2\,k\,W$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: $1000\,N$  force is required to lift a hook and ${\rm{10000 }}N$ force is requires to lift a load slowly. Find power required to lift hook with load with speed $v = 0.5\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$
explanation:
Net force required to lift a hook and load,
$F_{\text {net }}=1000+10000=11000 N$
$\because$ Power required to the lift hook,
$P=\dfrac{W}{t}$
We know that, $W=F_{\text {net }} d$
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore P=\dfrac{F_{\text {net }} d}{t}=F_{\text {net }}\left(\dfrac{d}{t}\right)=F_{\text {net }} v \\& \quad\left[\because v=\dfrac{d}{t}\right]\end{aligned}$
or $P = 11000 \times 0.5 = 5500\,W = 5.5\,kW$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: Water is falling on the blades of a turbine from a height of $25\,m$, and ${3 \times 10^{3} {~kg}}$ of water pours on the blade per minute. If whole of the energy is transferred to the turbine, the power delivered is
explanation:
Power delivered,
$P=\dfrac{m g h}{t}=\dfrac{3 \times 10^{3} \times 98 \times 25}{60}=12250 {~W}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A particle of mass ${\dfrac{10}{7} {~kg}}$ is moving in the positive ${x}$-direction. Its initial position is ${x=0}$ and initial velocity is ${1 {~m} / {s}}$. The velocity at ${x=10 m}$ is (use the graph given)
explanation:
Area under ${P-x}$ graph${=\int P d x=\int F \cdot v d x=\int\left(m \dfrac{d v}{d t}\right) v d x}$
${=\int_{1}^{v} m v^{2} d v=\left[\dfrac{m v^{3}}{3}\right]_{1}^{v}=\dfrac{10}{7 \times 3}\left(v^{3}-1\right)}$
From graph, area ${=\dfrac{1}{2}(2+4) \times 10=30}$
${\therefore \dfrac{10}{7 \times 3}\left(v^{3}-1\right)=30}$
${\therefore v=4 {~m} / {s}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A force ${\vec{F}=(3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}) N}$ acts on a $2\,kg$ movable object that moves from an initial position ${\vec{d}_{i}=(-3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}) m}$ to a final position ${\vec{d}_{f}=(5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}) m}$ in ${6 s}$. The average power delivered by the force during this interval is
explanation:
${\vec{d}=\vec{d}_{f}-\vec{d}_{i}=8 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}}$
${W=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}=24+24=48 {~J}}$
${P_{a v}=\dfrac{W}{t}=8 W}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A car of mass ${m}$ is accelerating on a smooth level road under a single force ${F}$. The power delivered to the car is constant and equal to ${P}$. If the car's velocity at an instant is ${v}$, then after traveling how much distance, it gets doubled?
explanation:
${P=F v=m\left(v \dfrac{d v}{d s}\right) v}$
${\therefore \int_{v}^{2 v} v^{2} d v=\dfrac{P}{m} \int_{0}^{s} d s \Rightarrow\left[\dfrac{v^{3}}{3}\right]_{v}^{2 v}}$
${=\dfrac{P s}{m} \Rightarrow s=\dfrac{7 m v^{3}}{3 P}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A force ${F}$ acting on a body depends on its displacement ${s}$ as ${F \propto s^{-1 / 3}}$. The power delivered by ${F}$ will depend on displacement as
explanation:
${P=F \cdot v=m \cdot a \cdot v=m v^{2} \dfrac{d v}{d s}\left[a=\dfrac{v d v}{d s}\right]}$
${\dfrac{v d v}{d s} \propto s^{-1 / 3}}$
On integration both sides, we get
${\therefore v \propto s^{1 / 3}}$
${\therefore P=F v \propto s^{0}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: Find the speed ${v}$ acquired by a car of mass ${m}$, driven with constant power ${P}$, and covers a distance ${x}$ traveled from origin.
explanation:
${P=F v}$
${P=mav}$
${P=m v^{2} \dfrac{d v}{d x} \quad\left[\because a=\dfrac{v d v}{d x}\right]}$
${\int_{0}^{v} v^{2} d v=\dfrac{P}{m} \int_{0}^{x} d x}$
${\dfrac{v^{3}}{3}=\dfrac{P}{m} x}$
$v=\left(\dfrac{3 x P}{m}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: At any instant of time ${t}$, the displacement of any particle is given by ${2 t-1}$ (SI unit) under the influence of force of $5\,N$. The value of instantaneous power is (in ${S I}$ unit):
explanation:
Given that ${x=2 t-1}$
${v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=2 {~ms}^{-1}}$
${P=F \cdot v=2 \times 5=10 {~W}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A particle of mass \[500\,g\] is at rest. It is free to move along a straight line. The power delivered to the particle varies with time according to the following graph : The momentum of the particle at \[t = 5\,s\] is
explanation:
Using work-energy theorem
${W=\Delta k E=}$ Area under the graph
${25=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m}}$
${25=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 \times 500 \times 10^{-3}}}$
$p = 5\,kg\,m/s.\,\,\,\,\,i.\,e.\,\,\,5\,Ns.$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: The ratio of powers of two motors is $\dfrac{3 \sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x}+1}$, that are capable of raising $300\,kg$ water in 5 minutes and $50\,kg$ water in 2 minutes respectively from a well of $100\,m$ deep. The value of $x$ will be
explanation:
Power is given by
$P=\dfrac{W}{t}=\dfrac{m g h}{t}$
$\frac{{{P_1}}}{{{P_2}}} = \frac{{{m_1}g{h_1}/{t_1}}}{{{m_2}g{h_2}/{t_2}}} = \frac{{{m_1}}}{{{m_2}}} \cdot \frac{{{t_2}}}{{{t_1}}}$
$ = \frac{{300}}{{50}} \times \frac{2}{5} = \frac{{12}}{5}$
$\therefore \dfrac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}=\dfrac{3 \times 4}{4+1}=\dfrac{3 \times \sqrt{16}}{\sqrt{16}+1}$
$=\dfrac{3 \times \sqrt{16}}{\sqrt{16}+1}$. On comparing with $\dfrac{3 \sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x}+1}$
$\Rightarrow x=16$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question:
Assertion: For two bodies, the sum of the mutual forces exerted between them is zero from Newton's third law

Reason: The sum of work done by the two forces must always cancel.
explanation: For two bodies, the sum of the mutual forces exerted between them is zero, following Newton's third law. However, the sum of the work done by these two forces may not always cancel out. While sometimes true, it may not hold in all cases. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:

Reason: Total energy of an isolated systern is not constant.
explanation:
"The universe, when viewed as an isolated system, maintains a constant total energy. If one part of the universe loses energy, another part must gain an equal amount of energy."
$\Rightarrow$ Reason is false
So correct option is (3)

question:
Assertion: A rigid body is not elastic.

Reason: If a force is applied on the elastic body, its dimensions shall change.
explanation: An ideal rigid body is one that maintains a perfectly definite and unchanging shape. An ideal rigid body is not compressible. So correct option is (1)

question:
Assertion: Soft steel can be made red hot by continued hammering on it, but hard steel cannot.

Reason: Energy transfer in case of soft iron is larger than in hard steel.
explanation: Hammer can do much more positive work in 'soft steel' as more deformation is possible by 'softness of steel' when compared to 'hardness of steel'. Hence, more energy transfer (by work-energy theorem) and so more rise in temperature in 'soft steel'. So correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: Mountain roads rarely go straight up the slope.

Reason: Slope of mountains are large therefore more chances of vehicle to slip from roads.
explanation: Mountain roads rarely go straight up the slope because the steep slope of mountains increases the chances of a vehicle slipping off the road. The reason correctly explains why mountain roads are typically constructed in a zigzag or 'switch-back pattern'. So correct option is (1)

question:
Assertion: Power of machine gun is determined by both, the number of bullets fired per second and kinetic energy of bullets.

Reason: Power of any machine is defined as work done (by it) per unit time.
explanation:
Kinetic energy of one bullet $=E_{k}$
Kinetic energy of $n$ bullet $=n E_{k}$
According to law of conservation of energy, the kinetic energy of bullets be equal to the work done by machine gun per sec.
Power $=\dfrac{\text { work }}{\text { time }}=n E_{k}$
So correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question:
Assertion: In one dimensional motion with constant acceleration, the power delivered is proportional to time.

Reason: In general, power can be expressed as dot product of force applied and displacement.
explanation:
Power
$P=\dfrac{W}{t}=\dfrac{\vec{F} \cdot \vec{s}}{t}=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{v}=(m a) \times a t=m a^{2} t$
$\therefore P \propto t$
Thus, power is proportional to time. So Assertion is correct and Reason is wrong. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question:
Assertion: Mass is not conserved separately, but mass and energy are conserved as a single entity called mass energy.

Reason: Mass and energy are inter-convertible in accordance with Einstein's relation, $E=m c^{2}$.
explanation: Mass is not conserved separately, but mass and energy are conserved as a single entity called mass-energy. This is in accordance with Einstein's famous relation, $E=m c^{2}$, which shows the equivalence between mass and energy. So correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
topic: Miscellaneous Problems on Work, Energy and Power
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Work, Energy and Power

question:
Assertion: A body cannot have energy without having momentum but it can have momentum without having energy.

Reason: Momentum and energy have different dimensions.
explanation: A body can possess potential energy due to its position, such as a compressed or stretched spring, even if it lacks momentum. However, when a body has no energy, its kinetic energy is zero, leading to zero momentum. Additionally, the dimensions of momentum, $\left[M L T^{-1}\right]$, differ from the dimensions of energy, $\left[M L^{2} T^{-2}\right]$, indicating that momentum and energy are distinct physical quantities. So correct option is (4)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: The power of a pump which raises $100\,kg$ of water in $10\,{\rm{sec}}$ to a height of $100\,m$ is $10\,kW$

Reason: A practical unit of power in British system is horse power (HP).
explanation:
The power of a pump can be calculated using the formula
$\therefore$ Power $=\dfrac{\text { work }}{\text { time }}=\dfrac{m g h}{t}=\dfrac{100 \times 10 \times 100}{10}$
$P=t m g h$, where ' $m$ ' is the mass, ' $g$ 'is the acceleration due to gravity, ' $h$ ' is the height, and ' $t$ ' is the time. Using this formula, the power is indeed $10\,kW$ in this case. It is a separate fact that British system of units uses 'Horse Power(HP)' as practical unit of power. So correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question:
Assertion: The instantaneous power of an agent is measured as the dot product of instantaneous velocity and the force acting on it at that instant.

Reason: The unit of instantaneous power is watt.
explanation:
The rate of doing work is called power. $\Rightarrow P=\dfrac{W}{t}$, If $\Delta W$ is the small amount of work done in a small time interval ( $\Delta t$ ), then instantaneous power is defined as $P=\lim _{\Delta t \rightarrow 0} \dfrac{\Delta W}{\Delta t}=\dfrac{d W}{d t}$,
$=\dfrac{\vec{F} \cdot d \vec{s}}{d t}=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{v}$
$\Rightarrow$ Assertion is correct
But it is a separate fact that the unit of power is Watt. So correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A cyclist rides up a hill at a constant velocity. Determine the power developed by the cyclist if the length of the connecting rod of the pedal is, $r = 25\,cm$, the time of revolution of the rod is $t=2 s$ and the mean force exerted by his foot on the pedal is $F = 15\,kg$.
explanation:
Linear velocity, $v=r \omega$
$=r\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{t}\right)=\dfrac{1}{4} \times \dfrac{2 \pi}{2}=\dfrac{\pi}{4} m s^{-1}$
$F=15 \times 9.8 N$
$\therefore P = F \times v = 15 \times 9.8 \times \frac{\pi }{4} = 115.5\,W$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A crane can lift up $10,000\,kg$ of coal in 1 hour from a mine of $180\,m$ depth. If the efficiency of the crane is $80 \%$, its input power must be ${\left( {g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}} \right)}$
explanation:
$\dfrac{80 P}{100}=\dfrac{m g h}{t}$
$ \Rightarrow P = \frac{{100}}{{80}} \times \frac{{mgh}}{t} = \frac{5}{4} \times \frac{{{{10}^4} \times 10 \times 180}}{{3600}}$
$ = 6.25\,kW.$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A particle moves with a velocity $(5\hat i + 3\hat j + 6\hat k)m{\rm{/}}s$ under the influence of a constant force $(10 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}+20 \hat{k}) N$. The instantaneous power applied to the particle is
explanation:
$P=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{V}$
$\Rightarrow P=(10 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}+20 \hat{k}) \cdot(5 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})$
$ \Rightarrow P = 50 + 30 + 120 = 200\,W$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question:
A 60 $HP$ electric motor lifts an elevator having a maximum total load capacity of 2000 $kg$. If the frictional force on the elevator is 4000 $N$, the speed of the elevator at full load is close to:
$\left( {1HP = 746\,W,\,g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}} \right)$
explanation:
$P=f v+M g v$
Applied power $=4000 \times v+20000 v$
$60 \times 746 = 4000v + 20000v$
$v \approx 1.9m/s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A car of weight $W$ is on an inclined road that rises by 100 $m$ over a distance of 1 $km$ and applies a constant frictional force $\frac{W}{{20}}$ on the car. While moving uphill on the road at a speed of $10\,m/s$, 1 the car needs power $P$. If it needs power $\dfrac{P}{2}$ while moving downhill at speed $v$ then value of $v$ is :
explanation:
While going up
$\left(\sin \theta=\tan \theta=\dfrac{1}{10}\right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951368/preptracker/questions/q_34846_explanation_1764951367695.png $\begin{gathered}{\left[W \sin \theta+\dfrac{W}{20}\right] 10=P} \\W(\sin \theta) 10+\dfrac{W}{20} 10=P \\\dfrac{3 W}{2}=P\end{gathered}$
While going down https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951369/preptracker/questions/q_34846_explanation_1764951368808.png $\frac{W}{{20}}v = \frac{P}{2} = \frac{{3W}}{4}$
$v = 15m/s.$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A wind - powered generator converts wind energy into electrical energy. Assume that the generator converts a fixed dfraction of the wind energy intercepted by its blades into electrical energy. For wind speed $v$, the electrical power output will be most likely proportional to
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955803/preptracker/questions/q_34827_explanation_1764955803033.png Let $\rho$ be the density of air and $A$ be the cross-section area of the blades. Consider cylinder with cross-sectional area A and length $x$
$\therefore K=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \rho A x v^{2},$
Time taken $\Delta t=\dfrac{x}{v}$
$\therefore$ Power generated, $P=\dfrac{K}{\Delta t}=\dfrac{1}{2} \rho A \cdot v^{3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: Two particles $A$ and $B$, move with constant velocities $\vec{v}_{1}$ and $\vec{v}_{2}$. At the initial moment their position vectors are $\vec{r}_{1}$ and $\vec{r}_{2}$ respectively. The condition for particles $A$ and $B$ for their collision is
explanation:
For two particles to collide, the direction of the relative velocity of one with respect to other should be directed towards the relative position of the other particle
i.e. $\dfrac{\vec{r}_{1}-\vec{r}_{2}}{\left|\vec{r}_{1}-\vec{r}_{2}\right|} \rightarrow$ direction of relative position of 1 w.r.t.2.& $\dfrac{\vec{v}_{1}-\vec{v}_{2}}{\left|\vec{v}_{1}-\vec{v}_{2}\right|} \rightarrow$ direction of velocity of 2 w.r..t.1
so for collision of A & B
$\dfrac{\vec{r}_{1}-\vec{r}_{2}}{\left|\vec{r}_{1}-\vec{r}_{2}\right|}=\dfrac{\vec{v}_{1}-\vec{v}_{2}}{\left|\vec{v}_{1}-\vec{v}_{2}\right|} \text { (Both are unit vectors) }$
subject: Physics
topic: Miscellaneous Problems on Work, Energy and Power
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Work, Energy and Power

question: Power applied to a particle varies with time as $P = \left( {3{t^2} - 2t + 1} \right)W$, there $t$ is in second. Find the change in its kinetic energy between $t = 2s$ and $t = 4s$
explanation:
$\Delta KE = W = \int\limits_2^4 {Pdt} = \int\limits_2^4 {\left( {3{t^2} - 2t + 1} \right)dt} $
$ = \left[ {{t^3} - {t^2} + t} \right]_2^4$
$ = 46J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: An engine develops 10 $kW$ of power. how much time will it take to lift a mass of 200 $kg$ to a height of $40\,m\left( {\,g = 10\,m/{s^2}} \right)$
explanation:
$P = \frac{W}{t}$
$\therefore t = \frac{W}{P} = \frac{{mgh}}{P} = \frac{{200 \times 10 \times 40}}{{10 \times {{10}^3}}} = 8\,s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A motor pump lifts 6 tonns of water from a well of depth 25 $m$ to the first floor of height 35 $m$ from the ground floor in 20 minutes. The power of the pump (in $k W$ ) is $[g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}]$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: An engine exerts a force $F = (20\widehat i - 3\widehat j + 5\widehat k)N$ and moves with velocity $v = (6\hat i + 20\hat j - 3\hat k)m/s$. The power of the engine ( in watt) is
explanation:
Here, Force, $\vec F = (20\hat i - 3\hat j + 5\hat k)N$
Velocity, $\vec v = (6\hat i + 20\hat j - 3\hat k)m/s$
$P = \vec F \cdot \vec v = (20\hat i - 3\hat j + 5\hat k) \cdot (6\hat i + 20\hat j - 3\hat k)$
$120 - 60 - 15 = 45W$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A particle of mass $m$ is moving in a circular path of constant radius $r$ such that centripetal acceleration is varying with time $t$ as $k^{2} r t^{2}$, where $k$ is constant. The power delivered to the particle by the force acting on it is
explanation:
The centripetal acceleration,
${a_c} = \frac{{{v^2}}}{r} = {k^2}r{t^2}$
$ \Rightarrow v = krt$
$\therefore $ Tangential acceleration
${a_t} = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = kr$
Power $ = {F_t}v = \left( {m{a_t}} \right)\left( {krt} \right)$
$ = \left( {mkr} \right)\left( {krt} \right) = m{k^2}{r^2}t$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A particle moves with a velocity $5\hat i - 3\hat j + 6\hat k$ $m{\text{/}}s$ under the influence of a constant force $F = 10\hat i + 10\hat j + 20\hat k$ $N$. The instantaneous power applied to the particle is
explanation:
$P=F \cdot v=(10 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}+20 \hat{k}) \cdot(5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$
$=50-30+120$
$ = 140\,J/s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: An engine pumps water continuously through a hose. Water leaves the hose with a velocity $v$ and $m$ is the mass per unit length of the water jet. What is the rate at which kinetic energy is imparted to water?
explanation:
As $m$ is the mass per unit length, then rate of mass per second $=\dfrac{m x}{t}=m v$
$\therefore$ Rate of $\mathrm{KE}=\dfrac{1}{2}(m v) v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: An electric lift with a maximum load of 2000 $kg$  (lift + passengers) is moving up with a constant speed of $1.5\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. The frictional force opposing the motion is 3000 $N$. The minimum power delivered by the motor to the lift in watts is:$(g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}})$
explanation:
Constant velocity $\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=0$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{W}+\mathrm{f} \\& =20000+3000 \\& =23000 N\end{aligned}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951234/preptracker/questions/q_34276_explanation_1764951234017.png $\Rightarrow$ Power $=\mathrm{Tv}$
$=23000 \times 1.5=34500 \text { watts }$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: The energy that will be ideally radiated by a 100 $k W$ transmitter in 1 hour is
explanation:
$\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{P} \times \mathrm{t}=100 \times 10^{3} \times 3600$
$=36 \times 10^{7} J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: Water falls from a height of 60 $m$ at the rate of 15 $kg/s$ to operate a turbine. The losses due to frictional force are $10 \%$ of the input energy. How much power is generated by the turbine? $\left( {g = 10\,m/{s^2}} \right)$
explanation:
Power generated by Turbine $=P$
$\begin{aligned}& P=0.9\left(\dfrac{\Delta m}{\Delta t}\right) g h \\& P=0.9 \times 15 \times 10 \times 60 \\& =8.1 \mathrm{~kW}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A spring of force constant $k$ is cut into lengths of ratio 1:2:3. They are connected in series and the new force constant is  $k'$. Then they are connected in parallel and force constant is $k''$. Then ${k^\prime }:k''$ is
explanation:
Length of the spring segments $=\dfrac{\ell}{6}, \dfrac{\ell}{3}, \dfrac{\ell}{2}$
As we know $K \propto \dfrac{1}{\ell}$
So spring constants for spring segments will be
$K_{1}=6 K, K_{2}=3 K, K_{3}=2 K$
For parallel combination
$K^{\prime \prime}=K_{1}+K_{2}+K_{3}=11 K$
For series combination
$K^{\prime}=K$ (As it will become original spring)
so $K^{\prime}: K^{\prime \prime}=1: 11$
subject: Physics
topic: Miscellaneous Problems on Work, Energy and Power
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Work, Energy and Power

question:
Assertion: Kilowatt hour is the unit of power.

Reason: One kilowatt hour is equivalent to $1 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~J}$.
explanation:
$1 \mathrm{KWhr}=10^{3} \times \dfrac{J}{s} \times 3600 s=3.6 \times 10^{5} J$
So option (3) is correct.

question: A body is initially at rest. It undergoes onedimensional motion with constant acceleration. The power delivered to it at time $t$ is proportional to
explanation:
Power $P=F v=m a v$
If $a$ is constant then $v=a t(u=0)$
$P=m a^{2} t \Rightarrow P \alpha t$

question: A man squatting on the ground gets straight up and stands. The force of reaction of ground on the man during the process is
explanation: When the man gets straight up and stands, reaction of ground on the $m a n=m g$. However, when the man is squatting on the ground, reaction of ground is more than $mg$, as the man is to exert some extra force on the ground to stand up.

question: Which of the diagram in figure correctly shows the change in kinetic energy of an iron sphere falling freely in a lake having sufficient depth to impart it a terminal velocity?
explanation: When an iron sphere falls freely in a lake, its motion is accelerated due to gravity and retarded due to viscous froce. The overall effect is increase in velocity and hence increase in KE till the sphere acquires terminal velocity, which is constant. Choice (3) is correct.

explanation: When a pendulum oscillates in air, it loses energy continuously in overcoming resistance due to air. Therefore, total mechanical energy of the pendulum decreases continuously with time. The variation of total mechanical energy E with time $t$ is shown correctly by curve (3).

question: A body is moving unidirectionally from rest under the influence of a source of constant power supplying energy. Which of the diagrams shown in figure correctly shows the displacement-time curve for its motion?
explanation:
Here, $P=\left[M L^{2} T^{-3}\right]=$ constant
As, mass $\mathrm{M}$ of body is fixed,
$\begin{aligned}& L^{2} T^{-3}=\text { constant } \\& \dfrac{L^{2}}{T^{3}}=\text { constant or } L \propto T^{3 / 2} \text { or }\end{aligned}$
displacement $(\mathrm{S}) \propto T^{3 / 2}$.

question: If a ball collides with a wall at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ and rebounds perpendicularly with its initial direction then find the impulse acting on the ball
explanation:
$\Delta P=P_{2}-P_{1}$
$\Delta P_{2}=-2 m v \cos 45^{\circ} \hat{i}$
