question: An engine pump is used to pump a liquid of density $\rho$ continuously through a pipe of cross-sectional area $A$. If the speed of flow of the liquid in the pipe is $v$, then the rate at which kinetic energy is being imparted to the liquid is
explanation:
Volume of liquid flowing per second $=A v$
Mass of liquid flowing per second, $m=A \rho v$
The rate at which kinetic energy is being imparted to the liquid is
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{d m}{d t} v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2}(A v \rho) v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} A \rho v^{3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A force of $2\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k\,N$ acts on a body for 4 second, produces a displacement of $(3\hat i + 4\hat j + 5\hat k)m$. The power used is
explanation:
$P=\dfrac{\vec{F} \cdot \vec{s}}{t}$
$ = \frac{{(2\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k) \cdot (3\hat i + 4\hat j + 5\hat k)}}{4} = \frac{{38}}{4} = 9.5\,W$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A body of mass $m$ accelerates uniformly from rest to $v_{1}$ in time $t_{1}$. As a function of time $\mathrm{t}$, the instantaneous power delivered to the body is
explanation:
$P=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{v}=m a \times a t=m a^{2} t[$ as $\mathrm{u}=0]$
$=m\left(\dfrac{v_{1}}{t_{1}}\right)^{2} t=\dfrac{m v_{1}^{2}}{t_{1}^{2}} \times t \quad\left[a=v_{1} / t_{1}\right]$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A particle of mass $m$ is driven by a machine that delivers a constant power $K$ watts. If the particle starts from the rest the velocity of the particle at time $'t'$ is
explanation:
$p=F v=K$
$\begin{aligned}& m \dfrac{d v}{d t} v=K \\& v d v=\dfrac{K}{m} d t \\& \dfrac{v^{2}}{2}=\dfrac{K}{m} t \Rightarrow v=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 K}{m}} t\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A motor is used to deliver water at a certain rate through a given horizontal pipe to deliver ntime the water through the same pipe in the same time the power of the motor must be increased as follows:
explanation:
If the motor pumps water (density $=\rho$ ) continuously through a pipe of area of cross-section A with velocity $v$, then mass flowing out per second.
$m = Av\rho {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Rate of increase of kinetic energy
$ = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{dm}}{{dt}}{v^2} = \frac{1}{2}(Av\rho ){v^2} = \frac{1}{2}A\rho {v^3}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (2)$
$\dfrac{P^{\prime}}{P}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} A \rho v^{13}}{\dfrac{1}{2} A \rho v^{3}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{P^{\prime}}{P}=\left(\dfrac{v^{\prime}}{v}\right)^{3}$
Now, $\dfrac{m^{\prime}}{m}=\dfrac{A \rho v^{\prime}}{A \rho v}=\dfrac{v^{\prime}}{v}$
As, $\quad m^{\prime}=n m \Rightarrow v^{\prime}=n v$
$\therefore \dfrac{P^{\prime}}{P}=n^{3} \Rightarrow P^{\prime}=n^{3} P$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: An athlete of mass 60 $kg$ skips at the rate of 20 steps per minute through an average height of 25 $cm$. The power developed is:
explanation:
$W=m g h=60 \times 9.8 \times(25 / 100)=147 J$
Energy in one minute $=147 \times 20=2940 \mathrm{~J}$
$\text { Power }=\dfrac{2490}{60}=49 \mathrm{~W}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question:
A motorcycle of mass $m$ resting on a frictionless road moves under the influence of a constant force $F$. The work done by this force in moving the motorcycle is given by $F^{2} t^{2} / 2 m$, where $\mathrm{t}$ is the time in seconds.
Ratio of instantaneous power to average power of the motorcycle in $t = T$ second is:
explanation:
For instantaneous power
$\begin{aligned}& P_{i n s}=\dfrac{d W}{d t}=\dfrac{F^{2}}{2 m} \cdot 2 t \Rightarrow P_{\text {ins }}=\dfrac{2 F^{2} T}{2 m} \\& P_{a v g}=\dfrac{\Delta W}{T}=\dfrac{F^{2}\left(T^{2}-0\right)}{2 m T}=\dfrac{F^{2} T}{2 m} \\& \dfrac{P_{\text {ins }}}{P_{\text {avg }}}=2\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A pump motor is used to deliver water at a certain rate from a given pipe. To obtain thrice as much water from the pipe in the same time power of the motor has to be increased:
explanation:
The power through a pipe
$P \propto v^{3}$
where $v$ - velocity of liquid
The rate of volume of the liquid that comes out from a pipe is $\dfrac{d V}{d t} \propto v$
If rate is to be made three times then velocity is also to be made three times
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}\right)^{3}=\left(\dfrac{v_{1}}{3 v_{2}}\right)^{3} \\& P_{2}=27 P_{1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: The change in kinetic energy per unit 'space' (distance) is equal to
explanation:
From work energy theorem $\Delta K=W$
$\dfrac{\Delta K}{S}=\dfrac{W}{S}=\text { Force }$
subject: Physics
topic: Miscellaneous Problems on Work, Energy and Power
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Work, Energy and Power

question: Two particles having position vectors $8 \hat{j},-8 \hat{i}$ metres are moving with velocities $\vec{v}_{1}=4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}$ and $\vec{v}_{2}=\alpha \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}$. If they collide after 2 seconds, the value of ' $\alpha$ ' is
explanation:
The displacement vector $\Delta \vec{r}$ between the particles is
$\Delta \,\vec r = {{\vec r}_2} - {{\vec r}_1} = - 8\hat i - 8\hat j$
$\left| {\Delta \,\vec r} \right| = \sqrt {{{( - 8)}^2} + {{( - 8)}^2}} = 8\sqrt 2 \,\,\,\,\,(1)$
The relative velocity is given by
${\overrightarrow v _{rel}} = {\overrightarrow v _2} - {\overrightarrow v _1} = (\alpha - 4)\hat i + 4\hat j$
$\left| {{{\overrightarrow v }_{rel}}} \right| = \sqrt {{{(\alpha - 4)}^2} + 16} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
When they collide in time $t$
$\left| {{{\overrightarrow v }_{rel}}} \right| = \frac{{\left| {\Delta \vec r} \right|}}{t} \Rightarrow \alpha = 8.$
subject: Physics
topic: Miscellaneous Problems on Work, Energy and Power
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Work, Energy and Power

question: An engine pumps water continuously through a hole. Speed with which water passes through the hole nozzle is $v$ and $k$ is the mass per unit length of the water jet as it leaves the nozzle. Find the rate at which kinetic energy is being imparted to the water.
explanation:
$K = \frac{{mass}}{{length}} = \frac{{dm}}{{dt}}$
$KE = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$
$ \Rightarrow P = $
$\frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{dm}}{{dt}}} \right){v^2} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{dm}}{{dx}} \times \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right){v^2} = \frac{1}{2}kv{v^2} = \frac{1}{2}k{v^3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A vehicle of mass $M$ is accelerated on a horizontal frictionless road under a force changing its velocity from $u$ to $v$ in distance $S$. A constant power $P$ is given by the engine of the vehicle, then $v=$
explanation:
Using $P=F v=M\left(\dfrac{d v}{d t}\right) v$
i.e., $\quad v^{2} d v=\dfrac{P}{M} v d t=\dfrac{P}{M} d S$
Integrating $\int_{u}^{v} v^{2} d v=\dfrac{P}{M} \int_{0}^{s} d S$
$\begin{aligned}v^{3}-u^{3} & =\dfrac{3 P S}{M} \\\Rightarrow v & =\left[\dfrac{3 P S}{M}+u^{3}\right]^{1 / 3}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A body of mass 1 $kg$ begins to move under the action of a time dependent force $\vec{F}=\left(2 t \hat{i}+3 t^{2} \hat{j}\right) N$, where $\hat{i}$ and $\hat{j}$ are unit vectors along $x$ and $y$ axis. What power will be developed by the force at the time $t$ ?
explanation:
$\vec{F}=2 t \hat{i}+3 t^{2} \hat{j}$
$\begin{aligned}& m \dfrac{d \vec{v}}{d t}=2 t \hat{i}+3 t^{2} \hat{j} \quad \quad(m=1 \mathrm{~kg}) \\\Rightarrow & \int_{0}^{\bar{v}} d \vec{v}=\int_{0}^{\bar{v}}\left(2 t \hat{i}+3 t^{2} \hat{j}\right) d t \Rightarrow \vec{v}=t^{2} \hat{i}+t^{3} \hat{j} \\\text { Power } & =\vec{F} \cdot \vec{v}=\left(2 t^{3}+3 t^{5}\right) W\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A car of mass ' $m$ ' is driven with acceleration ' $a$ ' along a straight level road against a constant external resistive force ' $R$ '. When the velocity of the car is ' $v$ ', the rate of which the engine of the car is doing work will be
explanation:
Force is required to oppose the resistive force $\mathrm{R}$ and also to accelerate the body of mass $\mathrm{m}$ with acceleration $a$.
Force $F=R+m a$
$\therefore$ Power $=(\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{m} a) v$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: If a machine gun fires $n$ bullets per second each with kinetic energy $K$, then the power of the machine gun is
explanation: $P = \frac{W}{t} \Rightarrow P = \frac{{nK}}{{1s}} = nK$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A block of mass $m$ is allowed to slide down a fixed smooth inclined plane of angle $\theta$ and length $l$. What is the magnitude of power developed by the gravitational force when the block reaches the bottom?
explanation:
The force that accelerates the block is $m g \sin \theta \Rightarrow a=g \sin \theta$
Velocity of the block after travelling distance $l$ is
$v^{2}=2 g \sin \theta l$
The instantaneous power is
$\begin{aligned}& P=F v=m g \sin \theta \sqrt{2 g \sin \theta l} \\& P=\sqrt{2 m^{2} g^{3} \sin ^{3} \theta l}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A motor of power $p_{0}$ is used to deliver water at certain rate through a given horizontal pipe. To increase the rate of water through the same pipe $n$ times, the power of the motor is increased to $p_{1}$. The ratio of $p_{1}$ to $p_{0}$ is
explanation:
Power of motor initially $=p_{0}$
Let, rate of flow of motor $=(x)$
Since, power, $p_{0}=\dfrac{\text { work }}{\text { time }}=\dfrac{m g y}{t}=m g\left(\dfrac{y}{t}\right)$
$\frac{y}{t} = x = $ rate of flow of water
$ = mgx\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
If rate of flow of water is increased by $n$ times,i.e., $(\mathrm{n} x)$
Increased power, $p_{1}=\dfrac{m g y^{\prime}}{t}=m g\left(\dfrac{y^{\prime}}{t}\right)$,
$=\operatorname{nmg} x$
The ratio of power
$\dfrac{p_{1}}{p_{0}}=\dfrac{n m g x}{m g x}=\dfrac{n}{1} \Rightarrow p_{1}: p_{0} \Rightarrow n: 1$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: An electric motor of 100 $W$ power drives a stirrer in a water bath. Only $80 \%$ of the energy supplied to the motor is used up in stirring water. The work done on water in 1 second is
explanation:
Power consumed
$ = \frac{{80}}{{100}} \times 100\,W = 80\,W = 80\,J{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A car of mass 500 $kg$ is driven with acceleration $1\,m/{s^2}$ along straight level road against constant external resistance of 1000 $N$. When the velocity is 5 $m/s$ the rate at which the engine is working is
explanation:
Here the car is under acceleration
$P = Fv\quad \Rightarrow a = \frac{{F - {f_r}}}{m} \Rightarrow 1 = \frac{{F - 1000}}{{500}} \Rightarrow F = 1500 \Rightarrow P = 1500 \times 5 = 7500{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} Watt$
$ \Rightarrow P = 1500 \times 5 = 7500\,Watt.$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: An elevator that can carry a maximum load of 1800$kg$  (elevator + passengers) is moving up with a constant speed of $2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. The frictional force opposing the motion is 400 $N$. What is minimum power delivered by the motor to the elevator?
explanation:
Here, $m = 1800\,kg$
Frictional force, $f = 4000N$
$v = 2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Downward force on elevator is
$F = mg + f$
$ = \left( {1800kg \times 10m{s^{ - 2}}} \right) + 4000N = 22000N$
The motor must supply enough power to balance this force. Hence,
$P = Fv = \left( {22000N} \right)\left( {2m{s^{ - 1}}} \right) = 44\,kW$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: Power of a water pump is 2 $kW$. If $g = 10\,m/{\sec ^2}$, the amount of water it can raise in one minute to a height of 10 $m$ is
explanation:
$P = \frac{{mgh}}{t} \Rightarrow m = \frac{{p \times t}}{{gh}} = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^3} \times 60}}{{10 \times 10}} = 1200\,kg$
As volume $ = \frac{{ mass }}{{ density }} \Rightarrow V = \frac{{1200\,kg}}{{{{10}^3}\,kg/{m^3}}} = 1.2\,{m^3}$
Volume $ = 1.2\,{m^3} = 1.2 \times {10^3}{\mathop{\rm litre}\nolimits} {\rm{ }} = 1200\,\,{\mathop{\rm litre}\nolimits} .$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A 60 $kg$ man runs up a staircase in 12 seconds while a 50 $kg$ man runs up the same staircase in 11 seconds. The ratio of the rate of doing their work is
explanation:
$P=\dfrac{m g h}{t} \Rightarrow \dfrac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}=\dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}} \times \dfrac{t_{2}}{t_{1}}$
[ as $h=$ constant $]$
$\therefore \dfrac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}=\dfrac{60}{50} \times \dfrac{11}{12}=\dfrac{11}{10} \text {. }$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: For power to be constant, the force has to vary with speed as:
explanation:
Power: $P = Fv$
For power to be constant: $F \propto \dfrac{1}{v}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A car of mass $m$ starts from rest and accelerates so that instantaneous power delivered to the car has a constant magnitude $P_{0}$. The instantaneous velocity of this car is proportional to
explanation:
Constant power of a car $P_{0}=F \cdot v=$ ma. $v$
$\begin{gathered}P_{0}=m \dfrac{d v}{d t} \cdot v \Rightarrow P_{0} d t=m v d v \Rightarrow P_{0} . t=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{2} \\v=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 P_{0} t}{m}} \Rightarrow v \alpha \sqrt{t} .\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A one -ton car moves with a constant velocity of $15 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ on a rough horizontal road. The total resistance to the motion of the car is $12 \%$ of the weight of the car. The power required to keep the car moving with the same constant velocity of $15\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ is [Take $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ ]
explanation:
The driving force on the car is
$F = \frac{{12}}{{100}} \times 1000 \times 10N = 1200N$
$P = Fv = 1200N \times 15\,m{s^{ - 1}} = 18\,kW$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A particle is acted upon by a force of constant magnitude which is always perpendicular to the velocity of the particle, the motion of the particle takes place in a plane. It follows that
explanation: When a force of constant magnitude which is perpendicular to the velocity of particle acts on a particle, work done is zero and hence change in kinetic energy is zero.

options.text: It moves in a straight line

explanation:
The gun has more mass than bullet but momentums are equal in manguitude. So
$K . E_{G U N} < K . E_{\text {BULLET }}$

options.text: Less than the kinetic energy of the bullet
equal to the kinetic energy of the bullet
double that of the kinetic enery of the bullet
Greater than the kinetic energy of the bullet

question: Power supplied to a particle of mass 2 $kg$  varies with time as $P=\dfrac{3 t^{2}}{2}$ watt. Here $t$ is in second. If velocity of particle at $t=0$ is $v = 0$, the velocity of particle at time $t = 2s$ will be:
explanation:
From work-energy theorem,
$\Delta KE = {W_{net}}\,or\,{K_f} - {K_i} = \int P dt$
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} - 0 = \int_0^2 {\left( {\frac{3}{2}{t^2}} \right)} \,dt\,\,{\mathop{\rm or}\nolimits} \,{v^2} = \left[ {\frac{{{r^3}}}{2}} \right]_0^2$
$ \Rightarrow v = 2\,m/s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: An electric pump is used to fill an overhead tank of capacity $9\,{m^3}$ kept at a height of 10 $m$ above the ground. If the pump takes 5 minutes to fill the tank by consuming 10 $kW$ power the efficiency of the pump should be (Take $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ )
explanation:
Work done in raising water $=m g h$
$\therefore W=($ volume $\times$ density $) g h=(9 \times 1000) \times 10 \times 10$ $\Rightarrow W=9 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~J}$
$\therefore$ Useful power $ = \frac{{ work }}{{ time }} = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^5}}}{{5 \times 60}} = 3\,kW$
$\therefore$ Efficiency $=\dfrac{3}{10} \times 100=30 \%$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A truck of mass 30000 $kg$ moves up an inclined plane of slope 1 in 100 at speed of 30 $kmph$. The power of the truck is (Given $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ )
explanation:
A truck is moving on an inclined plane therefore only component of weight
$(m g \sin \theta)$ will oppose the upward motion
Power $=$ force $\times$ velocity $=m g \sin \theta \times v$
$ = 30000 \times 10 \times \left( {\frac{1}{{100}}} \right) \times \frac{{30 \times 5}}{{18}} = 25\,kW.$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Average Power

question: A force $(4 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) N$ acting on a body maintains its velocity at $(2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k)m{s^{ - 1}}$. The power exerted is
explanation:
Here, Force, $\vec{F}=(4 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) N$
Velocity, $\vec{v}=(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) m s^{-1}$
Power, $P=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{v}=(4 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})$
$=(8+2-6) W=4 W$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A constant power $P$ is applied to a particle of mass $m$. The distance travelled by the particle when its velocity increases from $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ is (neglect friction):
explanation:
$P=F v=m a v \Rightarrow a=\dfrac{P}{m v}$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow v \dfrac{d v}{d s}=\dfrac{P}{m v} \Rightarrow v^{2} d v=\dfrac{P}{m} d s \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{P}{m} \int_{0}^{s} d s=\int_{v_{1}}^{v_{2}} v^{2} d v \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{P}{m} s=\dfrac{1}{3}\left(v_{2}^{3}-v_{1}^{3}\right) \Rightarrow s=\dfrac{m}{3 P}\left(v_{2}^{3}-v_{1}^{3}\right)\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: A body is moved along a straight line by a machine delivering constant power. The distance moved by the body in time $t$ is proportional to
explanation:
$P=F v=m a v=m\left(\dfrac{d v}{d t}\right) v \Rightarrow \dfrac{P}{m} d t=v d v$$\Rightarrow \dfrac{P}{m} \times t=\dfrac{v^{2}}{2} \Rightarrow v=\left(\dfrac{2 P}{m}\right)^{1 / 2}(t)^{1 / 2}$
Now $\quad s=\int v d t=\left(\dfrac{2 P}{m}\right)^{1 / 2}(t)^{1 / 2} d t$
$\therefore s=\left(\dfrac{2 P}{m}\right)^{1 / 2}\left[\dfrac{2 t^{3 / 2}}{3}\right] \Rightarrow s \alpha t^{3 / 2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Instantaneous Power

question: An ant starts from the origin and crawls $10\,cm$ along the $X$-axis and then $20\,cm$ along the $Y$-axis. The dot product of the ant's displacement vector with the position vector of a point that makes $45^{\circ}$ with the $X$-axis and has a magnitude of $\sqrt{2} {~cm}$ is
explanation:
Displacement, $s=(10 \hat{i}+20 \hat{j}) {cm}$
Position, $r=\left(r_{x} \hat{i}+r_{y} \hat{j}\right)=(1 \hat{i}+1 \hat{j}) {cm}$
Now, dot product of $s$ and $r$
$\vec{s} \cdot \vec{r}=(10 \hat{i}+20 \hat{j}) \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j})$
$=(10+20) \quad(\therefore \hat{i} \cdot \hat{i}=1, \hat{j} \cdot \hat{j}=1, \hat{i} \cdot \hat{j}=0)$$=30 {~cm}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: The angle between force $F=3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}$ and displacement $d=5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ is
explanation:
Given, force $\vec{F}=3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}$
$\Rightarrow|\vec{F}|=\sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}+5^{2}}=\sqrt{50}$
Displacement $\vec{d}=5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$
$|\vec{d}|=\sqrt{5^{2}+4^{2}+3^{2}}=\sqrt{50}$
$\vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}=|\vec{F}||\vec{d}| \cos \theta \quad$ [Dot product]
$\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\dfrac{\vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}}{|\vec{F}||\vec{d}|}=\dfrac{15+16-15}{50}=0.32$
$\Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}(0.32)$
Hence, correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: Find the angle between the two vectors $\vec{a}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}, \vec{b}=5 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$
explanation:
Given, $\vec{a}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}$ and$\vec{b}=5 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$
Since, $\quad \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \theta$
$\therefore \cos \theta=\dfrac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}$
$\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\dfrac{(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}) \cdot(5 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})}{\sqrt{3^{2}+2^{2}+5^{2}} \sqrt{5^{2}+3^{2}+1^{2}}}$
$\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\dfrac{15+6+5}{\sqrt{9+4+25} \sqrt{25+9+1}}$
$=\dfrac{26}{\sqrt{38} \sqrt{35}}=\dfrac{26}{\sqrt{1330}}$
$\therefore \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{26}{\sqrt{1330}}\right)$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: Find the component of vector $\vec{P}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}$ along the direction of vector $\vec{Q}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}$.
explanation:
Given, $\vec{P}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}$ and $\vec{Q}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}$
As we know that,
Magnitude of component of $\vec{P}$ along
$\vec{Q}=P \cos \theta=\dfrac{\vec{P} \cdot \vec{Q}}{|\vec{Q}|}$
$=\dfrac{(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j})}{\sqrt{1^{2}+1^{2}}}=\dfrac{2+3}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{5}{\sqrt{2}}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: The angle between vector $\vec{Q}$ and the resultant of $(2 \vec{Q}+2 \vec{P})$ and $(2 \vec{Q}-2 \vec{P})$ is
explanation:
Let the resultant be ${\vec R}$ its value is given by
$\therefore \quad \vec{R}=(2 \vec{Q}+2 \vec{P})+(2 \vec{Q}-2 \vec{P})$
or $\vec{R}=4 \vec{Q}$
Thus, resultant $\vec{R}$ is in the same direction along vector $\vec{Q}$. So $\vec{R}$ is parallel to $\vec{Q}$.
$\therefore$ The angle between vector $\vec{Q}$ and the resultant is zero.
So, option (1) is correct.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question:
The condition under which vectors $(a+b)$ and
($a-b$ ) should be at right angles to each other is
explanation:
The dot product of two vectors should be equal to zero, i.e.
$(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{a^2} - {b^2} = 0$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,|a| = |b|$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: If two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ having equal magnitude $R$ are inclined at an angle $\theta$, then
explanation:
Given : $|\vec{A}|=|\vec{B}|=R$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951671/preptracker/questions/q_36319_explanation_1764951670667.png
$|\vec{A}+\vec{B}|=\sqrt{|\vec{A}|^{2}+|\vec{B}|^{2}+2|\vec{A}||\vec{B}| \cos \theta}$
$=\sqrt{R^{2}+R^{2}+2 R^{2} \cos \theta}$
$=\sqrt{2 R^{2}+2 R^{2} \cos \theta}$
$=\sqrt{2 R^{2}(1+\cos \theta)}=R \sqrt{2\left(1+2 \cos ^{2} \dfrac{\theta}{2}-1\right)}$
$\left(\because \cos \theta=2 \cos ^{2} \dfrac{\theta}{2}-1\right)$
$=R \sqrt{4 \cos ^{2} \dfrac{\theta}{2}}=2 R \cos \dfrac{\theta}{2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: Given that $\vec{A}+\vec{B}=\vec{R}$ and $A^{2}+B^{2}=R^{2}$. The angle between $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ is
explanation:
$\cos \theta = \frac{{{R^2} - {A^2} - {B^2}}}{{2AB}} = \frac{{{R^2} - {R^2}}}{{2AB}} = 0$
$\therefore \theta=\pi / 2$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are unit vectors and angle between them is $\dfrac{\pi}{k}$. If $\vec{a}+2 \vec{b}$ and $5 \vec{a}-4 \vec{b}$ are perpendicular to each other then find the integer value of $k$.
explanation:
$(\vec{a}+2 \vec{b}) \cdot(5 \vec{a}-4 \vec{b})=0 \Rightarrow 5 a^{2}+10 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}-8 \vec{b}^{2}$
$-4 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} \Rightarrow-3+6 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=0$
$\Rightarrow a b \cos \theta=\dfrac{3}{6} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\dfrac{1}{2}=\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{3} \Rightarrow k=3$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: If ${\vec{a}, \vec{b}}$ and ${\vec{c}}$ are non-zero coplanar vectors, such that ${\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=0}$. If ${|\vec{a}|=1}$ unit and ${|\vec{c}|=6}$ units. The value of ${|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}|}$. Is
explanation:
${\because \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=0}$
It means ${\vec{a}}$ and ${\vec{b}}$ are perpendicular to each other. Similarly, ${\ddot{b}}$ and ${\vec{c}}$ are perpendicular to each other. So, we can say that ${\vec{a}}$ and ${\vec{c}}$ are parallel to each other
$\therefore \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=|a||c| \cos 0^{\circ}=1 \times 6=6 \text { units }$
subject: Physics

question: If ${\vec{A}=3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}}$ and ${\vec{B}=a \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}}$ are perpendicular to each other. The value of ${a}$ is
explanation:
For vectors to be perpendicular,
$\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}=0 \Rightarrow a=-4$

question:

explanation: $\begin{aligned}& \cos \theta=\dfrac{A \cdot B}{A B} \dfrac{(\hat{i}+\hat{j}) \cdot(\hat{i}-\hat{j})}{\sqrt{1^{2}+1^{2}} \times \sqrt{1^{2}+(-1)^{2}}}=\dfrac{1-1}{2}=0 \\& \Rightarrow \theta=90^{\circ}\end{aligned}$

explanation: The two vectors which are perpendicular to each other will have their dot product equal to zero.

question: The component of vector $\vec{A}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}$ along the vector $\vec{B}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}$ is
explanation: $\dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{|B|}=\dfrac{(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j})}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{2+3}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{5}{\sqrt{2}}$

question: If $\vec{a}+\vec{b}=\vec{c}$ and $a+b=c$, then the angle between $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ is
explanation:
Here, $\vec{a}+\vec{b}=\vec{c}$ and $c=a+b$
Let $\theta$ be angle between $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$.
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore c=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+2 a b \cos \theta} \\& a+b=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+2 a b \cos \theta}\end{aligned}$
Squaring both sides, we get
$\begin{aligned}& (a+b)^{2}=a^{2}+b^{2}+2 a b \cos \theta \\& a^{2}+b^{2}+2 a b=a^{2}+b^{2}+2 a b \cos \theta \\& 2 a b-2 a b \cos \theta=0 \text { or } 1-\cos \theta=0 \\& \cos \theta=1 \text { or } \theta=\cos ^{-1}(1)=0^{0} .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: Vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ include an angle $\theta$ between them.If $(\vec{A}+\vec{B})$ and $(\vec{A}-\vec{B})$ respectively subtend angles $\alpha$ and $\beta$ with $\mathrm{A}$, then $(\tan \alpha+\tan \beta)$ is
explanation:
The vector diagram is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951257/preptracker/questions/q_34563_explanation_1764951256927.png $\tan \alpha = \left( {\frac{{B\sin \theta }}{{A + B\cos \theta }}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Where $\alpha $ is the angle made by the vector $(\vec A + \vec B)$ with ${\vec A}$.
Similarly, $\tan \beta = \frac{{B\sin \theta }}{{A - B\cos \theta }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Where $\beta $ is the angle made by the vector $(\vec A - \vec B)$ with ${\vec A}$.
Note that the angle between ${\vec A}$ and $( - \vec B)$ is $(180^\circ - \theta ).$ Adding (1) and (2), we get
$\tan \alpha + \tan \beta = \frac{{B\sin \theta }}{{A + B\cos \theta }} + \frac{{B\sin \theta }}{{A - B\cos \theta }}$
$ = \frac{{2AB\sin \theta }}{{({A^2} - {B^2}{{\cos }^2}\theta )}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: Two forces $3 N$ and $2 N$ are at an angle $\theta$ such that the resultant is $R$. The first force is now increased to $6 N$ and the resultant becomes $2 R$. The value of $\theta$ is
explanation:
$A=3 N, B=2 N$
then $R=\sqrt{A^{2}+B^{2}+2 A B \cos \theta}$
$R = \sqrt {9 + 4 + 12\cos \theta } \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Now $A=6 N, B=2 N$ then
$2R = \sqrt {36 + 4 + 24\cos \theta } \,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) and (2) we get $\cos \theta=-\dfrac{1}{2} \therefore \theta=120^{\circ}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: Given that $\vec{A}+\vec{B}=\vec{C}$ and $\vec{C}$ is $\perp$ to $\vec{A}$. Further if $|\vec{A}|=|\vec{C}|$, then what is the angle between $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$
explanation:
Here, $\vec{A}+\vec{B}=\vec{C}$ and $\vec{B}=\vec{C}-\vec{A}$
Since $\vec{C} \perp \vec{A}$ therefore
$B^{2}=C^{2}+A^{2}-2 C A \cos \left(90^{\circ}\right)$
Also, $|\vec{C}|=|\vec{A}|$
$B^{2}=2 A^{2} \Rightarrow B=\sqrt{2} A$
Now, $A^{2}+B^{2}+2 A B \cos \theta=C^{2}=A^{2}$
Therefore we find $\cos \theta=-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
This gives $\theta=\dfrac{3 \pi}{4}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: If unit vectors $\hat{A}$ and $\hat{B}$ are inclined at an angle $\theta$, then $|\hat{A}-\hat{B}|$ is
explanation:
$|\hat{A}-\hat{B}|^{2}=(\hat{A}-\hat{B}) \cdot(\hat{A}-\hat{B})$
$=\hat{A} \cdot \hat{A}-\hat{A} \cdot \hat{B}-\hat{B} \cdot \hat{A}+\hat{B} \cdot \hat{B}$
$=1-\hat{A} \cdot \hat{B}-\hat{A} \cdot \hat{B}+1$
$=2-2 \cos \theta=2(1-\cos \theta)$
$=2\left(2 \sin ^{2} \dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)=4 \sin ^{2} \dfrac{\theta}{2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: Two unit vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ are inclined to each other at an angle $45^{\circ}$. The angle made by the resultant vector w.r.to $\vec{A}$ is?
explanation:
$\tan \alpha=\dfrac{B \sin \theta}{A+B \cos \theta}$
$\alpha$ - Angle made by the resultant vector with $\vec{A}$
$\begin{gathered}\theta=60^{\circ}, A=B=1 \Rightarrow \tan \alpha=\dfrac{2 \sqrt{2}}{1+\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\\alpha=\tan ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: If $A B \cos \theta=A B$, then the angle between $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ is
explanation:
As, $A B \cos \theta=A B$
$\cos \theta=1 \Rightarrow \theta=0^{\circ}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: What is the angle between $(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})$ and $\hat{i}$ ?
explanation:
Using, $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}=A B \cos \theta$
$\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{A B}$
or $\quad \cos \theta=\dfrac{(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \cdot \hat{i}}{\left(1^{2}+2^{2}+2^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{9}}=\dfrac{1}{3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: The resultant of two forces at right angle is 5 $N$. When the angle between them is $120^{\circ}$, the resultant is $\sqrt{13}$. Then, the force is
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: Given $\vec{R}=\vec{A}+\vec{B}$ and $R=A=B$. the angle between $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ is
explanation:
For the resultant,
$\begin{aligned}& R^{2}=R^{2}+R^{2}+2 R^{2} \cos \theta \\& \Rightarrow R^{2}=2 R^{2}+2 R^{2} \cos \theta \\& \dfrac{1}{2}=1+\cos \theta\end{aligned}$
or $\cos \theta=-\dfrac{1}{2}$ or $\theta=120^{\circ}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: If $\vec{A}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}$ and $\vec{B}=-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}$ then projection of $\vec{A}$ on $\vec{B}$ will be
explanation:
The projection of $\vec{A}$ on $\vec{B}=\dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{|\vec{B}|}$
$\begin{gathered}|\vec{B}|=\sqrt{(-1)^{2}+3^{2}+4^{2}}=\sqrt{1+9+16}=\sqrt{26} \\\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}=2(-1)+3 \times 3+(-1)(4)=3 \\\dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{|\vec{B}|}=\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{26}}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: If for two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$, sum $(\vec{A}+\vec{B})$ is perpendicular to the difference $(\vec{A}-\vec{B})$. The ratio of their magnitudes is
explanation:
$(\vec{A}+\vec{B})$ is perpendicular to $(\vec{A}-\vec{B})$. Thus $(\vec{A}+\vec{B}) \cdot(\vec{A}-\vec{B})=0$
or $A^{2}+\vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}-\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}-B^{2}=0$.
Because of commutative of dot product
$\begin{aligned}& \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}=\vec{B} \cdot \vec{A} \\& \therefore A^{2}-B^{2}=0 \text { or } A=B\end{aligned}$
Thus the ratio of magnitudes $A / B=1$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: The vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ are such that $|\vec{A}+\vec{B}|=|\vec{A}-\vec{B}|$ The angle between the two vectors is
explanation:
$|\vec{A}+\vec{B}|=|\vec{A}-\vec{B}|$
Squaring on both sides, $|\vec{A}+\vec{B}|^{2}=|\vec{A}-\vec{B}|^{2}$
$\Rightarrow(\vec{A}+\vec{B}) \cdot(\vec{A}+\vec{B})=(\vec{A}-\vec{B}) \cdot(\vec{A}-\vec{B})$$\Rightarrow \vec{A} \cdot \vec{A}+2 \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}+\vec{B} \cdot \vec{B}=\vec{A} \cdot \vec{A}+\vec{B} \cdot \vec{B}-2 \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}$$\Rightarrow 4 \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}=0 \Rightarrow 4 A B \cos \theta=0$$\Rightarrow \cos \theta=0 \Rightarrow \theta=90^{\circ}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: If $\hat{i}, \hat{j}$ and $\hat{k}$ represent unit vectors along the $x, y$ and $z$ - axes respectively, then the angle $\theta$ between the vectors $\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\hat{i}+\hat{j}$ is equal to
explanation:
Let the given vectors be
$\begin{gathered}\vec{A}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k} ; \vec{B}=\hat{i}+\hat{j} \\\therefore A=\sqrt{1^{2}+1^{2}+1^{2}}=\sqrt{3}, B=\sqrt{1^{2}+1^{2}}=\sqrt{2} \\\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}=2 \\\cos \theta=\dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{A B}=\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3} \sqrt{2}}=\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}}\right) \\\sin \theta=\sqrt{1-\cos ^{2} \theta}=\sqrt{1-\dfrac{2}{3}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{3}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\\theta=\sin ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: If a vector $2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}$ is perpendicular to the vector $4 \hat{j}-4 \hat{i}+\alpha \hat{k}$. Then the value of $\alpha$ is
explanation:
Let the given vectors be
$\vec{A}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k} \text { and } \vec{B}=-4 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+\alpha \hat{k}$
Dot product of these vectors should be equal to zero because they are perpendicular.
$\therefore \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}=-8+12+8 \alpha=0 \Rightarrow 8 \alpha=-4 \Rightarrow \alpha=-1 / 2$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: If $|\vec{A}-\vec{B}|=|\vec{A}|-|\vec{B}|$, the angle between $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ is
explanation:
$|\vec A - \vec B| = \sqrt {{A^2} + {B^2} - 2AB\cos \theta } $
Given that $\sqrt{A^{2}+B^{2}-2 A B \cos \theta}=A-B$
Squaring on both sides
$\begin{aligned}& A^{2}+B^{2}-2 A B \cos \theta=A^{2}+B^{2}-2 A B \\& \cos \theta=1 \Rightarrow \theta=0^{\circ}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: The resultant of two forces, each $P$, acting at an angle $\theta$ is
explanation:
As, $R^{2}=P^{2}+P^{2}+2 P^{2} \cos \theta$
$\Rightarrow R^{2}=2 P^{2}(1+\cos \theta)$
$R^{2}=4 P^{2} \cos ^{2} \dfrac{\theta}{2} \Rightarrow R=2 P \cos \dfrac{\theta}{2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: The angle between the two vectors $\vec{A}=5 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}$ and $\vec{B}=5 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}$ will be
explanation: $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}=0 \quad \therefore \theta=90^{\circ}$

explanation: As two vectors are same so the angle between them is zero.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: $\vec{A}=3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+7 \hat{k}$ and $\vec{B}=5 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+9 \hat{k}$. The direction cosine of the vector $\vec{A}+\vec{B}$ with $x$-axis is
explanation:
Let $\vec{C}=\vec{A}+\vec{B}=3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+7 \hat{k}+5 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+9 \hat{k}$
$\vec{C}=\vec{A}+\vec{B}=8 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+16 \hat{k}$
The direction cosine is $\cos \theta = \frac{{(8\hat i - 2\hat j + 16\hat k) \cdot \hat i}}{{\sqrt {{8^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(16)}^2}} }}$
$ = \frac{8}{{\sqrt {324} }}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: Two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ are such that $\vec{A}+\vec{B}=\vec{A}-\vec{B}$. Then
explanation:
$\vec A + \vec B = \vec A - \vec B \Rightarrow \vec B = - \vec B$
It is only possible when ${\vec B}$ is a null vector.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: If the sum of two unit vectors is a unit vector, then magnitude of difference is
explanation:
Let $\hat{n}_{1}$ and $\hat{n}_{2}$ are the two unit vectors,
then the sum is $\vec{n}_{s}=\hat{n}_{1}+\hat{n}_{2}$
or $n_{s}^{2}=n_{1}^{2}+n_{2}^{2}+2 n_{1} n_{2} \cos \theta$
$=1+1+2 \cos \theta$
Since it is given that $n_{s}$ is also a unit vector, therefore
$1=1+1+2 \cos \theta \Rightarrow \cos \theta=-\dfrac{1}{2} \therefore \theta=120^{\circ}$
Now the difference vector is $\hat{n}_{d}=\hat{n}_{1}-\hat{n}_{2}$ or
$\begin{aligned}& n_{d}^{2}=n_{1}^{2}+n_{2}^{2}-2 n_{1} n_{2} \cos \theta=1+1-2 \cos \left(120^{\circ}\right) \\& \quad \therefore n_{d}^{2}=2-2(-1 / 2)=2+1=3 \Rightarrow n_{d}=\sqrt{3}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Scalar Product of Vectors

question: A spring has a natural length $l$  with one end  fixed to the ceiling. The other end is fitted with a smooth ring which can slide over a horizontal  rod fixed at a distance $l$  below the ceiling. Initially, the spring makes an angle of $60^\circ $ with the vertical, when the system is released from rest. Find  the angle of the spring with the vertical, when the velocity of the ring reaches half of the maximum velocity, which the ring can attain during the motion.
explanation:
From diagram, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958007/preptracker/questions/q_108614_explanation_1764958006629.png $\cos 60^\circ = \frac{l}{h} \Rightarrow h = \frac{l}{{\cos 60^\circ }} \Rightarrow h = 2l$
Extension in spring $ = h - l = 2l - l = l$
At mean position,
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{1}{2}k{x^2} \Rightarrow {v^2} = \frac{k}{m}{l^2}$
If angle with vertical is when velocity is $v' = \frac{v}{2}$
Then,
$\frac{1}{2}mv{'^2} = \frac{1}{2}kx{'^2} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}m{\left( {\frac{v}{2}} \right)^2} = \frac{1}{2}k{\left( {\frac{l}{{\cos {\theta ^\prime }}} - l} \right)^2}$
As, $\cos \theta ' = \frac{l}{{h'}}\quad \Rightarrow \quad h' = \frac{l}{{\cos \theta '}}$
and extension, $X' = h' - l = \frac{l}{{\cos \theta '}} - l$
Substituting value of ${v^2},$ we have
$\frac{1}{2}m\left( {\frac{{k{l^2}}}{{4m}}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}k{\left( {\frac{1}{{\cos \theta '}} - l} \right)^2}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{l}{2} = \frac{l}{{\cos \theta '}} - l \Rightarrow \frac{{3l}}{2} = \frac{l}{{\cos \theta '}}$
$\cos \theta ' = \frac{2}{3}$
$\theta ' = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\frac{2}{3}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy of a Spring

question: A smooth chain of length $2 m$ is kept on a table such that its length of 60 $cm$ hangs freely from the edge of the table. The total mass of the chain is 4 $kg$ . The work done in pulling the entire chain on the table is, (Take $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$ )
explanation:
$\quad W = mgh = \left( {\frac{M}{L}l} \right)g\left( {\frac{l}{2}} \right)$
$ = \left( {\frac{4}{2} \times 0.6} \right) \times 10 \times \left( {\frac{{0.6}}{2}} \right) = 3.6\,J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy (Constant g)

question: A body thrown vertically upwards from the ground reaches a maximum height '$h$'. The ratio of the kinetic and potential energies of the body at a height $40 \%$ of $h$ from the ground is
explanation:
For vertically upward projection
At $y=40 \%$ of $h=0.4 h$,
${\text{PE}} = mgy = mg(0.4\,h) = 0.4mgh$
${\text{KE}} = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{1}{2}\,m\left( {{u^2} - 2\,g \times 0.4\,h} \right)$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m(2 g h-0.8 g h)=0.6 m g h$
$\therefore \dfrac{K E}{P E}=\dfrac{0.6 m g h}{0.4 m g h}=3: 2$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy (Constant g)

explanation: Statement A is false because in the real world, friction and air resistance will eventually dampen the oscillations, causing the system to come to rest. question: The potential energy of an object is $U(x)=\left(5 x^{2}-4 x^{3}\right) J$, where $x$ is the position in metre. The position at which the force becomes zero is
explanation:
Given, $U(x)=\left(5 x^{2}-4 x^{3}\right) J$
We know, $F=\dfrac{-d U(x)}{d x}$
$\Rightarrow F=\dfrac{-d}{d x}\left(5 x^{2}-4 x^{3}\right)=-10 x+12 x^{2}$
$\because \quad F=0 \quad$ (given)
$\therefore \quad 12 x^{2}-10 x=0 \Rightarrow 2 x(6 x-5)=0$
$\therefore \quad 2 x=0$ or $6 x-5=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=0$ or $x=\dfrac{5}{6} m$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy and Equilibrium

question: The potential energy of a particle in a central field has the form $U(r)=\dfrac{1}{r^{2}}-\dfrac{1}{r}$, where $r$ is the distance from the centre of the field. The magnitude of the maximum attractive force (in $N$) is
explanation:
Given, equation of potential energy,
$U(r)=\dfrac{1}{r^{2}}-\dfrac{1}{r}$
As we know that, conservative force,
$F=-\dfrac{d U}{d r}$
$\therefore \quad F=-\dfrac{d}{d r}\left(\dfrac{1}{r^{2}}-\dfrac{1}{r}\right)=-\dfrac{d}{d r}\left(r^{-2}-r^{-1}\right)$
$ = - \left( { - 2{r^{ - 3}} + {r^{ - 2}}} \right) = \left( {2{r^{ - 3}} - {r^{ - 2}}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Now, for $F_{\max }$ i.e. maximum value of force, $\dfrac{d F}{d r}=0$
$\therefore \dfrac{d}{d r}\left(2 r^{-3}-r^{-2}\right)=0 \Rightarrow-6 r^{-4}+2 r^{-3}=0$
$\Rightarrow 2 r^{-3}=6 r^{-4} \Rightarrow 1=3 r^{-1} \Rightarrow r=3$
Substituting in Eq. (1), we get
$F_{\text {max }}=\dfrac{2}{r^{3}}-\left.\dfrac{1}{r^{2}}\right|_{r=3}=\dfrac{2}{3^{3}}-\dfrac{1}{3^{2}}=\dfrac{2}{27}-\dfrac{1}{9}$
$=\dfrac{2}{27}-\dfrac{3}{27}=-\dfrac{1}{27}$ [Negative sign implies that the force is attractive]
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy and Equilibrium

question: A ball of mass $2\,kg$ is moving in $X Y$-plane with a potential energy given as $U=(12 x+16 y) J, x$ and $y$ being in metre. Assume the initial position of the ball at $t=0$ is at origin $(0,0)$ and it is moving with a velocity of $(15 \hat{i}+20 \hat{j}) {m} / {s}$ Then, identify the correct statement.
explanation:
Given, mass of the ball, $m=2 {~kg}$
and $U=(12 x+16 y) J$
$\Rightarrow \vec{F}=-\dfrac{d U}{d x} \hat{i}-\dfrac{d U}{d y} \hat{j}=-12 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}$
Initially (at $t=0$ ), the ball is at origin ( 0,0 ) and is moving with velocity
$\vec{v}_{i}=(15 \hat{i}+20 \hat{j}) {m} / {s}$
Now, $\vec{F}=m \vec{a}=m \dfrac{\vec{v}_{f}-\vec{v}_{i}}{t}$ (where $v_{f}$ is the velocity of the ball after ${t}=2 s$ )or $\quad \dfrac{\vec{F} t}{m}=\vec{v}_{f}-\vec{v}_{i}$
Substituting the given values in above equation, we get
$\therefore \quad(-12 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}) \times \dfrac{t}{m}=\vec{v}_{f}-\vec{v}_{i}$
$\Rightarrow(-12 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}) \times \dfrac{2}{2}=\vec{v}_{f}-(15 \hat{i}+20 \hat{j})$
$\Rightarrow \quad \vec{v}_{f}=15 \hat{i}-12 \hat{i}+20 \hat{j}-16 \hat{j}$
$=(3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}) {m} / {s}$
$\therefore \quad\left|v_{f}\right|=\sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}}=5 {~m} / {s}$
So, the correct option is (3).

options.text: The path of the ball is parabolic.
The direction of motion of the ball initially at $t=0$ is parallel to the direction of acceleration.
The speed of the ball at $t=2 s$ is $5 m / s$.
The magnitude of acceleration of the ball is $8 {~m} / {s}^{2}$.

question: A ball of mass $m=1 {~kg}$ is thrown from the top of a building with an initial velocity of $v=(20 {~m} / {s}) \hat{i}+(24 {~m} / {s}) \hat{j}$ at time $t=0$. The change in the potential energy of the ball between $t=0$ and $t=6 {~s}$, if the ball does not hit the ground, then (assume, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
Initial velocity, $\vec{u}=(20 \hat{i}+24 \hat{j}) {m} / {s}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957987/preptracker/questions/q_108494_explanation_1764957986660.png To find change in $P E$, we have to consider only vertical motion of the ball.
So, we have $u_{y}=24 {~m} / {s}, m=1 {~kg}$
$a_{y}=-10 {~m} / {s}^{2}, t=6 {~s}$
We take origin at the point of projection,
$h=u_{y} t-\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}=24 \times 6-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 36 {~m}$
$=144-180=-36 m$
So, vertical displacement of the particle is
$h=-36 m$ (below the point of projection).
Hence, change in $P E=m g h=1 \times 10 \times(-36) J$
$=-360 {~J}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy (Constant g)

question: Two blocks of equal masses are connected with a massless spring of spring constant $2500 {~N} / {m}^{2}$ and length 10 cm at rest on the frictionless horizontal plane. If a constant horizontal force $10\,N$ is applied as shown in the figure, find the maximum distance between the blocks.
explanation:
In the case of maximum elongation, whole system will be moving with a common acceleration $a$, so for the system https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952419/preptracker/questions/q_108487_explanation_1764952419516.png the centre of mass will also move with the same acceleration $a$.
Let us suppose that both the masses elongate the spring by a distance $x_{1}$ and $x_{2}$, respectively. So, on taking the frame of centre of mass there will act a pseudo force equal to $m a$ on the masses. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952420/preptracker/questions/q_108487_explanation_1764952420539.png On applying the work-energy theorem, taking the centre of mass as a reference point.
$W_{\text {all forces }}=\Delta K$
$(F-m a) x_{1}+(m a) x_{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} K\left(x_{1}+x_{2}\right)^{2}=0$
( $\because$ w.r.t. centre of mass $\Delta K=0$ )
$\Rightarrow\left(F-m \times \dfrac{F}{2 m}\right) x_{1}+\left(m \times \dfrac{F}{2 m}\right) x_{2}-$
$\dfrac{1}{2} K\left(x_{1}+x_{2}\right)^{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow\left(F-\dfrac{F}{2}\right) x_{1}+\left(\dfrac{F}{2}\right) x_{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} K\left(x_{1}+x_{2}\right)^{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{F}{2} \times x_{1}+\dfrac{F}{2} \times x_{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} K\left(x_{1}+x_{2}\right)^{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{F}{2}\left(x_{1}+x_{2}\right)=\dfrac{1}{2} K\left(x_{1}+x_{2}\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow F=K\left(x_{1}+x_{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow x_{1}+x_{2}=\dfrac{F}{K} \Rightarrow x_{\text {total }}=\dfrac{F}{K}$
Here, $F=10 {~N}, K=2500 {~N} / {m}^{2}$
So, $x_{\text {total }}=\dfrac{10}{2500} m=\dfrac{1}{250} m=\dfrac{100}{250} {~cm}$
$=0.4 {~cm}$
So, the maximum distance between the block will be $=$ natural length $+x_{\text {total }}$
$=10 {~cm}+0.4 {~cm}=10.4 {~cm}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Two Blocks Connected by a Spring

question: A block of mass $2\,kg$ is connected to an ideal spring and is placed on a smooth horizontal surface. The spring is pulled to move the block and at an instant, the speed of end $A$ of the spring and speed of the block were measured to be $6 {~m} / {s}$ and $3 {~m} / {s}$, respectively. At this moment the potential energy of stored in the spring is increasing at a rate $15 {~J} / {s}$. Find the acceleration of the block at this instant.
explanation:
Given, $m=2 {~kg}$
velocity of spring's end, $v_{1}=6 {~m} / {s}$
and velocity of block $v_{2}=3 {~m} / {s}$
The rate of change of potential energy,
$\dfrac{d U}{d t}=15 {~J} / {s}$
Velocity of the block relative to the free end of the spring is given by
$v_{r}=(6-3) {m} / {s}=3 {~m} / {s}$.
Let at time $t$, elongation produced in the spring be $x$.
$\therefore \quad v_{r}=\dfrac{d x}{d t}$.
Also at time $t$, potential energy stored in the spring is,
$U=\dfrac{1}{2} K x^{2}$
$\therefore \frac{{dU}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{2}K \cdot 2x \cdot \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = Kx \cdot {v_r}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
At time $t$, force acting on the block
$F = Kx\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From equations (1) and (2), acceleration of the block is,
$a = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{{Kx}}{m} = \frac{1}{{m{v_r}}} \cdot \frac{{dU}}{{dt}}$
$ = \frac{{15}}{{2 \times 3}}\,m/{s^2} = 2.5\,m/{s^2}$.
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy of a Spring

question: The potential energy function (in $J$ ) of a particle in a region of space is given as$U=\left(2 x^{2}+3 y^{3}+2 z\right)$. Here $x, y$ and $z$ are in meter. The magnitude of $x$-component of force (in $N$ ) acting on the particle at point $P(1,2,3) m$ is
explanation:
Relation between conservative force and potential energy is given by $F_{x}=\dfrac{-\partial U}{\partial x}$ or
$F_{x}=\dfrac{-\partial}{\partial x}\left(2 x^{2}+3 y^{3}+2 z\right)$
$F_{x}=-4 x$
Magnitude $x$-component of force, at given point, $P(1,2,3)$
$F_{x}=|-4(1)|=4 N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy and Equilibrium

question: If a rubber ball falls from a height $h$ and rebounds upto the height of $h / 2$. The percentage loss of total energy of the initial system as well as velocity of ball before it strikes the ground, respectively, are
explanation:
Velocity of ball before it strike the ground is given by
$v^{2}-0^{2}+2 g h \quad v=\sqrt{2 g h}$
After collision, it reaches to $\dfrac{h}{2}$
Initial energy, $E_{1}=m g h$
Final energy, $E_{2}=m g \dfrac{h}{2}$
Loss of initial energy is $\dfrac{E_{1}-E_{2}}{E_{1}} \times 100 \%$
$=\left(1-\dfrac{E_{2}}{E_{1}}\right) \times 100 \%$
$=\left(1-\dfrac{m g \dfrac{h}{2}}{m g h}\right) \times 100 \%$
$=\left(1-\dfrac{1}{2}\right) \times 100 \%$
$=50 \%$

question: A bead of mass $150\,g$ is constrained to move along a vertical smooth and fixed circular track of radius $3\,m$ as shown in figure is released at ${B}$. The spring is in the plane of the track has natural length of $1\,m$ and spring constant of ${3 / 16 {~N} / {m}}$. It starts from rest at ${B}$. What is the normal force  exerted by the track on the bead when it passes through ${A}$?
explanation:
Applying work energy theorem between ${A}$ and ${B}$
${m g h+\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}}$
${\dfrac{150 \times 10 \times 3}{1000}+\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{3}{16} \times 4^{2}=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{2}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955930/preptracker/questions/q_36020_explanation_1764955929979.png ${12=m v^{2}}$ or ${N=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}}$
${N=\dfrac{12}{3}=4 {~N}}$

question: A block of mass $1\,kg$ is released from top of a rough incline having ${\mu=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}$. The initial speed of block is ${2 {~m} / {s}}$. The incline plane is of unknown length and has a spring of constant ${k=1 {~N} / {m}}$ connected at base as in figure. Find  the maximum compression of spring.
explanation:
${m a=m g \sin 30^{\circ}-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} m g \cos 60^{\circ} \Rightarrow a=0}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951585/preptracker/questions/q_35996_explanation_1764951585307.png By energy conservation ${W_{m g}+W_{f r}+W_{\text {spring }}=\Delta K E}$
${\Rightarrow-\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}=0-\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}} v=2 {~m}}$

question: In the situation in figure, a block of mass $1\,kg$ is attached to a light spring of constant ${40 {~N} / {m}}$ whose other end is fixed to the roof of a building $50\,cm$ above the smooth horizontal surface. Initially, spring is in natural length and vertical. When a force ${F=20 \sqrt{3} {~N}}$ is applied on the block, the block starts to move. The speed at the instant it breaks off the surface below it is ${\sqrt{10 n} {~m} / {s}}$. The value of ${n}$ is
explanation:
If the block leaves the surface at point ${B}$, then
${\therefore \quad k x \cos \theta=m g \quad(\because N=0)}$
Or ${40 x \cos \theta=10}$
Or ${x \cos \theta=\dfrac{1}{4}}$
From the figure, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951611/preptracker/questions/q_35953_explanation_1764951611144.png ${\cos \theta=\dfrac{O A}{O B}=\dfrac{0.5}{0.5+x}}$ or ${\dfrac{1}{4 x}=\dfrac{0.5}{0.5+x}}$
Or ${0.5+x=2 x}$ or ${0.5=x}$
${\therefore \quad \cos \theta=\dfrac{1}{4 x}=\dfrac{1}{4 \times 0.5}=\dfrac{1}{2}}$
${\therefore \quad \theta=60^{\circ}}$
${\therefore \quad A B=O A \tan \theta=0.5 \times \sqrt{3}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}$
Applying work-energy theorem,
$W=\Delta K \text { or } W_{s}+W_{F}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}-0$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} - \frac{1}{2}k{(AB)^2} + F(AB) = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$
Or ${\kern 1pt} - \frac{1}{2} \times 40 \times \frac{3}{4} + 20\sqrt 3 \times \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1 \times {v^2}$
Or $ - 15 + 30 = \frac{{{v^2}}}{2}\,\,\,{\rm{or}}\,\,v = \sqrt {30} \,m/s$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} n = 3$
So correct option is (4).

question: The potential energy for a force field $\vec{F}$ is given by $U(x, y)=\cos (x+y)$. The force acting on a particle at position given by coordinates $(0, \pi / 4)$ is
explanation:

${\left. {{F_x} = \sin (x + y)} \right]_{(0,\pi /4)}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }},$

question: A particle which is constrained to move along the $x$-axis, is subjected to a force in the same direction which varies with the distance $x$ of the particle from the origin is  $F(x) =  - kx + a{x^3}.$ Here, $k$ and $a$ are positive constants. For $x \geq 0$ ,the functional from of the potential energy of $U(x)$ the particle is
explanation:
We know that,
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,F = \frac{{dU}}{{dx}}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,dU = - F \cdot dx$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,U = - \int_0^x {\left( { - kx + a{x^3}} \right)} dx$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,U = \frac{{k{x^2}}}{2} - \frac{{a{x^4}}}{4}$
$\therefore$ We get, $U=0$ at $x=0$
and $\quad x=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 k}{a}}$
Also we get,
$U=\text { negative for } x>\sqrt{\dfrac{2 k}{a}}$
From the given function, we can see that $F=0$ at $x=0$ i.e. slope of $U-x$ graph is zero at $x=0$.
Hence, option (4) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Total energy is negative for a bound system.

Reason: Potential energy of a bound system is negative and more than kinetic energy.
explanation: For a bound system, the total energy is indeed negative. This is because the potential energy of a bound system is negative and greater than the kinetic energy. This results in a negative total energy. So correct option is (1)

question:
Assertion: A man who falls from a height on a cement floor receives more injury than when he falls from the same height on a heap of sand.

Reason: The impulse applied by a cement floor is more than the impulse by sand floor.
explanation:
The impulse is equal to the product of the average force $(F)$ applied during the collision and the time interval $(\Delta t)$.
On a cement floor, which is hard, the man's fall gets stopped almost instantly, resulting in a very small time interval $(\Delta t)$. Conversely, when landing on a heap of sand, the man's momentum reduction to zero takes a considerably longer time. So correct option is (3)

question:

Reason: The potential energy of water at the top is converted into heat energy during falling.
explanation:
Water at the foot of a waterfall is indeed at a different temperature from that at the top. This is because the potential energy of water at the top is converted into heat energy during the fall, leading to a temperature increase.
So correct option is (1)

question: When a long spring is strectched by $2 \mathrm{~cm}$, potential energy stored in it is $U$. If the spring is stretched by $10 \mathrm{~cm}$, the potential energy stored in it will be:
explanation:
Potential energy stored $=U$
$U = \frac{1}{2}k{x^2}$
$U = \frac{1}{2}k \times {(2)^2}$
$k = \frac{{2U}}{4} = \frac{U}{2}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(}}1)$
After stretching by $10 \mathrm{~cm} . U^{\prime}=$ new potential energy to $U^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{\prime 2}$
using (1) $x^{\prime}=$ new distance $=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{U}{2} \times {(10)^2}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \left( {k = \frac{U}{2}} \right)$
$U' = 25\,U$

question: The figure shows a mass $m$ on a frictonless surface. It is connected to rigid wall by the mean of a mass-less spring of its constant $k$. Initially, the spring is at its natural position. If a force of constant magnitude starts acting on the block towards right, then the speed of the block when the deformation in spring is $x$, will be
explanation:
Free body diagram of block is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955836/preptracker/questions/q_35024_explanation_1764955835686.png Now, from the energy conservation,
$\begin{aligned}& W=\Delta E_{k} \\& \Rightarrow W_{F}+W_{s p}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \\& \Rightarrow F \cdot x-\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \\& \therefore v=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 F \cdot x-k x^{2}}{m}}\end{aligned}$

question: A load of mass $m$ falls from a height $h$ on the scale pan hung from a spring as shown in figure. If the spring constant is $k$ and mass of the scale pan is zero and the mass $m$ does not bounce relative to the pan, then the amplitude of vibration is
explanation:
Given, spring constant $=k$
From law of conservation of energy,
$m g h=\dfrac{1}{2} k x_{0}^{2}-m g x_{0}$
where, $x_{0}$ is maximum elongation in spring and $m g x_{0}$ is the potential energy due to elongation.
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} k x_{0}^{2}-m g x_{0}-m g h=0$$\Rightarrow x_{0}^{2}-\dfrac{2 m g}{k} x_{0}-\dfrac{2 m g}{k} h=0$
$x_{0}=\dfrac{\dfrac{2 m g}{k} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{2 m g}{k}\right)^{2}+4 \times \dfrac{2 m g}{k} h}}{2}$
Here, we take positive sign.
Amplitude $=$ elongation in spring for lowest extreme position - elongation in spring for equilibrium position
$\begin{array}{ll}\text { So, amplitude }=x_{0}-x_{1} & {\left[\because x_{1}=0\right]} \\=(m g / k) \sqrt{1+2 h k / m g} &\end{array}$

options.text: $m g / k \sqrt{1+2 h k / m g}$

question: The potential energy function \[U\left( x \right)\] is associated with a conservative force \[F\] and described by the graph given above. If a particle being acted upon by this force has a kinetic energy of \[1.0\,J\] at position \[{x_0}\]. What is the particle’s kinetic energy at position \[{x_4}\]?
explanation:
According to law of conservation of energy,
$U_{i}+\left(E_{k}\right)_{i}=U_{f}+\left(E_{k}\right)_{f}$
where, $U_{i},\left(E_{k}\right)_{i}$ are initial potential energy and kinetic energy of a particle at $x_{0}$ and $U_{f},\left(E_{k}\right)_{f}$ are final $P E$ and $K E$ of particle at $x_{4}$.
So, $4.0 + 1.0 = - 2.0 + {\left( {{E_k}} \right)_f} \Rightarrow {\left( {{E_k}} \right)_f} = 7.0\,J$

question:
Assertion: A spring of force constant $k$ is cut into two pieces having lengths in the ratio ${\rm{1:2}}$. The force constant of series combination of the two parts is $3 k / 2$.

Reason: The spring connected in series are represented by $k=k_{1}+k_{2} \quad$
explanation:
As we know, $k=\dfrac{F}{l} \Rightarrow k \propto \dfrac{1}{l}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\dfrac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
$k_{1}=2 k, k_{2}=k$
In series, $\dfrac{1}{k^{\prime}}=\dfrac{1}{k_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{k_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{2 k}+\dfrac{1}{k}=\dfrac{3}{2 k}$
$k' = \frac{{2k}}{3}$
So, both Assertion and Reason are incorrect.
So Correct option is (4)

$\frac{{{U_2}}}{{{U_1}}} = {\left( {\frac{8}{2}} \right)^2}$
${U_2} = 16U.$
Correct option is (3).

question: Potential energy as a function of $r$ is given by $U=\dfrac{A}{r^{10}}-\dfrac{B}{r^{5}}$, where $r$ is the interatomic distance, $A$ and $B$ are positive constants. The equilibrium distance between the two atoms will be:
explanation:
For equilibrium $r=r_{0}$.
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{d U}{d r}=0 \\& \dfrac{-10 A}{r_{0}^{11}}+\dfrac{5 B}{r_{0}^{6}}=0 \\& r_{0}^{5}=\dfrac{10 A}{5 B}=\dfrac{2 A}{B} \\& \Rightarrow r_{0}=\left(\dfrac{2 A}{B}\right)^{1 / 5}\end{aligned}$

question: A block of mass $m$, lying on a smooth horizontal surface, is attached to a spring (of negligible mass) of spring constant $k$. The other end of the spring is fixed, as shown in the figure. The block is initially at rest in its equilibrium position. If now the block is pulled with a constant force $F$, the maximum speed of the block is
explanation:
Extension in spring, $x = \frac{F}{k}$ (At new equilibrium)
Using work energy theorem
${W_F} + {W_{spring{\text{ }}}} = \Delta KE$
Let $v$ be the velocity of the block at new equilibrium.
$Fx + \left( { - \frac{1}{2}k{x^2}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}\,m{v^2} - 0\,{\mkern 1mu} F\left( {\frac{F}{k}} \right) - \frac{1}{2}k{\left( {\frac{F}{k}} \right)^2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\,m{v^2}v = \frac{F}{{\sqrt {mk} }}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy of a Spring

question: A particle is moving in a circle of radius $r$ under the action of a force $F = \alpha {r^2}$ which is directed towards centre of the circle. Total mechanical energy (kinetic energy + potential energy) of the particle is (take potential energy $=0$ for $r = 0$ :
explanation:
As we know, $\mathrm{dU}=$ F.dr
${\text{U}} = \int_0^{\text{r}} \alpha {{\text{r}}^2}{\text{dr}} = \frac{{a{r^3}}}{3}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
As, $\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}=\alpha r^{2}$
$K.E = \frac{1}{2}{m^2}{v^2} = \frac{1}{2}\alpha {r^3}\,\,\,(2)$
Total energy=Potential energy + kinetic energy
Now, from eqn (1) and (2)
Total energy $=\mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}+$ P.E.
$=\dfrac{\alpha r^{3}}{3}+\dfrac{\alpha r^{3}}{2}=\dfrac{5}{6} \alpha r^{3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Central Force

question: A spring of unstretched length $\ell$ has a mass $m$ with one end fixed to a rigid support. Assuming spring to be made of a uniform wire, the kinetic energy possessed by it if its free end is pulled with uniform velocity $v$ is
explanation:
Consider a small element of length $d x$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951365/preptracker/questions/q_34835_explanation_1764951364553.png $dK.E = \frac{1}{2}dm\,{\rm{v}}_P^2\quad \left( {{{\rm{v}}_p} = \frac{{\rm{v}}}{l}x} \right)$
$KE = {\int {\frac{1}{2}dm\left( {\frac{v}{\ell }x} \right)} ^2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\int {\frac{m}{\ell }} dx\frac{{{v^2}}}{{{\ell ^2}}}{x^2}$
$ = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{{2{l^3}}} \times \left[ {\frac{{{x^3}}}{3}} \right]_0^l$
$ = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{{2{l^3}}} \times \frac{{{l^3}}}{3} = \frac{1}{6}m{v^2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy of a Spring

question: A particle of mass $m$ is fixed to one end of a light spring of force constant $k$ and unstretched length $l$. The system is rotated about the other end of the spring with an angular velocity $\omega$, in gravity free space. The increase in length of the spring will be
explanation:
Let the extension produced in the spring due to rotation be $x$.
Then, for the body to be in equilibrium
$\begin{aligned}& F_{\text {spring }}=F_{\text {centrifugal }} \\& \Rightarrow k x=m(l+x) \omega^{2} \\& \Rightarrow x=\dfrac{m l \omega^{2}}{k-m \omega^{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy of a Spring

question: The potential energy $U$ in joule of a particle of mass 1 $kg$ moving in $x-y$ plane obeys the law $U=3 x+4 y$, where $(x, y)$ are the co-ordinates of the particle in metre. If the particle is at rest at $(6,4)$ at time $t=0$ then identify the wrong statement:
explanation:
$\mathrm{U}=3 x+4 y$
$\begin{gathered}F_{\mathrm{x}}=-\dfrac{\partial \mathrm{U}}{\partial x}=-3 \\F_{\mathrm{y}}=-\dfrac{\partial \mathrm{U}}{\partial \mathrm{y}}=-4 \\\Rightarrow \vec{F}=-(3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j})\end{gathered}$
If the particle crosses $\mathrm{y}$-axis then $\mathrm{x}$ displacement will be equal to -6
$\begin{aligned}& x=v_{x} t+\dfrac{1}{2} a_{x} t^{2} \\& -6=0-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 3 t^{2} \Rightarrow t=2 \sec\end{aligned}$
The resultant velocity is
$\vec v = 0 - (3\hat i + 4\hat j) \times 2 \Rightarrow |\vec v| = 10\,m/s$
In one second the displacement of the particle along $x$ and $y$ are
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta x=0-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 1^{2}=-1.5 \\& \Delta y=0-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 1^{2}=-2\end{aligned}$
Co-ordinate $=(6-1.5,4-2)=(4.5,2)$

options.text: The speed of the particle when it crosses $y$ - axis is 10 $m/s$
Co-ordinates of particle at $t=1$ sec is (4,5,2)

subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy and Equilibrium

question: Two blocks of mass 3 $kg$ and 6 $kg$ respectively are placed on a smooth horizontal surface. They are connected by a light spring of force constant $k = 200\,N/m$. Initially the spring is unstretched. The indicated velocities are imparted to the blocks. The maximum extension of the spring will be :-
explanation:
We know that
$K.{E_{sys}} = K.{E_{of\,C.M}} + K.{E_{w.r.to\,C.M.}}$
$K.{E_{sys}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times {1^2} + \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times {2^2} = 13.5J$
${V_{CM}} = \frac{{6 \times 2 - 3 \times 1}}{9} = 1\,m/s$
$K.{E_{of\,CM}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times {1^2} = 4.5J$
$ \Rightarrow K.{E_{w.r.to\,C.M}} = (13.5 - 4.5)J = 9J$
In the absence of external force on a system
$K . E_{o f C M}$ - always remains constant
So $K . E_{w . r . t o c M}$ will be converted into $P.E$
$K.{E_{w.r.to\,C.M}} = 9J = \frac{1}{2}Kx_{\max }^2$
${x_{\max }} = 0.3m = 30cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy of a Spring

question: A block of mass $m$ sliding down an incline at constant speed is initially at a height $h$ above the ground, as shown in the figure. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the mass and the incline is $\mu$. If the mass continues to slide down the incline at a constant speed, how much energy is dissipated by friction by the time the mass reaches the bottom of the incline?
explanation:
Taking block as system the work energy theorem is
$W_{f}=\Delta K+\Delta U=0-m g h$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy (Constant g)

question:
The potential energy at a point, relative to the reference point is always defined as the negative of work done by the force as the object moves from the reference point to the point considered. The value of potential energy at the reference point itself can be set equal to zero because we are always concerned only with differences of potential energy between two points and the associated change of kinetic energy. A particle $A$ is fixed at origin of a fixed coordinate system. A particle $B$ which is free to move experiences a force $\vec{F}=\left(-\dfrac{2 \alpha}{r^{3}}+\dfrac{\beta}{r^{2}}\right) \hat{r}$ due to particle $A$ where $\hat{r}$ is the position vector of particle $B$ relative to $A$. It is given that the force is conservative in nature and potential energy at infinity is zero. If $B$ has to be removed from the influence of $A$, energy has to be supplied for such a process. The ionisation energy $E_{o}$ is at work that has to be done by an external agent to move the particle from a distance $r_{o}$ to infinity slowly. Here ${r_o}$ is the equilibrium position of the particle.
What is potential energy function of particle as function of $r$?
explanation:
$F = \frac{{ - 2\alpha }}{{{r^3}}} + \frac{\beta }{{{r^2}}} = \frac{{ - dU}}{{dr}}$
$dU = \frac{{2\alpha }}{{{r^3}}}dr - \frac{\beta }{{{r^2}}}dr$
$\int\limits_0^U {dU} = 2\alpha \int\limits_\infty ^r {\frac{1}{{{r^3}}}dr} - \beta \int\limits_\infty ^r {\frac{1}{{{r^2}}}dr} $
$U = \frac{{ - \alpha }}{{{r^2}}} + \frac{\beta }{r}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy and Equilibrium

question: A particle free to move along $x$-axis has potential energy given by $U(x)=K\left[1-\exp (-x)^{2}\right]$ for $-\infty \leq x \leq \infty$, where $\mathrm{K}$ is a positive constant of appropriate dimensions. Then:
explanation:
The graph between $\mathrm{U} \& x$ is
$\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{K}\left[1-\mathrm{e}^{-x^{2}}\right]=\mathrm{K}\left[1-\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{e}^{x^{2}}}\right]$
A t $x=0 \quad \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{K}[1-1]=0$
As $x \rightarrow \infty \quad \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{K}\left[1-\mathrm{e}^{-\infty}\right]=\mathrm{K}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955782/preptracker/questions/q_34705_explanation_1764955782511.png It is an exponentially increasing graph of potential energy $U$. From the graph it is clear that at origin. Potential energy $U$ is minimum (therefore, kinetic energy will be maximum) and force acting on the particle is also zero because $F=\dfrac{-d U}{d x}=($ slope of $U-x$ graph $)=0$
Therefore, origin is the stable equilibrium position. Hence, particle will oscillate simple harmonically about $x=0$ for small displacements. Therefore, option $\mathrm{d}$ is correct.
(1), (2) and (3) options are wrong due to the following reasons:
From the graph we can see that slope is zero at $x=0$ and $x= \pm \infty$.
Now among these equilibriums stable equilibrium position is that where $U$ is minimum (Here $x=0$ ).
Unstable equilibrium position is that where $U$ is maximum (Here none).
Neutral equilibrium position is that where $U$ is constant (here $x= \pm \infty$ ).
Therefore, option (1) is wrong.
For any infinite non-zero value of $x$, force is directed towards the origin because origin is in stable equilibrium position. Therefore, option (2) is incorrect.
At the origin, potential energy is minimum, hence kinetic energy will be maximum. Therefore, option (3) is also wrong.

options.text: At point away from the origin, the particle is in unstable equilibrium
For any finite non-zero value of $x$, there is force directed away from the origin
If its total mechanical energy is $K / 2$, it has its minimum $\mathrm{KE}$ at the origin
For small displacement from $x=0$, the motion is simple harmonic

subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy and Equilibrium

question: The potential energy of a particle moving along $x$-axis is given $U=20+5 \sin (4 \pi x)$, where $U$ is in $J$ and $x$ is in metre under the action of conservative force:
explanation:
$\mathrm{U}=20+5 \sin (4 \pi x)$
$\mathrm{F}=\dfrac{-\mathrm{dU}}{\mathrm{d} x}=-20 \pi \cos (4 \pi x)$
At equilibrium $\mathrm{F}=0$
$4 \pi x=\dfrac{\pi}{2}, \dfrac{3 \pi}{2}, \dfrac{5 \pi}{2} \ldots$

options.text: If total mechanical energy is 20 $J$, then at $x = \frac{7}{8}\,m$, particle is at equilibrium
If total mechanical energy is 20 $J$, then at $x = \frac{1}{2}\,m$, particle is not at equilibrium
If total mechanical energy is 20 $J$, then at $x = \frac{1}{3}\,m$, particle is at equilibrium
If total mechanical energy is 20 $J$, then at $x = \frac{3}{8}\,m$, particle is not at equilibrium

question: The potential energy of a particle in a force field is $U=\dfrac{A}{r^{2}}-\dfrac{B}{r}$, where $A$ and $B$ are positive constants and $r$ is the distance of particle from the centre of the field. For stable equilibrium, the distance of the particle is
explanation:
Given, the potential energy of a particle in a force field, $U=\dfrac{A}{r^{2}}-\dfrac{B}{r^{1}}$
For stable equilibrium, $F=-\dfrac{d U}{d r}=0$
$=\dfrac{d U}{d r}=-2 A r^{-3}+B r^{-2}$
$0=-\dfrac{2 A}{r^{3}}+\dfrac{B}{r^{2}} \quad\left(\text { As } \dfrac{-d U}{d r}=0\right)$
The distance of particle from the centre of the field
$r=\dfrac{2 A}{B}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy and Equilibrium

question: The rectangular blocks $A$ and $B$ of masses 2 $kg$ and 2 $kg$ respectively are connected by a spring of spring constant $10.8\,N\,{m^{ - 1}}$ and are placed on a frictionless horizontal surface. The block $A$ was given an initial velocity of $0.15\,m\,{s^{ - 1}}$ in the direction shown in the figure. The maximum compression of the spring during the motion is
explanation:
Block $A$ moving with velocity 0.15 $m/s$ compresses the spring which pushes $B$ towards right. Spring copression continues till $A$ and $B$ move with same common velocity. Let this velocity be $v$. This happens when the spring is in a state of maximum compression. Let $x$ be the maximum compression.
According to the law of conservation of linear momentum, we get
${m_A}u = \left( {{m_A} + {m_B}} \right)v$
or $v = \frac{{{m_A}u}}{{{m_A} + {m_B}}} = \frac{{2 \times 0.15}}{{2 + 3}} = 0.06\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
According to the conservation of energy, we get
$\dfrac{1}{2} m_{A} u^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(m_{A}+m_{B}\right) v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}$
$\dfrac{1}{2} m_{A} u^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2}\left(m_{A}+m_{B}\right) v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}$
$\dfrac{1}{2} \times 2 \times(0.15)^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2}(2+3)(0.06)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}$
$0.0225-0.009=\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}$ or $0.0135=\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}$
or $x = \sqrt {\frac{{0.027}}{k}} = \sqrt {\frac{{0.027}}{{10.8}}} = 0.05\,m$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Two Blocks Connected by a Spring

question: A long spring is strecthed by 2 $cm$. Its potential energy is $U$. If the spring is strecthed by 10 $cm$, its potential energy would be
explanation:
$U = \frac{1}{2}K{x^2}\,or{\rm{ }}U \propto {x^2}$
Strecth is increased to 5 times. Therefore stored potential energy will be increased by 25 times.

question: A body of mass 0.1 $kg$ moving with a velocity of 10 $m/s$ hits a spring ( fixed at the other end) of force constant 1000 $N/m$ and comes to rest after compressing the spring. The compression of the string is
explanation:
Decrese in kinetic energy = increase in elastic potential energy
$\therefore \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{1}{2}K{x^2}$
or $x = \sqrt {\frac{m}{K}} \cdot v$
$=\sqrt{\dfrac{0.1}{1000}} \times 10$
$ = 0.1\,m$

question:

Reason: In compressing or stretching, work is done on the spring against the restoring force.
explanation: The work done on the spring against the restoring force is stored as potential energy in both conditions when it is compressed or stretched. So option (1) is correct.

question: A particle is moving in a circular path of radius $a$ under the action of an attractive potential $U=-\dfrac{k}{2 r^{2}}$. Its total energy is :
explanation:
$F=-\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial r}=\dfrac{K}{r^{3}}$
Since it is performing circular motion
$\begin{aligned}& F=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}=\dfrac{K}{r^{3}} \\& m v^{2}=\dfrac{K}{r^{2}} \\& \Rightarrow K \cdot E=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{K}{2 r^{2}}\end{aligned}$
Total energy $=P . E .+K \cdot E$
$=-\dfrac{K}{2 r^{2}}+\dfrac{K}{2 r^{2}}=0$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy and Equilibrium

question: The potential energy of a conservative system is given by $\mathrm{U}(x)=\left(x^{2}-3 x\right)$ joule. Then its equilibrium position is
explanation:
we know that
$F=\dfrac{-d U}{d x}=-\dfrac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}-3 x\right)$
$F=-(2 x-3)$
For equilibrium $\mathrm{F}=0$
$0=2 x-3 \quad \Rightarrow x=3 / 2=1.5$
$\dfrac{d U}{d x^{2}}=\dfrac{d}{d x}(2 x-3)=2$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy and Equilibrium

question: A rectangular plank of mass $m_{1}$ and height $a$ is kept on a horizontal surface. Another rectangular plank of mass $m_{2}$ and height $b$ is placed over the first plank. The gravitational potential energy of the system is
explanation:
Height of $CG$ of mass $m_{1}=\dfrac{a}{2}$
Height of $CG$ of mass $m_{2}=a+\dfrac{b}{2}$
$\therefore$ Gravitational potential energy of system
$\begin{aligned}& =\left[\dfrac{m_{1}}{2}+m_{2}\right] g a+m_{2} g \dfrac{b}{2} \\& =\left[\left(\dfrac{m_{1}}{2}+m_{2}\right) a+m_{2} \dfrac{b}{2}\right] g\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy (Constant g)

question: A string of length $L$ and force constant $K$ is stretched to obtain extension $l$. It is further stretched to obtain extension $l_{1}$. The work done in second stretching is
explanation:
Work done in stretching a string to obtain an extension $l$ is
$W_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2} K l$
Similarly, work done in stretching a string to obtain extension $l_{1}$ is
$W_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} K l_{1}^{2}$
$\therefore$ Work done in second case
$=W_{2}-W_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2} K\left(l_{1}^{2}-l^{2}\right)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy of a Spring

question: The potential energy function for the force between two atoms in a diatomic molecule is approximately given by $U(x)=\dfrac{a}{x^{12}}-\dfrac{b}{x^{6}}$, where $a$ and $b$ are constants and $x$ is the distance between the atoms. The dissociation energy of the molecule is  $D = \left[ {U(x = \infty ) - {U_{{\rm{at}}\,\,{\rm{equilibrium }}}}} \right],D$ is
explanation:
At equilibrium $\frac{{dU\left( x \right)}}{{dx}} = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{ - 12a}}{{{x^{11}}}} = \frac{{ - 6b}}{{{x^5}}} \Rightarrow x = {\left( {\frac{{2a}}{b}} \right)^{\frac{1}{6}}}$
$\therefore {U_{at\,equilibrium}} = \frac{a}{{{{\left( {\frac{{2a}}{b}} \right)}^2}}} - \frac{b}{{\left( {\frac{{2a}}{b}} \right)}} = - \frac{{{b^2}}}{{4a}}$
${U_{\left( {x = \infty } \right)}} = 0$
$\therefore \,D = 0 - \left( { - \frac{{{b^2}}}{{4a}}} \right) = \frac{{{b^2}}}{{4a}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy and Equilibrium

question: The potential energy of a 1 $kg$  particle free to move along the $x$-axis is given by $V(x)=\left(\dfrac{x^{4}}{4}-\dfrac{x^{2}}{2}\right) J$. The total mechanical energy of the particle is 2$J$. Then, the maximum speed (in $m/s$) is
explanation:
Potential energy $V=\dfrac{x^{4}}{4}-\dfrac{x^{2}}{2}$
For maximum kinetic energy, potential energy of a particle should be minimum.
For minimum value of $V$, $\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = 0\,and\,\frac{{{d^2}V}}{{d{x^2}}} > 0$
Force $F = - \left( {\frac{{dV}}{{dx}}} \right) = \frac{{4{x^3}}}{4} - \frac{{2x}}{2} = 0 \Rightarrow {x^3} - x = 0$
$\Rightarrow x\left(x^{2}-1\right)=0$
i.e., at $x=0, x=+1$, and $x=-1$ force on the particle will be zero.
Now, $\dfrac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}=3 x^{2}-1$
For $x=+1$, and $x=-1 \dfrac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}>1$
It means the potential energy of the particle will be minimum at $x=1, x=-1$.
Now substituting these values in expression of potential energy
$V_{\min }=\left[\dfrac{(1)^{4}}{4}-\dfrac{(1)^{2}}{2}\right] J=-\dfrac{1}{4} J$
$(\text { Kinetic energy })_{\max }=$ Total energy
$-(\text { Potential energy })_{\min }=2-\left(-\dfrac{1}{4}\right)$
$\frac{1}{2}m\upsilon _{\max }^2 = \frac{9}{4} \Rightarrow \upsilon _{\max }^2 = \frac{9}{2} \Rightarrow {\upsilon _{\max }} = \frac{3}{{\sqrt 2 }}m/\sec .$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy and Equilibrium

question: An elastic string of unstretched length $L$ and force constant $k$ is stretched by another small $x$. It is further stretched by another small length $y$. The work done in the second stretching is
explanation:
$W=\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} k(x+y)^{2}$ $W=\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} k\left(x^{2}+y^{2}+2 x y\right)$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} k y(-2 x-y)$
The work done against elastic force is
$W_{e x t}=-W=\dfrac{k y}{2}(2 x+y)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy of a Spring

question: The block of mass $M$ moving on the frictionless horizontal surface collides with the spring of spring constant $k$ and compresses it by length $L$. The maximum momentum of the block after collision is
explanation:
Form conservation of energy
$\begin{aligned}\dfrac{1}{2} M v^{2} & =\dfrac{1}{2} k L^{2} \\\Rightarrow \quad v & =\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{M}} \cdot L\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned}\text { Momentum } & =M \times v=M \times \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{M}} \cdot L \\& =\sqrt{k M} \cdot L\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy of a Spring

question: A particle is released from rest at origin. It moves under influence of potential field $U=x^{2}-3 x$, kinetic energy at $x=2$ is:
explanation:
As the total energy is constant
$T.{E_i} = T.{E_f}$
${K_i} + {U_i} = {K_f} + {U_f}$
${K_i} = \frac{1}{2}m\left( {{0^2}} \right) = 0$
${U_i} = 0$ (At origin)
${U_f} = {\left( 2 \right)^2} - 3\left( 2 \right) = - 2J$
$ \Rightarrow {K_f} = 2J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy and Equilibrium

question: The potential energy of a particle of mass 5 $kg$ moving in the $x y-$ plane is given by $U=(-7 x+24 y) J, x$ and $y$ being in metre. If the particle starts from rest and from origin, then speed of particle at $t = 2\,s$ is
explanation:
$F=-\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial x} \hat{i}-\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial y} \hat{j}=7 \hat{i}-24 \hat{j}$
$\therefore \quad a_{x}=\dfrac{F_{x}}{m}=\dfrac{7}{5}=1.4 m s^{-2}$ along positive $x$-axis
$a_{y}=\dfrac{F_{Y}}{m}=-\dfrac{24}{5}$
$=-4.8 m s^{-2}$ along negative $y$-axis
$\therefore \quad {v_X} = {a_x}t = 1.4 \times 2 = 2.8\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
and ${v_Y} = 4.8 \times 2 = 9.6\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore \quad v=\sqrt{v_{X}^{2}+v_{Y}^{2}}=10 m s^{-1}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Potential Energy and Equilibrium

question: An ice cube has been given a push and slides without friction on a level table. Which is correct?
explanation: The ice cube is in neutral equilibrium. Since it has zero acceleration

question: A mass of 0.5 $kg$ moving with a speed of 15 $m/s$ on a horizontal smooth surface, collides with a nearly weightless spring of force constant $k = 50\,N/m$. The maximum compression of the spring would be:
explanation:

$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow m v^{2}=k x^{2} \Rightarrow 0.5 \times(1.5)^{2}=50 \times x^{2} \\& \therefore x=0.15 m\end{aligned}$

$\therefore \dfrac{U_{1}}{U_{2}}=\dfrac{3000}{1500}=\dfrac{2}{1} \text {. }$

question: A particle of mass $m$ is moving in a horizontal circle of radius $r$, under a centripetal force equal to $-\left(k / r^{2}\right)$ where $k$ is constant. The total energy of the particle is
explanation:
Since the particle is moving in horizontal circle, centripetal force,
$F = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} = \frac{k}{{{r^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$m{v^2} = \frac{k}{r}$
Kinetic energy of the particle,
$K=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{k}{2 r}(\operatorname{Using}(\mathrm{1}))$
As $F=\dfrac{-d U}{d r}$
$\therefore$ Potential energy,
$U=-\int_{\infty}^{r} F d r=-\int_{\infty}^{r}\left(\dfrac{-k}{r^{2}}\right) d r=K \int_{\infty}^{r} r^{-2} d r=\dfrac{-k}{r}$
$\therefore$ Total energy $=K+U=\dfrac{k}{2 r}-\dfrac{k}{r}=\dfrac{-k}{2 r}$.
