question: A vertical spring with force constant $K$ is fixed on a table. A ball of mass $m$ at a height $h$ above the free upper end of the spring falls vertically on the spring, so that the spring is compressed by a distance $d$. The net work done by the spring in the process is
explanation:

When mass $m$ falls vertically on spring, the spring is compressed by distance $\mathrm{d}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950036/preptracker/questions/q_33254_explanation_1764950036311.png From work energy theorem $W_{g}+W_{s p r}=\Delta K E$
The body velocity is zero both at initial and final stages.
As $\triangle K E=0$
$\begin{gathered}W_{g}=m g(h+d) \\\Rightarrow W_{s p r}=-m g(h+d)\end{gathered}$

question: A block of mass '$m$' with an initial kinetic energy '$E$' moves up an inclined plane of inclination '$\theta$'. If '$\mu$' is the coefficient of friction between the plane and the body, the work done against friction before coming to rest is
explanation:
Let before coming to rest body slides up to distance $s . h=s \sin \theta$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958002/preptracker/questions/q_108607_explanation_1764958002327.png From the work- energy theorem,
$W_{\text {net }}=\Delta W_{f}+\Delta W_{m g}=\Delta K$
$\Rightarrow-\mu m g(\cos \theta) s-m g s \sin \theta=0-E$
$\Rightarrow \mu m g s \cos \theta+m g s \sin \theta=E$
$\Rightarrow s=\dfrac{E}{m g(\mu \cos \theta+\sin \theta)}$
$\therefore$ Work one by friction,
$\Delta W_{f}=-\mu m g s \cos \theta=\dfrac{\mu E \cos \theta}{\mu \cos \theta+\sin \theta}$
$=\dfrac{\mu E \cos \theta}{\sin \theta+\mu \cos \theta}$

B. The change in kinetic energy is the product of mass and velocity, as well as the rate of change of velocity, which is a result derived from the time rate of change of kinetic energy. (B-R)
C. Resistive Force of air brings the pendulum to rest. (C- Q)
D. Newton's Second Law relates force and acceleration at any instant, which is a fundamental concept when discussing variable forces and their effects on objects. (D-P)
Correct option is (3).

question: A body of mass $3\,kg$ is moving under the action of a force which causes a displacement of $\left(\dfrac{t^{3}}{3}\right) m$, where $t$ is the time in seconds. The work done by the force in first 2 sec is
explanation:
Given, mass $m=3 {~kg}$, displacement
$(s)=\dfrac{t^{3}}{3} m$
Velocity, $v=\dfrac{d s}{d t}=\dfrac{3 t^{2}}{3} \Rightarrow t^{2} {~m} / {s}$
Acceleration, $(a)=\dfrac{d v}{d t}=2 t m / s^{2}$
Now, work done by the force
$W=\int_{0}^{2} F \cdot d s=\int_{0}^{2} m a \cdot d s$
$=\int_{0}^{2} 3 \times 2 t \times t^{2} d t=\int_{0}^{2} 6 t^{3} d t$
$ = \frac{3}{2}\left[ {{t^4}} \right]_0^2 = 24\,J$
So, the correct option is (4).

question:
${\rm{Statement }}\,{\rm{I}}$ The slope of kinetic energy displacement curve of a body in motion will be directly proportional to its acceleration.
${\rm{Statement }}\,{\rm{II}}$ From a height of $15 m$, a ball is projected vertically upwards with a velocity of $30 {~m} / {s}$. If the ball rises to the same height after hitting the ground, the loss of its energy on hitting the ground is $30 \%$.
${\rm{Statement }}\,{\rm{III}}$ The velocity acquired by a body of mass $m$ after travelling a fixed distance from rest under the action of a constant force is directly proportional to mass $m$. Which of the following is correct?
explanation:
Kinetic energy, $K=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
$\therefore$ Slope $=\dfrac{d K}{d x}=m \cdot v \dfrac{d v}{d x}=m a$
$\therefore$ Slope $\alpha$ a. So statement I is correct
Loss of energy, $\Delta E=E_{i}-E_{f}$
Now $\Delta E=\left(\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+m g h\right)-m g h=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
$\therefore \frac{{\Delta E}}{{{E_i}}} = \frac{{{v^2}}}{{{v^2} + 2gh}}$
$ = \frac{{{{30}^2}}}{{{{30}^2} + 2 \times 10 \times 15}} = 0.75 = 75\% $
So statement II is incorrect.
Also, for a body starting from rest, $K E=$ Work done
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=F x=m \cdot a \cdot x$
$v=\sqrt{2 a x}$ which is mass independent.
$\therefore$ Statement III is incorrect.
So, the correct option is (3).

options.text: Statements I, II and III are true.
Statements I, III are true but statement II is false.
Statement I is true but statements II and III and false.
Statements I, II are true but statement III is false.

question: If the average terminal velocity of rain drop is$2 m s^{-1}$, then the energy transferred by rain to each square metre of the surface at a place which receives $100\,cm$ of rain in a year is
explanation:
Given that, $v_{t}=2 {~m} / {s}, h=100 {~cm}=1 {~m}, A=1 {~m}^{2}$
Now, $V=A h=1 {~m}^{3}$
$\rho=10^{3} {~kg} {~m}^{-3}=$ density of water
$m=V \rho=10^{3} {~kg}=$ mass of water collected in one year by $1 {~m}^{2}$ area
Now, energy transferred by rain per unit area
$=\dfrac{\text { Kinetic energy }}{\text { Area }}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}}{1}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10^{3} \times(2)^{2}=2 \times 10^{3} {~J} / {m}^{2}$
Hence, energy transferred by rain to each square metre is $2 \times 10^{3} {~J}$.
So, the correct option is (3).

question: A particle of mass $m$ is moving along a circle of radius $R$ such that its tangential acceleration $a_{t}$ varies with distance covered $x$ as $a_{t}=\alpha x^{2}$ where $\alpha$ is a constant. The kinetic energy, $K$ of the particle varies with the distance as $K=\beta x^{c}$, where $\beta$ and $c$ are constants. The values of $\beta$ and $c$ are
explanation:
Given, $m_{\text {particle }}=m, a_{t}=\alpha x^{2}$ and kinetic energy, $K=\beta x^{c}$
As we know that, $v \dfrac{d v}{d x}=a_{t}$,
$v \dfrac{d v}{d x}=\alpha x^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{v} v d v=\alpha \int_{0}^{x} x^{2} d x$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{v^{2}}{2}=\dfrac{\alpha}{3} x^{3}$
$\therefore$ Kinetic energy, $K=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{m \alpha}{3} \cdot x^{3}$.
Now, from the above equation we get,
$\beta=\dfrac{m a}{3}$ and $c=3$
So, the correct option is (1).

question: The kinetic energies of two similar cars $A$ and $B$ are $100\,J$ and $225\,J$ respectively. On applying breaks, car A stops after $1000\,m$ and car $B$ stops after $1500\,m$. If $F_{A}$ and $F_{B}$ are the forces applied by the breaks on cars and respectively, then the ratio of $\dfrac{F_{A}}{F_{B}}$ is
explanation:
By work-energy theorem,
$ \Rightarrow - FS = {K_f} - {K_i}$
$ \Rightarrow FS = {K_i} - {K_f} = K\left( {{K_f} = 0} \right)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{F_{A}}{F_{B}}=\dfrac{K_{A}}{K_{B}} \times \dfrac{S_{B}}{S_{A}}=\dfrac{100}{225} \times \dfrac{1500}{1000}=\dfrac{2}{3}$

question: A body of mass $0.25\,kg$ travels along a straight line from $x=0$ to $x=2 m$ with a speed $v=k x^{3 / 2}$ where $k=2\,S I$ units. The work done by the net force during this displacement is
explanation:

At $x=2 m, v_{f}=2 \times 2^{3 / 2}=2^{5 / 2} m / s$
Using work energy theorem, $W=K_{f}-K_{i}$$\Rightarrow W=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{f}^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{i}^{2}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.25 \times\left(2^{5 / 2}\right)^{2}-0=4 J$

question: A particle of mass $m$ moves on a straight line with its velocity increasing with distance according to the equation $v=\alpha \sqrt{x}$, where $\alpha$ is a constant. The total work done by all the forces applied on the particle during its displacement from $x=0$ to $x=d$, will be
explanation:

or $\Rightarrow W=\dfrac{1}{2} m \alpha^{2} d-0=\dfrac{m \alpha^{2} d}{2}$

question: Four packages each having a mass of 4 $kg$ are attached on the belt at equal distances $d=200 {~mm}$ as shown in the figure. Initially belt is at rest. If a constant force of magnitude 840 $N$ is applied to the belt, determine the velocity of package 2 (in ${m} / {s}$ ) as it falls off the belt at point $A$. Assume that the mass of the belt and pulleys is small as compared with the mass of the packages. Assume that the radius of pulley is negligible in comparison to width $d$.
explanation:
$F d=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times v^{2} \times 4 \Rightarrow v^{2}=\dfrac{F d}{8}$
$Fd = \frac{1}{2} \times 3\left( {v_1^2 - {v^2}} \right) \times 4v_1^2$
$ = \frac{{Fd}}{6} + \frac{{Fd}}{8} = \frac{{7Fd}}{{24}}$
$ = \frac{{7 \times F \times d}}{{24}} = \frac{{7 \times 840 \times 0.2}}{{24}}$
$ = 49 \Rightarrow {v_1} = 7\,m{s^{ - 1}}$

question: Two blocks with masses ${m_{1}=3 {~kg}}$ and ${m_{2}=5 {~kg}}$ are connected by a light string that slides over a frictionless pulley as shown in figure. Initially, ${m_{2}}$, is held $5\,m$ off the floor while ${m_{1}}$ is on the floor. The system is then released. At what speed  does ${m_{2}}$ hit the floor?
explanation:
The initial and final configurations are shown in the figure. It is convenient to set ${U=0}$ at the floor. Initially, only ${m_{2}}$ has potential energy. As it falls, it loses potential energy and gains kinetic energy. At the same time, ${m_{1}}$ gains potential energy and kinetic energy. Just before ${m_{2}}$ lands, it has only kinetic energy. Let ${v}$ the final speed of each mass. Then, using the law of conservation of mechanical energy. Final (Just before ${m_{2}}$ strikes the floor)
$\begin{aligned}& K_{f}+U_{f}=K_{i}+U_{i} \\& \dfrac{1}{2}\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) v^{2}+m_{1} g h=0+m_{2} g h\end{aligned}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951614/preptracker/questions/q_36008_explanation_1764951614198.png ${v^{2}=\dfrac{2\left(m_{2}-m_{1}\right) g h}{m_{1}+m_{2}}}$
Putting ${m_{1}=3 {~kg} ; m_{2}=5 {~kg} ; h=5 {~m}}$ and ${g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$
we get ${v^{2}=\dfrac{2(5-3)(10)(5)}{5+3}}$ or ${v=5 {~m} / {s}}$

question: For the situation shown in the given figure, the horizontal surface is rough. The coefficient of friction between the block of mass ${m_{1}=4 {~kg}}$ and the horizontal surface is ${\mu=0.5}$. The blocks are connected with combination of light spring of spring constant ${k}$ and a light string. The block of mass ${m_{2}}$ is released from rest, when spring is in its natural length. Find minimum value of ${m_{2}}$  such that the block of mass ${m_{1}}$ will start moving.
explanation:
Let for just shifting of the block of mass ${m_{1}}$, the elongation in spring is ${x_{1}}$
${\therefore \quad k x_{1}>\mu m_{1} g}$
${\because \quad x_{1 \text { min }}=\dfrac{\mu m_{1} g}{k}}$
For minimum value of ${m_{2}}$, the speed of block of mass ${m_{2}}$ is zero when the elongation in spring is ${x_{1 \text { min }}}$
According to the conservation principle of mechanical energy, Loss in gravitational potential energy of the block mass ${m_{2}}$ is equal to gain in elastic potential energy in the spring.
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & m_{2} g x_{1 \text { min }}=\dfrac{1}{2} k x_{\min }^{2} \\\therefore & x_{1 \text { min }}=\dfrac{2 m_{2} g}{k} \\\text { Or } & \dfrac{\mu m_{1} g}{k}=\dfrac{2 m_{2} g}{k} \\& m_{2}=\dfrac{\mu m_{1}}{2}=\dfrac{0.5 \times 4}{2}=1 {~kg}\end{array}$

question: For the situation shown in the given figure, block ${A}$ of mass $10\,kg$ is at the ground. The block ${B}$ is released from rest when the light spring is unstretched. The minimum mass of the block ${B}$ in $kg$ such that block ${B}$ may leave the contact of ground is
explanation:

For minimum value of ${m, x_{0}}$ should be maximum elongation in the spring. At the instant of maximum elongation, the velocity of block ${B}$ will be zero.
${\therefore}$ Loss in gravitational potential energy of block ${B=}$ Gain in elastic potential energy by spring ${\therefore \quad m g x_{0}=\dfrac{1}{2} k x_{0}^{2}}$
${\therefore \quad x_{0}=\dfrac{2 m g}{k}}$ or ${\dfrac{M g}{k}=\dfrac{2 m g}{k} \Rightarrow m=\dfrac{M}{2}=5 {~kg}}$

question:
A bullet of mass $100\,g$ moving horizontally with a velocity $210\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ gets embedded in a block of mass $2\,kg$ kept on a rough horizontal surface. If the coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and surface is $0.5.$ Calculate the distance block - bullet system will move before coming to rest.
(Take acceleration due to gravity $ = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$).
explanation:
Let mass of bullet be m and mass of block be $M$.
After collision, the block-bullet system will move with velocity $\mathrm{v}_{\text {sys }}$
According to conservation of linear momentum,
$mv = (m + M)\,\,{v_{sys}}$
$\therefore 0.1 \times 210 = 2.10 \times {v_{sys{\rm{ }}}}$
$\therefore \quad {v_{sys}} = 10\,m/s$
Also, K.E. acquired by system is converted to work done in moving the system, i.e., according to work-energy theorem,
$\frac{1}{2}(\,m + M){\left( {{v_{sys{\rm{ }}}}} \right)^2} = F \times s$
$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2}(\,m + M){(10)^2} = \mu (m + M)g \times s$
$\therefore s = \frac{{100}}{{2 \times 0.5 \times 10}} = 10\,m$
subject: Physics

question:
Consider an elliptically shaped rail $PQ$ in the vertical plane with $OP = 6\,m$ and $OQ = 8\,m$. A block of mass $1\,kg$ is pulled along the rail from $P$ to $Q$ with a force of $15\,N,$ which is always parallel to line $PQ$ (see the figure given).
Assuming no frictional losses, the kinetic energy of the block when it reaches $Q$ is $(n \times 10)$ joules.
The value of $n$ is
(Take acceleration due to gravity $ = 10\,m{{\rm{s}}^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
Using work energy theorem
${W_{mg}} + {W_F} = \Delta K\,.E.$
$ - mgh + Fd = \Delta K.E$
$\therefore-1 \times 10 \times 8+15(10)=\Delta K E$
$\therefore \quad \Delta K . E .=70$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=7$

question: A body of  $2\,kg$ mass is initially moving along a straight line with a velocity of $1.5\,m{\rm{/}}s$. A force of $4\,N$ is applied to it at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ to the initial direction of motion. The change in its $K.E.$ after the first second is
explanation:
$a = \frac{{F\cos \theta }}{m} = \frac{{4\cos 60^\circ }}{2} = 1\,m/{s^2}$
$v = u + at = 1.5 + 1 \times 1 = 2.5\,m/{s^2}$
Change in $K.E.$ $ = \frac{1}{2}\,m\left( {{v^2} - {u^2}} \right) = \frac{1}{2} \times 2\left( {{{2.5}^2} - {{1.5}^2}} \right)$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,K.E. = 4\,J$

question: A block of mass $0.24\,kg$ is attached to a spring of force constant $4\,N{\rm{/}}m.$ The coefficient of friction between the block and the floor is $0.2.$ Initially the block is at rest and the spring is unstretched. An impulse is given to the block as shown in the figure. The block slides a distance of $0.18\,m$ and comes to rest for the first time. The initial velocity of the block in $m{\rm{/}}s$ is $v = {\left( {\frac{N}{{100}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}$ then, $N$ is   (Take $g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$ )
explanation:
Decrease in mechanical energy $ = $ Work done against friction
$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} - \frac{1}{2}k{x^2} = \mu \,\,mgx$
$\therefore v = \sqrt {\frac{{2\mu \,\,mgx + k{x^2}}}{{\,m}}} $
$\therefore v = \sqrt {\frac{{2 \times 0.2 \times 0.24 \times 10 \times (0.18) + 4{{(0.18)}^2}}}{{0.24}}} $
$\therefore \quad v = \sqrt {\frac{{63}}{{50}}} $
$\therefore v = \sqrt {\frac{{126}}{{100}}} \,m/s\quad \Rightarrow N = 126$

explanation:

Mechanical energy ${(E)}$ of the body is$E = mgh = 5 \times 9.8 \times 30\,J$
$E = {\text{ Heat produced }}(H) = 5 \times 9.8 \times 30\,J$
$ = \frac{{5 \times 9.8 \times 30}}{{4.2}}cal$
$(1cal = 4.2\,J)$
$ = 350cal$.
So correct option is (3)

question: A block of mass $10\,kg$ is moving in $x$-direction with a constant speed of $10\,m\,{s^{ - 1}}$. It is subjected to a retarding force ${F_r} =  - 0.1x\,J\,{m^{ - 1}}$ during its travel from $x = 20\,m$ to $x = 30\,m$. Its final kinetic energy will be
explanation:
Here,$m = 10\,kg,{v_i} = 10\,m\,{s^{ - 1}}$
Initial kinetic energy of the block is
${K_i} = \frac{1}{2}mv_i^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times (10kg) \times {\left( {10m{s^{ - 1}}} \right)^2}$$ = 500J$
Work done by retarding force
$W = \int {{F_r}} dx = \int_{20}^{30} - 0.1\,xdx = - 0.1\left[ {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2}} \right]_{20}^{30}$
$ = - 0.1\left[ {\frac{{900 - 400}}{2}} \right] = - 25J$
According to work-energy theorem
$W = {K_f} - {K_i} \Rightarrow {K_f} = W + {K_i}$
$ = - 25J + 500J = 475J$

By work-energy theorem,
$W = \Delta \,K = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times {(10)^2} - 0 = 500\,J$

question: A skier starts from rest at point $A$ and slides down the hill without turning or breaking. The friction coefficient is $\mu$. When he stops at point $B$, his horizontal displacement is $s$. What is the height difference between points $A$ and $B$ ? (The velocity of the skier is small, so that the additional pressure on the snow due to the curvature can be neglected. Neglect also the friction of air and the dependence of $\mu$ on the velocity of the skier.)
explanation:
According to question, the situation is shown in the figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951546/preptracker/questions/q_35600_explanation_1764951546153.png For a sufficiently, safe-horizontal displacement $\Delta s$ can be considered straight. If the corresponding length of path element is $\Delta L$, the friction force is given by $\Delta F=\mu m g(\Delta L \cos \theta)$
From, $\Delta \,OPQ$
$\cos \theta=\dfrac{\Delta s}{\Delta L}$
So, $\Delta F=\mu m g \Delta L\left(\dfrac{\Delta s}{\Delta L}\right)=\mu m g \Delta s$
Adding up, we find that along the whole path, the total work done by the friction force is $\mu m g s$. By energy conservation, this must equals to the decrease $m g h$ in potential energy of skier.
$\therefore \mu m g s=m g h$
Hence, $h=\mu s$
subject: Physics

question: The rate of decrease of kinetic energy is  $9.6\,J{s^{ - 1}}.$ Find the magnitude of force acting on particle when its speed is  $3\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$
explanation:
Given, rate of decrease in kinetic energy,$\dfrac{d K}{d t}=9.6 J^{-1}$
When speed, $v = 3\,m{s^{ - 1}},$ then force $F=$ ?
Kinetic energy, $K=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
Differentiating with respect to $t$, we get
$\frac{{dK}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{2}\,\,\,m2v\,\,\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = vm\frac{{dv}}{{dt}}$
$ = vm\,\,\frac{{dp}}{{dt}}$
$\frac{{dK}}{{dt}} = vF\,\,\,\left[ {\because {\text{ force}},{\text{ }}F = m\frac{{dp}}{{dt}}} \right]$
$\therefore \quad F = \frac{1}{v} \cdot \frac{{dK}}{{dt}}$
$ = \frac{1}{3} \times 9.6$
$ = 3.2\,N$

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
A truck and a car moving with same kinetic energy are brought to rest by applying breaks which provides equal retarding forces. Both come to rest in equal distance.
A car moving towards east takes a turn and moves towards north, the speed remains unchanged. The acceleration of the car is zero.
explanation:
$K \cdot E=\dfrac{1}{2} m V_{m}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} M V_{M}^{2}$
Where $m=$ Velocity of car
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,M = $ Velocity of truck.
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{V_m} = $ Velocity of car
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{V_M} = $ Velocity of truck.
$\therefore V_{m}^{2}=\dfrac{M}{m} V_{M}^{2}$
Now for the car, $\therefore V_{m}^{2}=2 \cdot a_{m} \cdot S_{m}$
$\Rightarrow S_{m}=\dfrac{V_{m}^{2}}{2 a_{m}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{M}{m} V_{m}^{2}}{2 \dfrac{F}{m}}=\dfrac{M V_{m}^{2}}{2 F}$.
For the truck,
$S_{M}=\dfrac{V_{M}^{2}}{2 a_{M}}=\dfrac{V_{M}^{2}}{2 \dfrac{F}{M}}=\dfrac{M V_{M}^{2}}{2 F}=\dfrac{m V_{m}^{2}}{2 F}$
$\therefore S_{m}=S_{M}$.
Initial velocity of the car $\vec{V}_{i}=V \hat{i}$ and final velocity $\vec{V}_{f}=V \hat{j}$. As velocity changes, acceleration of the car cannot be zero. So statement (4) is correct.

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are correct.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.
Both Statement A and Statement B are incorrect.
Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.

explanation:

$18 - 11 = 0.6 \times 10 \times d$
$d = \frac{7}{6} = 1.17\,m$.

question: A dog of mass $m = 4\,kg$ runs from the left end of a curved ramp with speed ${V_0} = 8\,m{\rm{/}}s$ at height $8\,m$ above the ground. It then slides to the right and comes to rest when it reaches a height $10\,m$ from the ground. The maximum increase in thermal energy of the dog-ramp system because of sliding is $\left( {g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}} \right)$
explanation:
Loss of energy $=E_{i}-E_{f}$
${W_{slide{\rm{ }}}} + {W_{conservative{\rm{ }}}} = 0 - \frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 64$
${W_s} + \left( {{U_1} - {U_2}} \right) = - 128$
${W_s} + 4 \times 10 \times 8 - 4 \times 10 \times 10 = - 128$
${W_s} + 320 - 400 = - 128 \Rightarrow {W_s} - 80 = - 128$
${W_s} = - 128 + 80\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \Rightarrow {W_s} = - 48\,J$

question: A particle experiences a variable force $\vec{F}=\left(4 x \hat{i}+3 y^{2} \hat{j}\right)$ in a horizontal $x$-$y$ plane. Assume distance in meter and force in newton. If the particle moves from point $(1,2)$ to point $(2,3)$ in the $x-y$ plane, the Kinetic energy changes by
explanation:
$F=4 x \hat{i}+3 y^{2} \hat{j}$
We use work-energy theorem
Work done $=$ change in kinetic energy
$W=\Delta E_{K}$
Work $W=\int \vec{F} \cdot(d x \hat{i}+d y \hat{j})$
$\begin{aligned}& =\int_{1}^{2} 4 x d x+\int_{2}^{3} 3 y^{2} d x \\& \Rightarrow W=\left(2 x^{2}\right)_{1}^{2}+\left(y^{3}\right)_{2}^{3} \\& =(8-2)+(27-8) \\& =6+19=25 J \\& \Rightarrow \Delta E_{K}=25 J\end{aligned}$

explanation:

question: A uniform chain of length $L$ and mass $M$ overhangs a horizontal table with its two-third part on a table. The friction coefficient between the table and the chain is $\mu$. The work done by the friction during the period the chain slips off the table is
explanation:
Let $x$ be the length of the chain on the table. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955780/preptracker/questions/q_34677_explanation_1764955779681.png The frictional force on the chain is
$\begin{aligned}& f=\mu(\lambda x) g \quad\left(\mu=\dfrac{M}{L}\right) \\& d W=f d x=\mu \lambda g x d x\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned}& W=\mu \lambda g \int_{2 L / 3}^{0} x d x=\dfrac{\mu M g}{2 L}\left[x^{2}\right]_{2 L / 3}^{0} \\& W=\dfrac{\mu M g}{2 L}\left[-\left(\dfrac{2 L}{3}\right)^{2}\right]=\dfrac{-\mu M g}{2 L} \times \dfrac{4 L^{2}}{9} \\& W=\dfrac{-2 \mu M g L}{9}\end{aligned}$

question: A ball is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity of 10 $m/s$. It returns to the ground with a velocity of 9 $m/s$. If  $g = 9.8\,m/{s^2}$, then the maximum height attained by the is nearly ( assume air resistance to be uniform)
explanation:
Total loss in friction $ = {K_i} - {K_f}$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\,m(100 - 81)$
Half of the loss will be in upward direction Hence,
$mgh + \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{19}}{2}\,m} \right) = {K_i} = \frac{1}{2}\,m(100) = 50\,m$
Therefore, $h = 4.61\,m$

question: A truck accelerates from speed $v$ to 2 $v$. Work done in during this is
explanation:
Let $m$ be mass of the truck.
According to work-energy theorem
$W = {K_f} - {K_i}$
Work done in accelerating the truck from rest to speed $v$ is
$\begin{aligned}& W_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}-0 \quad(\because u=0) \\& =\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}\end{aligned}$
Work done in accelerating the truck from speed $v$ to 2$v$ is
$W_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m(2 v)^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} m(v)^{2}$
$=\dfrac{3}{2} m v^{2}=3 \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=3 W_{1} \quad(\operatorname{Using}(1))$

options.text: Three times as the work done in accelerating it from rest to $v$
Same as the work done in accelerating it from rest to $v$
Four times as the work done in accelerating it from rest to $v$
Lless than the work done in accelerating it from rest to $v$

question: The trolley starts accelerating with acceleration $a$. The maximum angle deflected by thread from vertical will be:
explanation:
By work energy theorem w.r.to trolley
$\begin{aligned}& W_{m g}+W_{F_{s}}=\Delta K . E \\& W_{m g}=-m g L(1-\cos \theta) \\& W_{F_{s}}=m a L \sin \theta\end{aligned}$
Where $F_{s}$ is the pseudo force $\Delta K . E=0$
$ \Rightarrow \tan \frac{\theta }{2} = \frac{a}{g}$
$ \Rightarrow \theta = 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{a}{g}} \right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951188/preptracker/questions/q_33970_explanation_1764951188535.png

question: A 1 $kg$ mass is projected down a rough circular track placed in vertical plane as shown. The speed of the mass at point$A$ is 3 $m/s$  and at point $B$, it is 60 $m/s$. How much work is done on the mass between $A$ and $B$ by the force of friction?
explanation:
The $K.E$ at $A$ and $B$ are
$\begin{aligned}& K_{A}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 1 \times 3^{2}=4 \cdot 5 \mathrm{~J} \\& K_{B}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 1 \times 6^{2}=18 \mathrm{~J} \\& K_{B}-K_{A}=13 \cdot 5 \mathrm{~J} \\& \Delta U_{B A}=m g h=1 \times 10 \times 2=20 \\& \left|W_{f}\right|=20-13 \cdot 5=6.5 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

question: The block of mass $m$ is pulling, vertically up with constant speed, by applying force $P$. The free end of the string is pulled by $l$ meter, the increase in potential energy of the block is
explanation:

$l=y_{P}+2 y_{B}+C$
where $\mathrm{C}$ and $l$ are constant
By differentiating the equation
$0=\Delta y_{P}+2 \Delta y_{B}$
As $\left(\Delta y_{P}\right)=l \Rightarrow\left(\Delta y_{B}\right)=\dfrac{l}{2}$
$\Delta U=\dfrac{m g l}{2}$

question: A body of mass 3 $kg$ is under a force which causes a displacement in it and is given by $S=\dfrac{t^{3}}{3}$ (in metres). Find the work done by the force in first 2 seconds.
explanation:
$S=\dfrac{t^{3}}{3} \Rightarrow d S=t^{2} d t$
$a=\dfrac{d^{2} S}{d t^{2}}=\dfrac{d^{2}}{d t^{2}}\left[\dfrac{t^{3}}{3}\right]=2 t m / s^{2}$
Now work done by the force
$\begin{aligned}& W=\int_{0}^{2} \vec{F} \cdot d \vec{S}=\int_{0}^{2} m a \cdot d S \\& \int_{0}^{2} 3 \times 2 t \times t^{2} d t=\int_{0}^{2} 6 t^{3} d t=\dfrac{3}{2}\left[t^{4}\right]_{0}^{2}=24 J\end{aligned}$

question: How much work must be done by a force on 50 $kg$  body in order to accelerate it from rest to 20 $m/s$ in $10 s ?$
explanation:
From equation of motion
$\begin{aligned}& v=u+a t \Rightarrow 20=0+a \times 10 \\& 20=a \times 10 \Rightarrow a=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\end{aligned}$
Now, distance
$s=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2} \Rightarrow s=0+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10 \times 10$
or $\mathrm{s}=100 \mathrm{~m}$
$W=F . s=\text { mas }=50 \times 2 \times 100=10^{4} \mathrm{~J} .$

question: The earth circles the sun once a year. How much work would have to be done on the earth to bring it to rest relative to the sun, (ignore the rotation of earth about-its own axis) Given that mass of the earth is $6 \times {10^{24}}\,kg$ and distance between the sun and earth is $1.5 \times {10^{11}}\,m$
explanation:
As $T=(2 \pi / \omega)$,
So $\omega=2 \pi /\left(3.15 \times 10^{7}\right)=1.99 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$
Now $v=r \omega=1.5 \times 10^{11} \times 1.99 \times 10^{-7}$
$ \approx 3 \times {10^4}\,m/s$
Now by work-energy theorem,
$W = {K_f} - {K_i} = 0 - \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$
$ = - \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times {10^{24}}{\left( {3 \times {{10}^4}} \right)^2} = - 2.7 \times {10^{33}}J$

When $\mathrm{t}=0$, then $v=0$. When $\mathrm{t}=2$, then $v = 4\,m/s$
Work done in first two seconds = change in KE
$W=\dfrac{1}{2} m\left[(4)^{2}-(0)^{2}\right]=\dfrac{1}{2} 2 \times 16=16$

explanation:

$=\dfrac{M^{2} g h}{(M+m) d}+(M+m) g$

question: Two cylindrical vessel of equal cross-sectional area contain water upto height $h_{1}$ and $h_{2}$. The vessels are interconnected so that the levels in them become equal. The work done by the forces of gravity during the process is:
explanation:
When level becomes equal then
$h=\dfrac{h_{1}+h_{2}}{2}$
Work done by gravity $=$ change in potential energy of the water column
$\begin{aligned}& =2(A h \rho) g \dfrac{h}{2}-\left[A h_{1} \rho g \dfrac{h_{1}}{2}+A h_{2} \rho \dfrac{g h_{2}}{2}\right] \\& =A \rho g h^{2}-\left[A \rho g \dfrac{h_{1}^{2}}{2}+\dfrac{A \rho g h_{2}^{2}}{2}\right] \\& =A \rho g\left[\left(\dfrac{h_{1}+h_{2}}{2}\right)^{2}-\dfrac{h_{1}^{2}+h_{2}^{2}}{2}\right] \\& =A \rho g\left[\dfrac{h_{1}-h_{2}}{2}\right]^{2}\end{aligned}$

question: A variable force, given by the 2-dimensional vector $\vec{F}=\left(3 x^{2} \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}\right)$, acts on a particle. The force is in newton and $\mathrm{x}$ is in metre. What is the change in the kinetic energy of the particle as it moves from the point with coordinates $(2,3)$ to $(3,0)$ ( the coordinates are in metres)
explanation:
$\vec{F}=\left(3 x^{2} \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}\right), \vec{r}=x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}$
$\therefore d \vec{r}=d x \hat{i}+d y \hat{j}$
Work done,
$\begin{aligned}W & =\int \vec{F} \cdot d \vec{r}=\int_{(2,3)}^{(3,0)}\left(3 x^{2} \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}\right) \cdot(d x \hat{i}+d y \hat{j}) \\& =\int_{(2,3)}^{(3,0)}\left(3 x^{2} d x+4 d y\right)=\left[x^{3}+4 y\right]_{(2,3)}^{(3,0)} \\& =\left(3^{3}+4 \times 0\right)-\left(2^{3}+4 \times 3\right)=+7 J\end{aligned}$
Change in the kinetic energy $=$ Work done,
$\mathrm{W}=+7 \mathrm{~J}$

question: A bullet of mass $25\,gm$ moves horizontally at a speed of $250\,m/s$  is fired into a wooden block of mass $1\,kg$ suspended by a long string. The bullet crosses the block and emerges on the other side. If the centre of the mass of the  block rises through a height of $20\,cm$. The  speed of the bullet as it emerges from the block  is (take, $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
The given situation is shown in the following figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958005/preptracker/questions/q_108612_explanation_1764958004430.png Let ${v_1}$ and ${v_2}$ are the final velocities of bullet and block, respectively, as block rises upto height $h$.
Hence, according to conservation of energy,
$\frac{1}{2}M\,v_2^2 = M\,gh \Rightarrow {v_2} = \sqrt {2gh} $
Given, $h = 20\,cm = 20 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{v_2} = \sqrt {2 \times 10 \times 20 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} = 2\,m/s$
Now, conservation of momentum gives
$mu = m{v_1} + M{v_2}$
$\frac{{25}}{{1000}} \times 250 = \frac{{25}}{{1000}} \times {v_1} + 1 \times 2$
$\frac{{25}}{4} = \frac{{25}}{{1000}} \times {v_1} + 2 \Rightarrow {v_1} = 170\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question: A ball of mass $2 g$ released from the top of an inclined plane describes a circular motion of radius $20\,cm$ in the vertical plane upon reaching the bottom. The minimum height of the inclined plane is
explanation:
The motion of the ball is as shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952418/preptracker/questions/q_108531_explanation_1764952417934.png At bottom of incline (at $P$)
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=m g h$
$ \Rightarrow \quad v = \sqrt {2gh} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
To complete the vertical circle,
$\Rightarrow \quad v \geq \sqrt{5 g R}$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{2 g h} \geq \sqrt{5 g R}$ [from Eq.(1)]
$\Rightarrow h \geq \dfrac{5}{2} R$ or $h_{\text {min }}=\dfrac{5}{2} \times 20=50 {~cm}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question: A body of mass $m$ slides down along a frictionless inclined plane from height $h$ and just completes motion in a vertical circle of radius $2 m$ after reaching the bottom. What is the value of $h$ ? [Use, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ ]
explanation:
The given situation is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958001/preptracker/questions/q_108529_explanation_1764958000562.png Radius of vertical circle, $r=\dfrac{A B}{2}=2 m$
$\Rightarrow A B=$ diameter $=d=2 \times 2=4 {~m}$
Using the concept of conservation of energy, Total energy at point $X=$ Total energy at point $Y$.
$\Rightarrow m g h+0=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+0$
$h = \frac{{{v^2}}}{{2g}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Since, the body just completes motion in vertical circle, hence
$v = \sqrt {5gr} {\text{ }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$h=\dfrac{(\sqrt{5 g r})^{2}}{2 g}=\dfrac{5 g r}{2 g}=\dfrac{5 r}{2}=\dfrac{5 \times 2}{2}=5 {~m}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question: Consider a particle moving with a minimum speed $v$ at highest point of vertical circle of radius $R$. If the radius of the circle is doubled then the corresponding minimum speed will be
explanation:
When a particle moves along a vertical circle,
$v_{\text {min }}=\sqrt{g R} \Rightarrow v_{\text {min }} \propto \sqrt{R}$
so, $\dfrac{\left(v_{\text {min }}\right)_{1}}{\left(v_{\text {min }}\right)_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{v}{\left(v_{\text {min }}\right)_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{R}{2 R}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{v}{\left(v_{\min }\right)_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow\left(v_{\min }\right)_{2}=\sqrt{2} v$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question: A light rigid wire of length $1 m$ is attached to a ball $A$ of mass $500\,gm$ to one end. The other end of the wire is fixed, so that the wire can rotate freely in the vertical plane about its fixed end. At the lowest point of the circular motion, the ball is given a horizontal velocity of $6 {~m} / {s}$. Determine the radial component of the acceleration of the ball, when this rigid wire makes an angle $60^{\circ}$ with the upward vertical. (Take, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
Given, $l=1 {~m}, m_{\text {ball }}=500 {~g}=0.5 {~kg}$
velocity of ball at a point, $A=v m / s$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956159/preptracker/questions/q_108479_explanation_1764956158985.png Motion of body in vertical plane is shown in the figure. In $\triangle B O A$,
$\cos 60^{\circ}=\dfrac{O B}{O A} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2}=\dfrac{h}{1} \Rightarrow h=\dfrac{1}{2} m$
$\therefore C B=C O+O B=l+h=1+\dfrac{1}{2}$
$CB = \frac{3}{2}m\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
According to the laws of conservation of energy, ($K E+P E$) at a point $C=(K E+P E)$ at point $A$
$\dfrac{1}{2} m u^{2}+0=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+m g(C B)$
$\Rightarrow u^{2}=v^{2}+2 g(C B)$
$\Rightarrow 6^{2}=v^{2}+2 g \cdot \dfrac{3}{2}$ [From Eq.(1)]
$\Rightarrow 6^{2}=v^{2}+2 \times 10 \times \dfrac{3}{2}$
$v^{2}=6 m^{2} s^{-2}$
$\therefore$ Radial component of acceleration of the ball $a_{c}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{l}=\dfrac{6}{1}=6 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question: A bob of heavy mass $m$ is suspended by a light string of length $l$. The bob is given a horizontal velocity ${v_0}$ as shown in figure. If the string gets slack at some point $P$ making an angle $\theta$ from the horizontal, the ratio of the speed $v$ of the bob at point $P$ to its initial speed $v_{0}$ is:
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952412/preptracker/questions/q_108464_explanation_1764952411523.png At Point $P$ tension in the string is zero
$mg\sin \theta = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{l}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
By conservation of mechanical energy at point $P$ and $Q$
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+m g(l+l \sin \theta)$
$\dfrac{v_{0}^{2}}{2}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{2}+g l(1+\sin \theta)$
Put $g l=\dfrac{v^{2}}{\sin \theta}$ using (1)
$\dfrac{v_{0}^{2}}{2}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{2}+\dfrac{v^{2}}{\sin \theta}(1+\sin \theta)$
$\dfrac{v_{0}^{2}}{2}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{2}+\dfrac{v^{2}}{\sin \theta}+v^{2}$
$v_{0}^{2}=v^{2}\left[3+\dfrac{2}{\sin \theta}\right]$
$\dfrac{v}{v_{0}}=\left(\dfrac{\sin \theta}{3 \sin \theta+2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question: A bob of mass ' $m$ ' is suspended by a light string of length ' $L$ '. It is imparted a minimum horizontal velocity at the lowest point $A$ such that it just completes half circle reaching the top most position $B$. The ratio of kinetic energies  $\frac{{{{({\rm{ }}K.E.{\rm{ }})}_A}}}{{{{({\rm{ }}K.E.{\rm{ }})}_B}}}$  is
explanation:
The critical velocity at $A$ is $v_{L}=\sqrt{5 g L}$ and at $B$ is $v_{H}=\sqrt{g L}$.
$K E_{A}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{L}^{2} \quad K E_{B}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{H}^{2}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956870/preptracker/questions/q_36270_explanation_1764956869752.png
So, $\dfrac{K E_{A}}{K E_{B}}=\dfrac{v_{L}^{2}}{v_{H}^{2}}=\dfrac{5 g L}{g L}=\dfrac{5}{1}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question:
The bob of a pendulum was released from a horizontal position. The length of the pendulum is $10\,m$ . If it dissipates $10 \%$ of its initial energy against air resistance, the speed with which the bob arrives at the lowest point is
[Use, $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ ]
explanation:
Let the speed at lowest point is $v$.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951663/preptracker/questions/q_36266_explanation_1764951662710.png
Using work energy theorem $\triangle K E=$ Work done by all the forces
$K{E_B} = mgl - \frac{{10}}{{100}}mgl$
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{{90}}{{100}}mgl$
$\frac{1}{2}{v^2} = \frac{9}{{10}} \times 10 \times 10$
$ \Rightarrow v = 6\sqrt 5 \,m/s$
Therefore, option (4) is correct.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question:
A body of mass $m\,kg$ slides from rest along the curve of vertical circle from point $A$ to $B$ in frictionless path. The velocity of the body at $B$ is

(Given : $R = 14\,m,$ $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$ and $\sqrt 2 = 1.4$ )
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955948/preptracker/questions/q_36227_explanation_1764955947858.png
Height, $A C=R \sin 45^{\circ}=\dfrac{R}{\sqrt{2}}$
According to law of conservation of mechanical energy
$K E_{A}+P E_{A}=K E_{B}+P E_{B}$
$0+m g\left(R+\dfrac{R}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{B}^{2}+0$
or $g R\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{2}+1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=\dfrac{1}{2} v_{B}^{2}$
${v_B} = \sqrt {\frac{{2 \times 10(1.4 + 1) \times 14}}{{1.4}}} = 21.9\,m/s$
subject: Physics

question: An insect jumps from ball $A$ onto ball $B$. which are suspended from inextensible light strings each of length $L=8 {~cm}$. The mass of each ball and insect is same. What should be the minimum relative velocity   of jump of insect w.r.t. ball $A$, if both the balls manage to complete the full circle?
explanation:
For completing the full circle: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951634/preptracker/questions/q_36116_explanation_1764951634151.png $\dfrac{u}{2}=\sqrt{5 g L} \Rightarrow u=4 {~ms}^{-1}$
Relative velocity $=2 u=8 {~ms}^{-1}$

question:
The mass of a pendulum bob is $200$ gram and the string is $2$ metre long. The bob is held so that the string is horizontal, and it is then allowed to fall. The K.E. of the bob when string makes an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with vertical is
(Take $g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$ and $\sqrt{2}=1.41$ )
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951593/preptracker/questions/q_35877_explanation_1764951593474.png Height through which the bob falls,
$h = l\sin \theta = l\sin 45^\circ = 2 \times \sin 45^\circ $
$ = \sqrt 2 = 1.41\,m$
K.E. gained $=$ P.E. lost $ = mgh$
$ = \frac{{200}}{{1000}} \times 10 \times 1.41 = 2.82\,J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question: A particle is given an initial speed $u$ inside a smooth spherical shell of radius $R = 2\,m$ so that it just completes a circle. Acceleration of the particle when it is in vertical circle is   $(g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2},\sqrt {10}  = 3.16)$
explanation:
Vertical velocity at bottom,
${u^2} = 5gR$
$\therefore \quad {v^2} = {u^2} - 2\,\,gR = 5\,\,gR - 2\,\,gR = 3\,\,gR$
Tangential acceleration at bottom is ${a_t} = g$
(downwards)
Centripetal acceleration at bottom is
${a_c} = \frac{{{v^2}}}{R} = 3\,\,g$
$\therefore \quad a = \sqrt {a_c^2 + a_1^2} = \sqrt {{g^2} + 9{g^2}} $
$ = g\sqrt {10} = 10\sqrt {10} $
$\therefore \,\,\,\,a = 31.6\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question: The velocity of a body moving in a vertical circle of radius $r$ is $\sqrt{7 g r}$ at the lowest point of the circle. What is the ratio of maximum and minimum tension?
explanation:
Tension is maximum at the lowest point and minimum at the highest point.
Tension at the lowest point,
${T_L} = mg + \frac{{mv_L^2}}{r} = mg + \frac{{7mgr}}{r}$
$\left( {\because {v_L} = \sqrt {7gr} } \right)$
${T_L} = 8\,mg$
$As\,{T_L} - {T_H} = 6mg$
$\therefore {T_H} = {T_L} - 6mg = 8mg - 6mg = 2mg$
$\therefore \frac{{{T_L}}}{{{T_H}}} = \frac{4}{1}\,or\,\frac{{{T_{\max }}}}{{{T_{\min }}}} = \frac{4}{1}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question: A body of mass $1\,kg$ is rotating in a vertical circle of radius $1\,m.$ what will be the difference in its kinetic energy at the top and bottom of the circle?(take, $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ )
explanation:
Kinetic energy is the energy possessed by a body of mass $m$ due to its
velocity $v, K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
Velocity at the top is
$\sqrt{g r}$ and that at the bottom is $\sqrt{5 g r}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951559/preptracker/questions/q_35655_explanation_1764951558775.png Hence, required difference in kinetic energy
$\Delta K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m_{1} v_{1}^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{2}^{2}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m(5 g r-g r)=2 g m r$
Given, $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}},\,\,m = 1\,kg$
and $r=1 m$
$ = 2 \times 10 \times 1 \times 1 = 20\,J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question: One end of a string of length $1.0\,m$ is tied to a body of mass $0.5\,kg.$ It is whirled in a vertical circle with angular frequency $4\,rad{s^{ - 1}}.$ The tension in the string when the body is at the lower most point of its motion will be equal to (take, $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ )
explanation:
Given, $r=1 m$,
$m = 0.5\,kg$
and $\quad \omega = 4\,rad{s^{ - 1}}$
As, the tension in the string,
$T=m \omega^{2} r+m g$
$=0.5 \times(4)^{2} \times 1$
$+0.5 \times 10$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951557/preptracker/questions/q_35643_explanation_1764951557090.png $=13 N$
subject: Physics

question: Find the maximum radius of circle, so that the block can complete the circular motion.
explanation:
From energy conservation,
$m g H=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
$ \Rightarrow {v^2} = 2gH\,\,or\,\,v = \sqrt {2gH} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
To complete circular loop, minimum speed at bottom point should be
${v_{\min }} = \sqrt {5gR} {\rm{ }}\quad \quad (2)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$\sqrt {2gH} \ge \sqrt {5gR} $
$ \Rightarrow \quad R \le \frac{{2H}}{R}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {R_{\max }} = \frac{{2H}}{R} = \frac{{2(5)}}{5} = 2\,cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question: A particle is suspended from a string of length ${R}$. It is given a velocity ${v=3 \sqrt{g R}}$ at the bottom. Find velocity at point ${B}$.
explanation:
$\text { At } B, \dfrac{1}{2} m v_{B}^{2}+m g R=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
$\begin{aligned}& v_{B}^{2}-v^{2}=-2 g R \\& v_{B}^{2}=9 g R-2 g R \\& v_{B}^{2}=7 g R \Rightarrow v_{B}=\sqrt{7 g R}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A block of mass ${m}$ is pressed against a spring and released, as shown in the figure. What is the minimum compression in spring for which normal reaction of the block and velocity of the block, simultaneously, become zero in subsequent motion on loop?
explanation:
Velocity becomes zero when height attained by block is maximum, and the spring force is stored as potential energy.
$\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}=m g R \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 m g R}{k}}$

question: A stone of mass $1\,kg$ tied on one end of the light string is whirled in vertical circle, as shown in the figure. The tension in the string (in ${N}$) when the string becomes horizontal is
explanation:
Velocity when the string becomes horizontal,
${v=\sqrt{u^{2}-2 g l}=\sqrt{60-20} ; v=\sqrt{40} {~m} / {s}}$
${\Rightarrow T=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{l}=\dfrac{1(40)}{1} \Rightarrow T=40 {~N}}$

question: A ball suspended by a thread swings in a vertical plane so that its magnitude of acceleration in the extreme position and lowest position are equal. The angle $(\theta)$ of thread deflection in the extreme position will be :
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951454/preptracker/questions/q_35358_explanation_1764951454410.png As per question
$\dfrac{v^{2}}{l}=g \sin \theta_{0}$
using energy conservation
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{2 g l\left(1-\cos \theta_{0}\right)}{l}=g \sin \theta_{0}$
$\Rightarrow 2\left(1-\cos \theta_{0}\right)=\sin \theta_{0}$
$\Rightarrow \tan \dfrac{\theta_{0}}{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \theta_{0}=2 \tan ^{-1} \dfrac{1}{2}$

question: A bob of mass $M$ is suspended by a massless string of length $L$. The horizontal velocity $V$ at position $A$ is just sufficient to make it reach the point $B$. The angle $\theta$ at which the speed of the bob is half of that at $A$, satisfies
explanation:
$v=\sqrt{5 g \ell}$
$v_{\text {final }}=\dfrac{v}{2}=\dfrac{\sqrt{5 g \ell}}{2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{5}{4} g \ell}=\sqrt{u^{2}-2 g h}$
$\sqrt{\dfrac{5 g \ell}{4}}=\sqrt{u^{2}-2 g \ell(1-\cos \theta)}$
$\dfrac{5 g \ell}{4}-5 g \ell=-2 g \ell(1-\cos \theta)$
$\dfrac{15}{4}=2(1-\cos \theta) \Rightarrow \cos \theta=-\dfrac{7}{4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{3 \pi}{4} < \theta < \pi$.

question: A stone of mass $m$ is tied to a string and is moved in a vertical circle of radius $r$ making $n\,rev{\rm{/}}\min $. The total tension in the string when the stone is at the lowest point is
explanation:
Tension at lowest point is given by
$T=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}+m g=m r \omega^{2}+m g$
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2024_02_14_1c8494a226fb2c4787ebg-4.jpg?height=388&width=331&top_left_y=1966&top_left_x=366
$=m r\left(\dfrac{2 \pi n}{60}\right)^{2}+m g=m\left(\dfrac{\pi^{2} n^{2} r}{900}+g\right)$

question: One end of the string of length $1.0 \mathrm{~m}$ is tied to a body of mass $0.5 \mathrm{~kg}$. It is whirled in a vertical circle with angular frequency $4 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. The tension in the string when the body is at the lower most point of its motion will be equal to (take, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2024_02_14_1c8494a226fb2c4787ebg-4.jpg?height=290&width=203&top_left_y=1086&top_left_x=381
$F_{c}=T-m g$
$T=F_{c}+m g$
$=m w^{2} r+m g$
$\therefore$ Tension in the string, $T$ is
$T = 0.5 \times {(4)^2} \times 1 + 0.5 \times 10 = 13\,N$

explanation:

Centripetal acceleration $a_{c}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{R}=\dfrac{(\sqrt{g R})^{2}}{R}=g$

question: A pilot of mass $m$ can bear a maximum apparent weight 7 times of $m g$. The aeroplane is moving in a vertical circle. If the velocity of aeroplane is $210\,m{\rm{/}}s$ while diving up from the lowest point of vertical circle, the minimum radius of vertical circle should be
explanation:
The normal force experienced by the passenger is given by
$N = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} + mg = 7\,mg$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} = 6mg \Rightarrow r = \frac{{{v^2}}}{{6g}} = \frac{{210 \times 210}}{{6 \times 10}} = 735\,m$

question:
Assertion: For looping a vertical loop of radius, $r$ the minimum velocity at lowest point should be $\sqrt{5 g r}$.

Reason: In this event, the velocity at the highest point will be zero.
explanation:
At the lowest piont of a vertical circle, the minimum velocity at bottom
$v_{\min }=\sqrt{5 g r}$
Velocity at highest point $(v)=\sqrt{g r}$
So, Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: A stone of mass $m$, tied to a string is being whirled in a vertical circle with a uniform speed. The tension in the string is:
explanation:
In physics, tension is described as the pulling force transmitted by a string (or cable etc). [Tension can also be described as the action: reaction pair of forces acting at each end of string so, we show https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951353/preptracker/questions/q_34874_explanation_1764951352790.png in string generally]. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951354/preptracker/questions/q_34874_explanation_1764951353650.png Vertical circular motion is described as non-uniform due to the fact that velocity and tension vary.
$h=R(1-\cos \theta)$
$\dfrac{1}{2} m\left(u^{2}-v^{2}\right)=m g h$
$T-m g \cos \theta=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{R}$
If the maximum is substracted from $\mathrm{T}$, then tension T will be minimum. We know $(\cos \theta)$ is maximum when $\theta=0$ i.e., lowest position.

options.text: minimum at the highest position of the circular path
minimum at the lowest position of the circular path
minimum when the rope is in the horizontal position

subject: Physics

question: A ball weighting 1.0 $kg$ is tied to a string 15 $cm$ long. Initially the ball is held in position such that the string is horizontal. The ball is now released. A nail $N$ is situated vertically below the support at a distance $L$. The minimum value of $L$ such that the string will be wound round the nail is
explanation:
The velocity of the bob at the lowest position is
$u = \sqrt {2g{L_0}} $
If the bob has to make circular motion then
$u = \sqrt {2g{L_0}} = \sqrt {5g({L_0} - L)} $
$L = \frac{3}{5}{L_0} = 9cm$
subject: Physics

question: In vertical circular motion, the ratio of kinetic energy of a particle at highest point to that at lowest point is
explanation:
Velocity of a particle at its lowest point in vertical cirular motion is given by ${v_l} = \sqrt {5gr} $ and at highest point is given by ${v_h} = \sqrt {rg} $
So,$KE$ at highest point, ${K_h} = \frac{1}{2}m{({v_h})^2} = \frac{{mrg}}{2}$
and $KE$ at lowest point, ${K_l} = \frac{1}{2}m{(v{}_l)^2} = \frac{{5mgr}}{2}$
$\frac{{{K_h}}}{{{K_i}}} = \frac{{\frac{{mgr}}{2}}}{{\frac{{5mgr}}{2}}} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question: A particle moves down the inclined surface and completes a vertical circular motion. The ratio of $H$ and $h$ is
explanation:
Velocity at the bottom of the
circle $=\sqrt{5 g r}$
Velocity at the top of the circle $ = \sqrt {gr} $
Using $P.E.$ = $K.E.$, we get
$mgH = \frac{1}{2}m{(\sqrt {5gr} )^2} = \frac{1}{2}m(5gr){\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,(1)$
$mgh = \frac{1}{2}m{(\sqrt {gr} )^2} = \frac{1}{2}mgr{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (2)$
From eq's (1) and (2), we get
$\dfrac{H}{h}=5$

question: A small ball is shot into each of the four tracks as shown below. Each of the track rises to the same height. The speed with which the ball enters the track is the same in all cases. At the highest point of the track, the normal reaction is maximum in case of
explanation:
Here, speed of the block at the highest points in all the four cases will be same. The normal reactions at these points will depend upon the centripetal acceleration experienced by the block.
Centripetal acceleration $=\dfrac{v^{2}}{R}$
Radius of curvature in the first case will be minimum, so centripetal acceleration and hence reaction will be maximum in this case.

question: A ball is released from rest at point $A$. Find the minimum height $h$ so that the ball just completes the circular motion. (All surfaces are smooth).
explanation:
From conservation of energy at both the top positions $m g h=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+2 m g R$
$g h=\dfrac{v^{2}}{2}+2 g R$
Required velocity at the top, $v=\sqrt{g R}$
$g h=\dfrac{g R}{2}+2 g R \Rightarrow h=\dfrac{5}{2} R$

question: A girl in a swing is 2.5 $m$ above ground at the maximum height and at 1.5 $m$ above the ground at the lowest point. Her maximum velocity in the swing is $\left(g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}\right)$
explanation:
At the highest point, $v=0, h_{1}=2.5 m$
$\therefore$ Toatal energy, $E_{1}=m g h_{1}+0=m g h_{1}$
At the lowest point,$v = ?,{h_2} = 1.5\,cm$ $\therefore$ Total energy, $E_{2}=m g h_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
$\begin{gathered}E_{1}=E_{2} \\m g h_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+m g h_{2} \\v^{2}=2 g\left(h_{1}-h_{2}\right) \\v=\sqrt{2 \times 10 \times(2.5-1.5)}=2 \sqrt{5} m s^{-1}\end{gathered}$

question: A particle moves along a vertical circle of radius $r$ with a velocity $\sqrt{10 r g}$ at $Y$. If $T_{A}, T_{B}, T_{X}, T_{Y}$ represent tension at $A$, $B$, $X$ and $Y$, respectively, then
explanation:
From conservation of energy
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{y}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{x}^{2}+m g(2 r)$
$v_{y}=\sqrt{10 g r}$
$10 g r=v_{X}^{2}+4 g r \Rightarrow v_{x}=6 g r$
$T_{Y}=m g+\dfrac{m v_{y}^{2}}{r}=m g+10 m g=11 m g$
$T_{X}=\dfrac{m v_{x}^{2}}{r}-m g=6 m g-m g=5 m g$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {T_Y} - {T_X} = 6\,mg$
subject: Physics

question: The bob of a pendulum at rest is given a sharp hit to impart a horizontal velocity $\sqrt{10 g l}$, where $l$ is the length of the pendulum. Find the tension in the string when the string makes an angle $60^{\circ}$ w.r.to $+y$-axis
explanation:
Let the velocity at point $\mathrm{D}$ be $v_{2}$.
From conservation of mechanical energy between $A$ and $B$, $\Delta K + \Delta U = 0$
$\begin{aligned}& \left(\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{2}^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{1}^{2}\right)+\left[m g l\left(1+\cos 60^{\circ}\right)\right]=0 \\& \Rightarrow v_{2}^{2}=7 g l\end{aligned}$
So, the tension in the string is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955711/preptracker/questions/q_34006_explanation_1764955710871.png $T_{B}=\dfrac{m v_{2}^{2}}{l}-m g \cos 60^{\circ}$
$=m \dfrac{(7 g l)}{l}-0.5 m g=7 m g-0.5 m g=6.5 m g$

question: A stone of mass 1 $kg$ tied to a light inextensible string of length $L = 10/3\,m$ is whirling in a circular path of radius $L$ in a vertical plane. If the ratio of the maximum tension in the string to the minimum tension is 4 and if $g$ is taken to be $10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$, the speed of the stone at the highest point of the circle is
explanation:
Given that $m=1 \mathrm{~kg} \Rightarrow L=\dfrac{10}{3} m$
$\dfrac{T_{\max }}{T_{\min }}=4$
and $v_{L}=\sqrt{v_{H}^{2}+4 g L}$
where $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{L}}$ and $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{H}}$ are the velocities at the lowest and heighest points of the circle.
${T_{{\rm{min }}}} = \frac{{mv_H^2}}{K} - mg{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
${T_{\max }} = mv_L^2/L + mg = \frac{{m\left( {v_H^2 + 4gL} \right)}}{L} + mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From eq's (1) & (2)
$m\left[\dfrac{v_{H}^{2}+4 g L}{L}+g\right]=4 \times m\left(\dfrac{v_{H}^{2}}{L}-g\right)$
$\Rightarrow v_{H}=\sqrt{3 g L}=10 m s^{-1}$
subject: Physics

question: A 1- $kg$ stone at the end of 1 $m$ long string is whirled in a vertical circle at a constant speed of $4\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. The tension in the string is $6\,N$ when the stone is
explanation:
The centrifugal force on the sphere is
$\dfrac{m v^{2}}{R}=\dfrac{1 \times 4^{2}}{1}=16 \mathrm{~N}$
$m g=1 \times 10=10 N$
The tension in the string is
$\Rightarrow T=m g \cos \theta+\dfrac{m v^{2}}{R}$
$6=10 \cos \theta+16$
$\cos \theta=-1 \Rightarrow \theta=-180^{\circ}$

options.text: At the top of the circle
At the bottom of the circle

question: A heavy particle hanging from a string of length $l$ is projected horizontally with speed $\sqrt{g l}$. The speed of the particle at the point where the tension in the string equals weight of the particle is
explanation:
$T-m g \cos \theta=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{R}$
Given $\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{mg}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951192/preptracker/questions/q_33994_explanation_1764951192267.png $\begin{aligned}& m g-m g \cos \theta=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{R} \\& g(1-\cos \theta)=\dfrac{v^{2}}{R}\end{aligned}$
From conservation of energy
$\begin{aligned}& \left(\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} m u^{2}\right)+m g(R-R \cos \theta)=0 \\& \Rightarrow v^{2}-u^{2}=-2 g R(1-\cos \theta) \\& \Rightarrow v^{2}-(\sqrt{g l})^{2}=-2 v^{2} \\& 3 v^{2}=g l \Rightarrow v=\sqrt{\dfrac{g l}{3}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question:
A ball whirls around in a vertical circle at the end of a string. The other end of the string is fixed at the centre of circle. Assuming the total energy of the ball-Earth system remains constant. What is the difference of tension in string at bottom and top during circular motion
$
\left(T_{\text {bottom }}-T_{\text {top }}=\right.\text { ?) }
$
explanation:
Applying Newton's Second Law at the bottom (2) and top (t) of the circle gives
$T_{b}-m g=\dfrac{m v_{b}^{2}}{R} \text { and } T_{t}+m g=\dfrac{m v_{t}^{2}}{R}$
Adding these gives https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951166/preptracker/questions/q_33823_explanation_1764951165661.png $T_{b}=T_{t}+2 m g+\dfrac{m\left(v_{b}^{2}-v_{t}^{2}\right)}{R}$
From conservation of energy
$\dfrac{m\left(v_{b}^{2}-v_{t}^{2}\right)}{2}+(0-2 m g R)=0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{m\left(v_{b}^{2}-v_{t}^{2}\right)}{R}=4 m g$
Substituting into the above equation gives
$\begin{aligned}T_{b} & =T_{t}+6 m g \\\Rightarrow T_{b}-T_{t} & =6 m g\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question: Figure shows a smooth vertical circular track $A B$ of radius $R$. A block slides along the surface $AB$ when it is given a velocity equal to $\sqrt{6 g R}$ at point $A$. The ratio of the force exerted by the track on the block at point $A$ to that at point $B$ is
explanation:
The forces that act on the block are shown in the figure.
$\begin{aligned}& N_{A}+m g \cos 60^{\circ}=\dfrac{m v_{A}^{2}}{R} \\& N_{A}=\dfrac{m v_{A}^{2}}{R}-m g \cos 60^{\circ} \\& =6 m g-0.5 m g=5.5 m g\end{aligned}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951163/preptracker/questions/q_33820_explanation_1764951163243.png $\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{2} m v_{A}^{2}+m g R\left(1+\cos 60^{\circ}\right)=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{B}^{2} \\& v_{A}^{2}+2 g R\left(1+\dfrac{1}{2}\right)=v_{B}^{2} \\& 6 g R+3 g R=v_{B}^{2} \\& v_{B}^{2}=9 g R \Rightarrow v_{B}=\sqrt{9 g R} \\& N_{B}^{2}=m g+\dfrac{m v_{B}^{2}}{R}=10 m g\end{aligned}$
$\frac{{{N_A}}}{{{N_B}}} = 0.55$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question: A mass $m$ starting from $A$ reaches $B$ of a frictionless track. On reaching $B$, it pushes the track with a force equal to $\mathrm{x}$ times its weight, then the applicable relation is
explanation:
From conservation of mechanical energy
$K.{E_B} = P.{E_A} - P.{E_B}$
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = mgh - mg2r = mg(h - 2r)$
${v^2} = 2g(h - 2r)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Given that
$\frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} = xmg + mg$
$ \Rightarrow {v^2} = (x + 1)rg\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From Eqs (1) and (2), we get
$2 g(h-2 r)=(x+1) g r$
or $2 g h=(x+1) g r+4 g r=(x+5) g r$
$h=\left(\dfrac{x+5}{2}\right) r$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Vertical Circular Motion when the Circle Centre is at Rest

question: A small block slides with velocity $v_0=0.5 \sqrt{g r}$ on the horizontal frictionless surface as shown in the figure. The block leaves the surface at point $\mathrm{C}$. The angle $\theta$ in the figure is
explanation:
At point $\mathrm{C}: m g \cos \theta-N=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955686/preptracker/questions/q_33814_explanation_1764955686522.png (When block leaves the surface normal force becomes zero, so putting $N$=0) $ \Rightarrow g\cos \theta = \frac{{{v^2}}}{r}$
$\begin{gathered}v^{2}=v_{0}^{2}+2 g h, h=r-r \cos \theta \\g r \cos \theta=(0.5 \sqrt{g r})^{2}+2 g(r-r \cos \theta) \\\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\dfrac{1}{4}+2-2 \cos \theta \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\dfrac{3}{4}\end{gathered}$

question: With what minimum speed $v$ must a small ball should be pushed inside a smooth vertical tube from a height $h$ so that it may reach the top of the tube? Radius of the tube is $R$.
explanation:
Velocity of a ball to just reach the top of the tube should be given by;
$0=v^{2}-2 g h_{0}$
Where the velocity will be zero at topmost point.
Here $h_{0}=(2 R-h)$
Thus $v=\sqrt{2 g(2 R-h)}$

question:
A bob of mass \(m\) is suspended by a light string of length \(L\). It is imparted a horizontal velocity at the lowest point \(A\) such that it completes a circle in the vertical plane.
Match Column I with Column II
A ${v_A}$
B ${v_B}$
C ${v_C}$
D Ratio of kinetic energy of $A$ and $C$
P 5
Q $\sqrt {gL} $
R $\sqrt {5gL} $
S $\sqrt {3gL} $
explanation:
Total mechanical energy (E) of the system is conserved.
If we take potential energy of the system to be zero at the lowest point $A$, then
At $A,E = \frac{1}{2}mv_A^2\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
For Newton's second law
At $C,E = \frac{1}{2}mv_C^2 + mg(2L)\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
At the highest point $C$, to complete the circle tension in the string will be minimum (zero).
From Newton's second law $m g=\dfrac{m v_{C}^{2}}{L}$
$ \Rightarrow {v_C} = \sqrt {gL} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
From eq's (1), (2) and (3)
$v_{A}=\sqrt{5 g L}$
At $B, E=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{B}^{2}+m g(L)$
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{B}^{2}=E-m g(L)=\dfrac{5}{2} m g L-m g L$
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{B}^{2}=\dfrac{3}{2} m g L \Rightarrow v_{B}=\sqrt{3 g L}$
The ratio of kinetic energies at $A$ and $C$ is
$\therefore \dfrac{K_{A}}{K_{C}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{A}^{2}}{\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{C}^{2}}=\dfrac{5 g L}{g L}=\dfrac{5}{1}$
Option (3) is correct.

options.text: A - P,	B - Q,	C - S,	D - R
A - Q,	B - R,	C - P,	D - S
A - R,     B - S, 	  C - Q,	D - P
A - S,     B - P,	  C - R,	D - Q

question: A frictionless track $A B C D E$ ends in a circular loop of radius $R$. A body slides down the track from point $A$ which is at a height $h=5 \mathrm{~cm}$. Maximum value of $R$ for the body to successfully complete the loop is
explanation:
As the body moves on a circular track the body can complete vertical circular motion When the velocity at $D$ is $\sqrt{g R}$
From conservation of energy
$T \cdot {E_A} = T \cdot {E_D}mgh = mg(2R) + \frac{1}{2}m{(\sqrt {gR} )^2} \Rightarrow h = \frac{{5R}}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow R = \frac{{2h}}{5} = 2\,cm$
From the given options ${R_{\max }} = 2\,cm$

question: A stone tied to a string of length $L$ is whirled in a vertical circle with the other end of the string at the centre. At a certain instant of time, the stone at its lowest position has speed $u$. The magnitude of the change in its velocity as it reaches a position where the string is horizontal is
explanation:
In the case motion of stone is in vertical circle of radius $L$ and centre at $O$.
The change in velocity is
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta \vec{v}=v \hat{j}-u \hat{i} \\& |\Delta v|=\sqrt{(v)^{2}+(-u)^{2}}=\sqrt{v^{2}+u^{2}}\end{aligned}$
According to work-energy theorem
${W_g} = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} - \frac{1}{2}m{u^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$ - mgL = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} - \frac{1}{2}m{u^2}$
$\because \,{v^2} = {u^2} - 2gL$
$\therefore \,\left| {\Delta \,v} \right| = \sqrt {{v^2} + {u^2}} = \sqrt {2\left( {{u^2} - gL} \right)} $

question: A block of mass '$m$' with an initial kinetic energy '$E$' moves up an inclined plane of inclination '$\theta$'. If '$\mu$' is the coefficient of friction between the plane and the body, the work done against friction before coming to rest is
explanation:
Let before coming to rest body slides up to distance $s . h=s \sin \theta$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958002/preptracker/questions/q_108607_explanation_1764958002327.png From the work- energy theorem,
$W_{\text {net }}=\Delta W_{f}+\Delta W_{m g}=\Delta K$
$\Rightarrow-\mu m g(\cos \theta) s-m g s \sin \theta=0-E$
$\Rightarrow \mu m g s \cos \theta+m g s \sin \theta=E$
$\Rightarrow s=\dfrac{E}{m g(\mu \cos \theta+\sin \theta)}$
$\therefore$ Work one by friction,
$\Delta W_{f}=-\mu m g s \cos \theta=\dfrac{\mu E \cos \theta}{\mu \cos \theta+\sin \theta}$
$=\dfrac{\mu E \cos \theta}{\sin \theta+\mu \cos \theta}$
subject: Physics

question: A constant force of $(8 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}) N$ acting on a body of mass $2\,kg$ displaces the body from $(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})$ to $(4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}) m$. The work done in the process is
explanation:
$\vec{F}=(8 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}) N, m=2 k g$
$\vec{r}_{1}=(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}) m, \vec{r}_{2}=(4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}) m$
$\therefore$ Displacement, $\vec{s}=\vec{r}_{2}-\vec{r}_{1}$
$=(4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 k)-(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})$
$=(2 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k}) m$
$\therefore$ Work done, $W=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{s}$
$=(8 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k})$
$=(8)(2)+(-2)(-6)+(6)(10)=88 J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: Match the concepts related to work and force in Column I with their corresponding scientific descriptions in Column II. Choose the correct answer using the codes given below:
explanation:
A. The Work-Energy Theorem for a variable force is essentially an integral of the work done over a displacement or interval of time, which correlates to the change in kinetic energy. (A - S)
B. The change in kinetic energy is the product of mass and velocity, as well as the rate of change of velocity, which is a result derived from the time rate of change of kinetic energy. (B-R)
C. Resistive Force of air brings the pendulum to rest. (C- Q)
D. Newton's Second Law relates force and acceleration at any instant, which is a fundamental concept when discussing variable forces and their effects on objects. (D-P)
Correct option is (3).

question: A body of mass $3\,kg$ is moving under the action of a force which causes a displacement of $\left(\dfrac{t^{3}}{3}\right) m$, where $t$ is the time in seconds. The work done by the force in first 2 sec is
explanation:
Given, mass $m=3 {~kg}$, displacement
$(s)=\dfrac{t^{3}}{3} m$
Velocity, $v=\dfrac{d s}{d t}=\dfrac{3 t^{2}}{3} \Rightarrow t^{2} {~m} / {s}$
Acceleration, $(a)=\dfrac{d v}{d t}=2 t m / s^{2}$
Now, work done by the force
$W=\int_{0}^{2} F \cdot d s=\int_{0}^{2} m a \cdot d s$
$=\int_{0}^{2} 3 \times 2 t \times t^{2} d t=\int_{0}^{2} 6 t^{3} d t$
$ = \frac{3}{2}\left[ {{t^4}} \right]_0^2 = 24\,J$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: A ball of mass $1\,kg$ moves in a straight line with velocity $v=c x^{\alpha}$, where $c=1$ (SI unit) and $\alpha$ is a constant. If the work done by the net force during its displacement from $x=0$ to $x=4 m$ is $128 J$, then the value of $\alpha$ is
explanation:
Given mass $m=1 {~kg}$ and $c=1$
By work - energy theorem,
$W=\Delta K=K_{f}-K_{i}$
$\Rightarrow W=\dfrac{1}{2} m\left(c x^{\alpha}\right)^{2}-0=\dfrac{1}{2} m c^{2} x^{2 \alpha}$
$\Rightarrow W=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 1 \times 1 \times(4)^{2 \alpha} J$
$\therefore \dfrac{1}{2}(4)^{2 \alpha}=128$
$\therefore 2^{4 \alpha}=2^{8} \Rightarrow \alpha=2$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question:
${\rm{Statement }}\,{\rm{I}}$ The slope of kinetic energy displacement curve of a body in motion will be directly proportional to its acceleration.
${\rm{Statement }}\,{\rm{II}}$ From a height of $15 m$, a ball is projected vertically upwards with a velocity of $30 {~m} / {s}$. If the ball rises to the same height after hitting the ground, the loss of its energy on hitting the ground is $30 \%$.
${\rm{Statement }}\,{\rm{III}}$ The velocity acquired by a body of mass $m$ after travelling a fixed distance from rest under the action of a constant force is directly proportional to mass $m$. Which of the following is correct?
explanation:
Kinetic energy, $K=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
$\therefore$ Slope $=\dfrac{d K}{d x}=m \cdot v \dfrac{d v}{d x}=m a$
$\therefore$ Slope $\alpha$ a. So statement I is correct
Loss of energy, $\Delta E=E_{i}-E_{f}$
Now $\Delta E=\left(\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+m g h\right)-m g h=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
$\therefore \frac{{\Delta E}}{{{E_i}}} = \frac{{{v^2}}}{{{v^2} + 2gh}}$
$ = \frac{{{{30}^2}}}{{{{30}^2} + 2 \times 10 \times 15}} = 0.75 = 75\% $
So statement II is incorrect.
Also, for a body starting from rest, $K E=$ Work done
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=F x=m \cdot a \cdot x$
$v=\sqrt{2 a x}$ which is mass independent.
$\therefore$ Statement III is incorrect.
So, the correct option is (3).

options.text: Statements I, II and III are true.
Statements I, III are true but statement II is false.
Statement I is true but statements II and III and false.
Statements I, II are true but statement III is false.

question: If the average terminal velocity of rain drop is$2 m s^{-1}$, then the energy transferred by rain to each square metre of the surface at a place which receives $100\,cm$ of rain in a year is
explanation:
Given that, $v_{t}=2 {~m} / {s}, h=100 {~cm}=1 {~m}, A=1 {~m}^{2}$
Now, $V=A h=1 {~m}^{3}$
$\rho=10^{3} {~kg} {~m}^{-3}=$ density of water
$m=V \rho=10^{3} {~kg}=$ mass of water collected in one year by $1 {~m}^{2}$ area
Now, energy transferred by rain per unit area
$=\dfrac{\text { Kinetic energy }}{\text { Area }}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}}{1}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10^{3} \times(2)^{2}=2 \times 10^{3} {~J} / {m}^{2}$
Hence, energy transferred by rain to each square metre is $2 \times 10^{3} {~J}$.
So, the correct option is (3).

question: A horizontal force $\vec{F}=\left(g-x^{2}\right) \hat{i} N$ acts on a wooden block resting on a horizontal smooth surface. The work done to move the block from $x=0$ to $x=3 m$ (in $J$ ) is (Use $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
Given that, force acting on wooden block
$\vec{F}=\left(g-x^{2}\right) \hat{i} N$
Displacement, $d \vec{x}=d x \hat{i}$
Work done by $\vec{F}$ for displacement from $x=0$ to $x=3 m$
$W=\int_{0}^{3} \vec{F} \cdot d \vec{x}=\int_{0}^{3}\left(g-x^{2}\right) d x=\left[g x-\dfrac{x^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{3}$
$ = \left( {3g - \frac{{27}}{3}} \right) - 0 = 3 \times 10 - 9 = 30 - 9 = 21\,J$
So, the correct option is (4).

explanation:

question: A force $F=(2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}) N$ is applied on an object of mass $M$. What is the work done by this force in moving the object horizontally along the $X$-axis by $3 m$ ?
explanation:
Displacement of object is along $X$-axis by $3\,m$,
$\vec{x}=3 \hat{i} m$
Force $=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j} N$
Work done by force,
$W = \vec F \cdot \vec x = (2\hat i + 4\hat j) \cdot 3\hat i = 6\,J$
So, the correct option is (3).

question: A force of $4\,N$ acts on a $10\,kg$ body initially at rest. Let $W_{1}$ is the work done by the force during $0 \leq t \leq 1 s$. Likewise $W_{2}$ is the work done by the force during $1 s \leq t \leq 2 s$, where $t$ is time in second. The ratio $W_{2} / W_{1}$ is
explanation:
Given, mass of body $m=10 {~kg}$,
Force, $F=4 N$
Acceleration produced in the body due to applied force,
$a=\dfrac{F}{m}=\dfrac{4}{10}=0.4 {~ms}^{-2}$
For $0 \leq t \leq 1 s, t_{1}=1 s$
$\therefore$ Distance travelled by the body is given as
$s_{1}=u t_{1}+\dfrac{1}{2} a t_{1}^{2}=0 \times 1+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.4 \times 1^{2}=0.2 {~m}$
$\therefore$ Work done, $W_{1}=F \times s_{1}=4 \times 0.2=0.8 {~J}$
For $1 \leq t \leq 2 s, t_{2}=1 s$
Velocity of the body after $t_{1}=1 s$ is $v$, then$
v=0+a t_{1}=0+0.4 \times 1=0.4 {~ms}^{-1}$
Distance travelled by the body in this situation is given as,
$s_{2}=v t_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} a t_{2}^{2}=0.4 \times 1+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.4 \times 1^{2}=0.6 {~m}$
$\therefore$ Work done, $W_{2}=F s_{2}=4 \times 0.6=2.4 {~J}$
$\therefore \dfrac{W_{2}}{W_{1}}=\dfrac{2.4}{0.8}=\dfrac{3}{1}=3$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: An elevator of mass $500\,kg$ is ascending upward with a constant acceleration $a=2 {~m} / {s}^{2}$. What is the work done by the tension in the elevator cable during its climb by $12\,m$? (Take, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
Given $m_{e}=500 {~kg}, a_{e}=2 {~ms}^{-2}$ (upward)
When elevator is going upward, tension in the elevator cable,
$T=m_{e}(g+a)=500(10+2)=6000 N$
$\therefore$ Work done by the tension force in the cable,
$W=T \times s=6000 \times 12 \quad[\because$ given $s=12 {~m}]$
$=72000 {~J}=72 {~kJ}$
So, the correct option is (3).

question:
The block starts from rest as shown in the figure. Find the work done by a force of $10\,N$ and friction force in the time 0 to $4 s$.
[ Take, $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$]
explanation:
Here, applied force, $F=10 N$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952425/preptracker/questions/q_108491_explanation_1764952424859.png Friction force, $f=\mu N=0.2 \times m g$
$=0.2 \times 2 \times 10=4 {~N}$
Net force, $F_{\text {net }}=F-f=10-4=6 N$
$\therefore$ Acceleration, $a=\dfrac{F_{n e t}}{m}=\dfrac{6}{2}=3 {~ms}^{-2}$
Distance covered in $4 s$,
$s=0+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 3 \times(4)^{2}=24 m$
Work done by applied force of $10\,N$,
${W_1} = fs = 10 \times 24 = 240\,J$
Work done by friction force of $4 N$,
${W_2} = fs\cos 180^\circ = - 4 \times 24 = - 96\,J$
So, the correct option is (1).

question: Under the action of force, a $2\,kg$ body moves such that its position $x$ as function of time $t$ is given by $x=\alpha t^{2} / 2$, where $x$ is in metre, $t$ is in seconds and $\alpha=1 {~m} / {s}^{2}$. The work done by the force in the first two seconds is
explanation:
Given, $x=\dfrac{\alpha t^{2}}{2}$
As we know that,
$v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {\frac{{\alpha {t^2}}}{2}} \right) = \alpha t\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
According to work-energy theorem Work done, $W=K_{f}-K_{i}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m(\alpha t)^{2}$
[using Eq. (1)]
$ = \frac{1}{2}m{\alpha ^2}{t^2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times {(1)^2} \times {(2)^2} = 4\,J$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: A force acts on a $30\,gm$ particle in such a way that the position of the particle as a function of time is given by $x=\alpha t^{2}$, where $x$ is in metre, $t$ is in seconds and $\alpha=1 {~m} / {s}^{2}$. The work done during the first $4 s$ is
explanation:
Given, $m=30 {~g}=3 \times 10^{-2} {~kg}$
$x=\alpha t^{2}$
$\therefore v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=\dfrac{d}{d t} \alpha t^{2} \Rightarrow v=2 \alpha t$
At $t=4 s, v=2 \times 1 \times 4 {~m} / {s}=8 {~m} / {s}$
By work energy theorem,
$W=K_{f}-K_{i}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{30}{1000} \times 8^{2}-0$
$=0.96 {~J}$.
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: A particle of mass $m$ is moving along a circle of radius $R$ such that its tangential acceleration $a_{t}$ varies with distance covered $x$ as $a_{t}=\alpha x^{2}$ where $\alpha$ is a constant. The kinetic energy, $K$ of the particle varies with the distance as $K=\beta x^{c}$, where $\beta$ and $c$ are constants. The values of $\beta$ and $c$ are
explanation:
Given, $m_{\text {particle }}=m, a_{t}=\alpha x^{2}$ and kinetic energy, $K=\beta x^{c}$
As we know that, $v \dfrac{d v}{d x}=a_{t}$,
$v \dfrac{d v}{d x}=\alpha x^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{v} v d v=\alpha \int_{0}^{x} x^{2} d x$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{v^{2}}{2}=\dfrac{\alpha}{3} x^{3}$
$\therefore$ Kinetic energy, $K=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{m \alpha}{3} \cdot x^{3}$.
Now, from the above equation we get,
$\beta=\dfrac{m a}{3}$ and $c=3$
So, the correct option is (1).

question: A metal chain of mass $2\,kg$ and length $90\,cm$ over hangs a table with $60\,cm$ on the table. How much work needs to be done to put the hanging part of the chain back on the table?(Let $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
Given, mass of metal chain, $m=2 {~kg}$,
Total length of chain, $l=90 {~cm}=0.9 {~m}$,
Hanging length of chain, $l^{\prime}=(90-60) {cm}$
$=30 {~cm}=0.3 {~m}$
The distance of centre of gravity of hanging length from the table,
$l_{c}=\dfrac{l^{\prime}}{2}=\dfrac{0.3}{2}=0.15 {~m}$
Mass of the hanging part of chain,
$m^{\prime}=\dfrac{0.30}{0.90} \times 2 \Rightarrow m^{\prime}=\dfrac{2}{3} {~kg}$
$\therefore$ Work needed to put hanging part of the chain back on the table,
$W=m^{\prime} g l_{c}=\dfrac{2}{3} \times 10 \times 0.15=1 J$
$\left(\because g=10 m / s^{2}\right)$
So, the correct option is (3).

question: A mechanical system consists two springs of stiffness coefficients $k_{1}$ and $k_{2}$ are connected in series. The minimum work to be performed on the system to stretch it by $\Delta l$ is
explanation:
$\left[K_{\text {eq }}\right]$ in series $=\dfrac{k_{1} k_{2}}{k_{1}+k_{2}}$
Work done to stretch springs with elongation $\Delta l$ is
$W=\dfrac{1}{2} K_{e q} \cdot \Delta l^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{k_{1} k_{2}}{k_{1}+k_{2}}\right) \cdot \Delta l^{2}$
So, the correct option is (1).

question: A particle of mass $m {~kg}$ moves along the $X$ axis with its velocity varying with the distance travelled as $v=k x^{\beta}$, where $k$ is a positive constant. The total work done by all the forces during displacement of the particle from $x=0$ to $x=d$ is close to
explanation:
Velocity, $v=k x^{\beta}$
By work-energy theorem,
Net work done $=\Delta K E$
$=(K E)_{f}-(K E)_{i}$.
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{f}^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{i}^{2}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m\left(k d^{\beta}\right)^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} m(k \times 0)^{2}=\dfrac{m k^{2}}{2} \cdot d^{2 \beta}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: The kinetic energies of two similar cars $A$ and $B$ are $100\,J$ and $225\,J$ respectively. On applying breaks, car A stops after $1000\,m$ and car $B$ stops after $1500\,m$. If $F_{A}$ and $F_{B}$ are the forces applied by the breaks on cars and respectively, then the ratio of $\dfrac{F_{A}}{F_{B}}$ is
explanation:
By work-energy theorem,
$ \Rightarrow - FS = {K_f} - {K_i}$
$ \Rightarrow FS = {K_i} - {K_f} = K\left( {{K_f} = 0} \right)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{F_{A}}{F_{B}}=\dfrac{K_{A}}{K_{B}} \times \dfrac{S_{B}}{S_{A}}=\dfrac{100}{225} \times \dfrac{1500}{1000}=\dfrac{2}{3}$
subject: Physics

question: A body of mass $0.25\,kg$ travels along a straight line from $x=0$ to $x=2 m$ with a speed $v=k x^{3 / 2}$ where $k=2\,S I$ units. The work done by the net force during this displacement is
explanation:
At $x=0, v_{i}=2 \times 0^{3 / 2}=0$
At $x=2 m, v_{f}=2 \times 2^{3 / 2}=2^{5 / 2} m / s$
Using work energy theorem, $W=K_{f}-K_{i}$$\Rightarrow W=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{f}^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{i}^{2}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.25 \times\left(2^{5 / 2}\right)^{2}-0=4 J$
