question: There is force field $\vec{F}=x^{3} y \hat{i}+y^{2} \hat{j}$ in which a particle moves along the line $x=y$. Find work done by the force as the particle moves from $(0,0)$ to $(2,2)$
explanation:
Given that $\vec{F}=x^{3} y \hat{i}+y^{2} \hat{j}$
Displacement $d \vec{s}=d \times \hat{i}+d y \hat{j}$
Work done $W=\int \vec{F} \cdot d \vec{s}=\int_{0}^{2} F_{x} d x+\int_{0}^{2} F_{y} d y$
\begin{equation*}=\int_{0}^{2} x^{3} y d x+\int_{0}^{2} y^{2} d y\end{equation*}
For the path $x=y$ the work done is
$W = \int_0^2 {{x^4}} dx + \int_0^2 {{y^2}} dy$
$ = \left( {\frac{{{x^5}}}{5}} \right)_0^2 + \left( {\frac{{{y^3}}}{3}} \right)_0^2 = \frac{{{2^5}}}{5} + \frac{{{2^3}}}{3}$
$ = \frac{{32}}{5} + \frac{8}{3} = \frac{{96 + 40}}{{15}} = \frac{{136}}{{15}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: The displacement of a particle moving under the action of a force $\vec{F}=2 \hat{i}+b \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ is $\vec{d}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$. Find the value of $b$ if the work done by the force is zero.
explanation:
Work $=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{s}=(2 \hat{i}+b \hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})$
$=2+b+1=3+b=0 \Rightarrow b=-3$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: A particle of mass $m$ moves on a straight line with its velocity increasing with distance according to the equation $v=\alpha \sqrt{x}$, where $\alpha$ is a constant. The total work done by all the forces applied on the particle during its displacement from $x=0$ to $x=d$, will be
explanation:
$v=\alpha \sqrt{x}$
At $x=0, v_{i}=0$ and at $x=d, v_{f}=\alpha \sqrt{d}$
According to work-energy theorem,
$W=\Delta K E$
$\Rightarrow W=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{f}^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{i}^{2}$
or $\Rightarrow W=\dfrac{1}{2} m \alpha^{2} d-0=\dfrac{m \alpha^{2} d}{2}$
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question:
A block of mass $1 \,kg$ is pushed up a surface inclined to horizontal at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ by a force of $10\, N$ parallel to the inclined surface as shown in figure. When the block is pushed up by $10\, m$ along inclined surface, the work done against frictional force is
[$g = 10\,m/{s^2}$]
explanation:
As $f_{k}=\mu_{k} N$
$\therefore f_{k}=\mu_{k} m g \cos \theta$
$f_{k}=(0.1)(1)(10) \cos 60^{\circ}$
${f_k} = 0.5\,N$
Work done against frictional force is,
$W=f_{k} s \cos 180^{\circ}=0.5 \times 10 \times(-1)$
$W = - 5\,J.$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951657/preptracker/questions/q_36215_explanation_1764951657240.png
So correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: A block of mass $100\,kg$ slides over a distance of $10 \,m$ on a horizontal surface. If the coefficient of friction between the surfaces is 0.4 then the work done against friction (in $J$ ) is
explanation:
Work done against friction $=-$ Work done by friction
$\therefore \,\,\,\,W = {f_k}s = {\mu _k}Ns$
$ = {\mu _k}mgs$
$ = 0.4 \times 100 \times 10 \times 10 = 4000\,J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: One end of a spring of force constant $k_{1}$ is attached to the ceiling of an elevator. A block of mass 1.5 $kg$ is attached to the other end. Another spring of force constant $k_{2}$ is attached to the bottom of the mass and to the floor of the elevator as shown in figure. At equilibrium, the deformation in both the springs is equal and is 40 $cm$ . If the elevator moves with constant acceleration upward,the additional deformation in both the spring is 8 $cm$ . Find the elevator's acceleration $(g = 10\,\,m{s^{ - 2}})$.
explanation:
At equilibrium, $\left(k_{1}+k_{2}\right) x=m g$
$ \Rightarrow {k_1} + {k_2} = \frac{{mg}}{x}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\left( {x = 40\,cm,{x_1} = 48\,cm} \right){\rm{ }}$ In case of constant acceleration,$\begin{equation*}\left(k_{1}+k_{2}\right) x_{1}-m g=m a \tag{2}\end{equation*}$From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get $a=2 {~ms}^{-2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work Energy Theorem with respect to a Moving Frame

question: In figure, shown all the surfaces are frictionless, and mass of the block is $m=100 {~g}$. The block and the wedge are held initially at rest. Now the wedge is given a horizontal acceleration of $10 {~m} {~s}^{-2}$ by applying a force on the wedge, so that the block does not slip on the wedge. Then find the work done in joules by the normal force in ground frame on the block in 1 $s$ .
explanation:
If block does not slip then, $a=g \tan \theta$
$\Rightarrow \quad 10=10 \tan \theta \Rightarrow \theta=45^{\circ}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955939/preptracker/questions/q_36131_explanation_1764955938982.png $N\sin \theta = ma \Rightarrow N \times \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} = 0.1 \times 10$
$ \Rightarrow N = \sqrt 2 \,N$
$S=\dfrac{1}{2} \times a t^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10(1)^{2}=5 {~m}$
$W_{N}=N S \cos \theta=\sqrt{2} \times 5 \times 1 / \sqrt{2}=5 {~J}$
Alternatively: $W_{N}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2}(0.1)(a t)^{2}=5 {~J}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: Four packages each having a mass of 4 $kg$ are attached on the belt at equal distances $d=200 {~mm}$ as shown in the figure. Initially belt is at rest. If a constant force of magnitude 840 $N$ is applied to the belt, determine the velocity of package 2 (in ${m} / {s}$ ) as it falls off the belt at point $A$. Assume that the mass of the belt and pulleys is small as compared with the mass of the packages. Assume that the radius of pulley is negligible in comparison to width $d$.
explanation:
$F d=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times v^{2} \times 4 \Rightarrow v^{2}=\dfrac{F d}{8}$
$Fd = \frac{1}{2} \times 3\left( {v_1^2 - {v^2}} \right) \times 4v_1^2$
$ = \frac{{Fd}}{6} + \frac{{Fd}}{8} = \frac{{7Fd}}{{24}}$
$ = \frac{{7 \times F \times d}}{{24}} = \frac{{7 \times 840 \times 0.2}}{{24}}$
$ = 49 \Rightarrow {v_1} = 7\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: Two beads connected by a light inextensible string are placed over fixed vertical rings as shown in figure. The mass of each bead is $100\,g$ and all surface are frictionless. Find  the tension in the string   just after the beads are released from the shown position is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951622/preptracker/questions/q_36028_explanation_1764951621798.png ${N_{1}=\dfrac{T}{\sqrt{2}}+m g \dfrac{T}{\sqrt{2}}=m a}$
${\dfrac{T}{\sqrt{2}}=N_{2} m g-\dfrac{T}{\sqrt{2}}=m a}$
${m g-\dfrac{T}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{T}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow m g=T \sqrt{2}}$
${T=\dfrac{m g}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{\left(10^{-1}\right)(10)}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} {~N}=0.707 {~N}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Miscellaneous Problems on Work, Energy and Power
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Work, Energy and Power

question: Two blocks with masses ${m_{1}=3 {~kg}}$ and ${m_{2}=5 {~kg}}$ are connected by a light string that slides over a frictionless pulley as shown in figure. Initially, ${m_{2}}$, is held $5\,m$ off the floor while ${m_{1}}$ is on the floor. The system is then released. At what speed  does ${m_{2}}$ hit the floor?
explanation:
The initial and final configurations are shown in the figure. It is convenient to set ${U=0}$ at the floor. Initially, only ${m_{2}}$ has potential energy. As it falls, it loses potential energy and gains kinetic energy. At the same time, ${m_{1}}$ gains potential energy and kinetic energy. Just before ${m_{2}}$ lands, it has only kinetic energy. Let ${v}$ the final speed of each mass. Then, using the law of conservation of mechanical energy. Final (Just before ${m_{2}}$ strikes the floor)
$\begin{aligned}& K_{f}+U_{f}=K_{i}+U_{i} \\& \dfrac{1}{2}\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) v^{2}+m_{1} g h=0+m_{2} g h\end{aligned}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951614/preptracker/questions/q_36008_explanation_1764951614198.png ${v^{2}=\dfrac{2\left(m_{2}-m_{1}\right) g h}{m_{1}+m_{2}}}$
Putting ${m_{1}=3 {~kg} ; m_{2}=5 {~kg} ; h=5 {~m}}$ and ${g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$
we get ${v^{2}=\dfrac{2(5-3)(10)(5)}{5+3}}$ or ${v=5 {~m} / {s}}$
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: For the situation shown in the given figure, the horizontal surface is rough. The coefficient of friction between the block of mass ${m_{1}=4 {~kg}}$ and the horizontal surface is ${\mu=0.5}$. The blocks are connected with combination of light spring of spring constant ${k}$ and a light string. The block of mass ${m_{2}}$ is released from rest, when spring is in its natural length. Find minimum value of ${m_{2}}$  such that the block of mass ${m_{1}}$ will start moving.
explanation:
Let for just shifting of the block of mass ${m_{1}}$, the elongation in spring is ${x_{1}}$
${\therefore \quad k x_{1}>\mu m_{1} g}$
${\because \quad x_{1 \text { min }}=\dfrac{\mu m_{1} g}{k}}$
For minimum value of ${m_{2}}$, the speed of block of mass ${m_{2}}$ is zero when the elongation in spring is ${x_{1 \text { min }}}$
According to the conservation principle of mechanical energy, Loss in gravitational potential energy of the block mass ${m_{2}}$ is equal to gain in elastic potential energy in the spring.
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & m_{2} g x_{1 \text { min }}=\dfrac{1}{2} k x_{\min }^{2} \\\therefore & x_{1 \text { min }}=\dfrac{2 m_{2} g}{k} \\\text { Or } & \dfrac{\mu m_{1} g}{k}=\dfrac{2 m_{2} g}{k} \\& m_{2}=\dfrac{\mu m_{1}}{2}=\dfrac{0.5 \times 4}{2}=1 {~kg}\end{array}$
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: For the situation shown in the given figure, block ${A}$ of mass $10\,kg$ is at the ground. The block ${B}$ is released from rest when the light spring is unstretched. The minimum mass of the block ${B}$ in $kg$ such that block ${B}$ may leave the contact of ground is
explanation:
When the block ${A}$ just starts to leave the surface, then. ${k x_{0}=M g}$
For minimum value of ${m, x_{0}}$ should be maximum elongation in the spring. At the instant of maximum elongation, the velocity of block ${B}$ will be zero.
${\therefore}$ Loss in gravitational potential energy of block ${B=}$ Gain in elastic potential energy by spring ${\therefore \quad m g x_{0}=\dfrac{1}{2} k x_{0}^{2}}$
${\therefore \quad x_{0}=\dfrac{2 m g}{k}}$ or ${\dfrac{M g}{k}=\dfrac{2 m g}{k} \Rightarrow m=\dfrac{M}{2}=5 {~kg}}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question:
A bullet of mass $100\,g$ moving horizontally with a velocity $210\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ gets embedded in a block of mass $2\,kg$ kept on a rough horizontal surface. If the coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and surface is $0.5.$ Calculate the distance block - bullet system will move before coming to rest.
(Take acceleration due to gravity $ = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$).
explanation:
Let mass of bullet be m and mass of block be $M$.
After collision, the block-bullet system will move with velocity $\mathrm{v}_{\text {sys }}$
According to conservation of linear momentum,
$mv = (m + M)\,\,{v_{sys}}$
$\therefore 0.1 \times 210 = 2.10 \times {v_{sys{\rm{ }}}}$
$\therefore \quad {v_{sys}} = 10\,m/s$
Also, K.E. acquired by system is converted to work done in moving the system, i.e., according to work-energy theorem,
$\frac{1}{2}(\,m + M){\left( {{v_{sys{\rm{ }}}}} \right)^2} = F \times s$
$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2}(\,m + M){(10)^2} = \mu (m + M)g \times s$
$\therefore s = \frac{{100}}{{2 \times 0.5 \times 10}} = 10\,m$
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question:
Consider an elliptically shaped rail $PQ$ in the vertical plane with $OP = 6\,m$ and $OQ = 8\,m$. A block of mass $1\,kg$ is pulled along the rail from $P$ to $Q$ with a force of $15\,N,$ which is always parallel to line $PQ$ (see the figure given).
Assuming no frictional losses, the kinetic energy of the block when it reaches $Q$ is $(n \times 10)$ joules.
The value of $n$ is
(Take acceleration due to gravity $ = 10\,m{{\rm{s}}^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
Using work energy theorem
${W_{mg}} + {W_F} = \Delta K\,.E.$
$ - mgh + Fd = \Delta K.E$
$\therefore-1 \times 10 \times 8+15(10)=\Delta K E$
$\therefore \quad \Delta K . E .=70$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=7$
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: A body of  $2\,kg$ mass is initially moving along a straight line with a velocity of $1.5\,m{\rm{/}}s$. A force of $4\,N$ is applied to it at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ to the initial direction of motion. The change in its $K.E.$ after the first second is
explanation:
$a = \frac{{F\cos \theta }}{m} = \frac{{4\cos 60^\circ }}{2} = 1\,m/{s^2}$
$v = u + at = 1.5 + 1 \times 1 = 2.5\,m/{s^2}$
Change in $K.E.$ $ = \frac{1}{2}\,m\left( {{v^2} - {u^2}} \right) = \frac{1}{2} \times 2\left( {{{2.5}^2} - {{1.5}^2}} \right)$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,K.E. = 4\,J$
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: A block of mass $0.24\,kg$ is attached to a spring of force constant $4\,N{\rm{/}}m.$ The coefficient of friction between the block and the floor is $0.2.$ Initially the block is at rest and the spring is unstretched. An impulse is given to the block as shown in the figure. The block slides a distance of $0.18\,m$ and comes to rest for the first time. The initial velocity of the block in $m{\rm{/}}s$ is $v = {\left( {\frac{N}{{100}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}$ then, $N$ is   (Take $g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$ )
explanation:
Decrease in mechanical energy $ = $ Work done against friction
$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} - \frac{1}{2}k{x^2} = \mu \,\,mgx$
$\therefore v = \sqrt {\frac{{2\mu \,\,mgx + k{x^2}}}{{\,m}}} $
$\therefore v = \sqrt {\frac{{2 \times 0.2 \times 0.24 \times 10 \times (0.18) + 4{{(0.18)}^2}}}{{0.24}}} $
$\therefore \quad v = \sqrt {\frac{{63}}{{50}}} $
$\therefore v = \sqrt {\frac{{126}}{{100}}} \,m/s\quad \Rightarrow N = 126$
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: A body is acted upon by a force which is inversely proportional to the distance covered. The work done will be proportional to ( ${S=}$ distance covered)
explanation:
Force ${\propto \dfrac{1}{\text { Distance }}}$
$F=\dfrac{k}{S} \quad[k=\text { constant }]$
Work done in moving a particle by small distance ${d s}$ is
$d W=F d S \Rightarrow d W=\dfrac{k}{S} d S$
Total work done will be, ${\int d W=\int \dfrac{k}{S} d S}$$W=k \ln (S)$.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: A body of mass 5 kg falls from a height of 30 $m$ . If whole of its mechanical energy is changed into heat, then heat produced will be
explanation:
Here, ${m=5 {~kg}, h=30 {~m}}$
Mechanical energy ${(E)}$ of the body is$E = mgh = 5 \times 9.8 \times 30\,J$
$E = {\text{ Heat produced }}(H) = 5 \times 9.8 \times 30\,J$
$ = \frac{{5 \times 9.8 \times 30}}{{4.2}}cal$
$(1cal = 4.2\,J)$
$ = 350cal$.
So correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: A block of mass $10\,kg$ is moving in $x$-direction with a constant speed of $10\,m\,{s^{ - 1}}$. It is subjected to a retarding force ${F_r} =  - 0.1x\,J\,{m^{ - 1}}$ during its travel from $x = 20\,m$ to $x = 30\,m$. Its final kinetic energy will be
explanation:
Here,$m = 10\,kg,{v_i} = 10\,m\,{s^{ - 1}}$
Initial kinetic energy of the block is
${K_i} = \frac{1}{2}mv_i^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times (10kg) \times {\left( {10m{s^{ - 1}}} \right)^2}$$ = 500J$
Work done by retarding force
$W = \int {{F_r}} dx = \int_{20}^{30} - 0.1\,xdx = - 0.1\left[ {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2}} \right]_{20}^{30}$
$ = - 0.1\left[ {\frac{{900 - 400}}{2}} \right] = - 25J$
According to work-energy theorem
$W = {K_f} - {K_i} \Rightarrow {K_f} = W + {K_i}$
$ = - 25J + 500J = 475J$
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: A chain of mass $M$ is placed on a smooth table with $1 / n$ of its length $L$ hanging over the edge. The work done in pulling the hanging portion of the chain back to the surface of the table is
explanation:
$W = $ change in $PE$ of $COM$ of hanging part
$ = \frac{M}{n}g\frac{L}{{2n}} = \frac{{MgL}}{{2{n^2}}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: A body of mass $10\,kg$ initially at rest acquires velocity $10\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$ What is the work done?
explanation:
Given, $m = 10\,kg,\,\,u = 0$
and $v = 10\,\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
By work-energy theorem,
$W = \Delta \,K = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times {(10)^2} - 0 = 500\,J$

question: A $10\,kg$ mass moves $3.0\,m$ against a retarding force shown in the figure. If the force is zero at the beginning, then how much kinetic energy is changed?
explanation:
According to the work-energy theorem,
Loss in kinetic energy $=$ Work done
$=$ Area of $F-S$ curve
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \times$ base $\times$ perpendicular
$=-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 4=-6 J$
subject: Physics

question: A skier starts from rest at point $A$ and slides down the hill without turning or breaking. The friction coefficient is $\mu$. When he stops at point $B$, his horizontal displacement is $s$. What is the height difference between points $A$ and $B$ ? (The velocity of the skier is small, so that the additional pressure on the snow due to the curvature can be neglected. Neglect also the friction of air and the dependence of $\mu$ on the velocity of the skier.)
explanation:
According to question, the situation is shown in the figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951546/preptracker/questions/q_35600_explanation_1764951546153.png For a sufficiently, safe-horizontal displacement $\Delta s$ can be considered straight. If the corresponding length of path element is $\Delta L$, the friction force is given by $\Delta F=\mu m g(\Delta L \cos \theta)$
From, $\Delta \,OPQ$
$\cos \theta=\dfrac{\Delta s}{\Delta L}$
So, $\Delta F=\mu m g \Delta L\left(\dfrac{\Delta s}{\Delta L}\right)=\mu m g \Delta s$
Adding up, we find that along the whole path, the total work done by the friction force is $\mu m g s$. By energy conservation, this must equals to the decrease $m g h$ in potential energy of skier.
$\therefore \mu m g s=m g h$
Hence, $h=\mu s$
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: The rate of decrease of kinetic energy is  $9.6\,J{s^{ - 1}}.$ Find the magnitude of force acting on particle when its speed is  $3\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$
explanation:
Given, rate of decrease in kinetic energy,$\dfrac{d K}{d t}=9.6 J^{-1}$
When speed, $v = 3\,m{s^{ - 1}},$ then force $F=$ ?
Kinetic energy, $K=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
Differentiating with respect to $t$, we get
$\frac{{dK}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{2}\,\,\,m2v\,\,\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = vm\frac{{dv}}{{dt}}$
$ = vm\,\,\frac{{dp}}{{dt}}$
$\frac{{dK}}{{dt}} = vF\,\,\,\left[ {\because {\text{ force}},{\text{ }}F = m\frac{{dp}}{{dt}}} \right]$
$\therefore \quad F = \frac{1}{v} \cdot \frac{{dK}}{{dt}}$
$ = \frac{1}{3} \times 9.6$
$ = 3.2\,N$
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: The work done by applied variable force, $F=x+x^{3}$ from $x=0 m$ to $x=2 m$, where $x$ is displacement, is
explanation:
Given, $F=x+x^{3}$
and $x=0$ to $x=2$
We know that, work done, $W=\int F d x$
$\Rightarrow \quad W=\int_{0}^{2}\left(x+x^{3}\right) d x$
$\Rightarrow W=\left[\dfrac{x^{2}}{2}+\dfrac{x^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{2}$
$W=\left[\dfrac{4}{2}+\dfrac{16}{4}\right]$
$W=6 J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: A force $F=-(y \hat{i}+x \hat{j})$ acts on a particle moving in the $X-Y$ plane. Starting from the origin, the particle is taken along the positive $X$-axis to the point $(2 a, 0)$ and then parallel to the $Y$-axis to the point $(2 a, 2 a)$. The total work done on the particle is
explanation:
We know that, work done $=F \cdot s$
Work done is independent of path followed,
$F = [y\hat i + x\hat j]$
$s = [2a,2a] - [0,0]$
$ = (2a - 0),(2a - 0) = (2a,2a)$
$F = - [y\hat i - x\hat j] = 2a\hat i + 2a\hat j]$
$F \cdot s = - [2a\hat i + 2a\hat j] \cdot [2a\hat i + 2a\hat j]$
$ = - \sqrt {{{\left( {4{a^2}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {4{a^2}} \right)}^2}} = - 4{a^2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: A person is holding a bucket by applying a force of  $10\,N.$  He moves a horizontal distance of $5\,m$ and then climbs up a vertical distance of $10\,m.$ Find the total work done by him.
explanation:
Given $F=10 N, s=5 m$
and $\theta=90^{\circ}$ (for horizontal motion)
$\therefore$ Work done, $W_{1}=F_{S} \cos \theta$
$=10 \times 5 \times \cos 90^{\circ}=0$
For vertical, motion, $\theta = 0^\circ ,$ $s = 10\,m$
$\therefore$ Work done, $W_{2}=F s \cos \theta$
$ = 10 \times 10 \times \cos 0^\circ = 100\,J$
$\therefore$ Total work done,
$W = {W_1} + {W_2} = (0 + 100)J$
$ = 100\,J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: A particle is displaced from a position $(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})$ another position $(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k})$ under the action of force $(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k})$. The work done by the force in an arbitrary unit is
explanation:
Work done, $W=F \cdot r$
$ = (2\hat i + \hat j - \hat k) \cdot [(3\hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k)$
$ - (2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k)]({\rm{ given }})$
$ = (2\hat i + \hat j - \hat k) \cdot (\hat i + 3\hat j - 3\hat k)$
$W = 2 + 3 + 3 = 8{\rm{ unit }}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: In the given figure, ball ${A}$ is released from rest when the spring is at its natural (unstretched) length. For the block ${B}$ of mass ${M}$ to leave contact with the ground at some stage, the minimum mass of ${A}$ must be
explanation:
The spring will exert the maximum force when the ball is at its lowest position. If the ball has descended through a distance ${x}$ to reach this position,
$mgx = \frac{1}{2}k{x^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
where ${k}$ is the force constant of the spring. The block ${B}$ to leave contact, spring force
$ = kx = Mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$.
From equation (1) and (2), we get ${m=M / 2}$.

options.text: a function of ${M}$ and the force constant of the spring.

subject: Physics

explanation:
Change in velocity
${=\dfrac{\text { Area under the } F-T \text { graph }}{\text { Mass }}}$
${=\dfrac{40+(-10)}{5}=6 {~m} / {s}}$
${W_{F}=\Delta}$${\rm{ }}K.E.$ ${=\dfrac{1}{2} \times(5) \times(6)^{2}=90 {~J}}$

question: The velocity-time graph of a particle, moving in a straight line, is shown in the figure. The mass of the particle is $2\,kg$ . The work done by all the forces acting on the particle in time interval between ${t=0 s}$ to ${t=10 s}$ is
explanation:
From work-energy theorem,
${W=\Delta K \cdot E}$
$ = {K_f} - {K_i}$
$ = \frac{1}{2}m\left( {v_f^2 - v_i^2} \right)$
${=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 2(400-100)=300 J}$

question: The acceleration of a particle that moves along the positive ${x}$-axis varies with its position, as shown in the figure. If the velocity of the particle is ${0.8 {~m} / {s}}$ at ${x=0}$, then the velocity of the particle at ${x=1.4}$ is (in ${{m} / {s}}$ )
explanation:
Area under the ${F-x}$ curve gives work done. Change in ${K . E .=}$ work done
${\dfrac{1}{2}\left(v^{2}-u^{2}\right)=0.4 \Rightarrow v^{2}-(0.8)^{2}=0.8}$
${\Rightarrow v^{2}=1.44 {~m} / {s}, v=1.2 {~m} / {s}}$

question: A body of mass 200 g is moving along ${x-y}$ plane. Work done by the force ${\vec{F}=(2 x \hat{i}+y \hat{j})}$, when it displaces a body ${(0,0)}$ to ${(1,2)}$ is
explanation:
$W=\int_{(0,0)}^{(1,2)}(2 x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}) \cdot(d x \hat{i}+d y \hat{j})$
${\left[\because W=\int_{x_{1}}^{x_{2}} F . d s\right]}$
${\int_{0}^{1} 2 x d x+\int_{0}^{2} y d y}$
${=\left[2 \dfrac{x^{2}}{2}\right]+\left[\dfrac{y^{2}}{2}\right]=3}$ units

explanation:
${a=\dfrac{20}{2}=10 {~ms}^{-2}}$
${S=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}=20 m}$
${\vec{F}=m a=-20 {~N}}$
[Final velocity becomes zero, i.e., ${a}$ is $ - ve$]
${W D=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{S}=-400 J}$

question: A particle of mass $1\,kg$ is moving along ${x}$-axis, and a force ${F}$ is also acting along ${x}$-axis in a way that its displacement is varying as ${x=3 t^{2}}$. Find the work done by force when it covers a distance of ${2 m}$
explanation:
${x=3 t^{2}}$
${v=\dfrac{d x}{d t} \Rightarrow v=6 {tm} / {s}}$
Acceleration ${=\dfrac{d v}{d t}=6 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951533/preptracker/questions/q_35489_explanation_1764951533225.png Force acting on particle ${=m a=1 \times 6=6 N}$
Displacement ${=2 m}$
Work done ${=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{r}=F r \cos \theta\left[\because \theta=0^{\circ}\right]}$
${=6 \times 2=12 J}$

question: A particle is moved from a position ${\vec{r}_{1}=(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}) m}$ to a position ${\vec{r}_{2}=(14 \hat{i}+13 \hat{j}-9 \hat{k})}$ metre under the action of a force ${\vec{F}=(4 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) N}$. The workdone is equal to
explanation:
${W=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{s}}$
We know that,
${\vec{s}=\vec{r}_{2}-\vec{r}_{1}}$
${\vec{s}=(11 \hat{i}+11 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})}$
${W=(4 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \cdot(11 \hat{i}+11 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})}$
${=44+11-9}$
${=46 J}$

question:
Identify the correct statement from the following:
I. Work done by a man in lifting a bucket out of a well by means of a rope tied to the bucket is negative.
II. Work done by gravitational force in lifting a bucket out of a well by a rope tied to the bucket is negative.
III. Work done by friction on a body sliding down an inclined plane is positive.
IV. Work done by an applied force on a body moving on a rough horizontal plane with uniform velocity is zero.
V. Work done by the air resistance on an oscillating pendulum is negative.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
explanation:
I: False, it is positive as $\theta=0^{\circ}$.
II : True, it is negative as $\theta=180^{\circ}$
III : False, it is negative. as $\theta=180^{\circ}$
IV: False, it is non zero.
V: True, work done by resistive force is negative. as $\theta=180^{\circ}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951520/preptracker/questions/q_35409_explanation_1764951520059.png

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
A truck and a car moving with same kinetic energy are brought to rest by applying breaks which provides equal retarding forces. Both come to rest in equal distance.
A car moving towards east takes a turn and moves towards north, the speed remains unchanged. The acceleration of the car is zero.
explanation:
$K \cdot E=\dfrac{1}{2} m V_{m}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} M V_{M}^{2}$
Where $m=$ Velocity of car
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,M = $ Velocity of truck.
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{V_m} = $ Velocity of car
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{V_M} = $ Velocity of truck.
$\therefore V_{m}^{2}=\dfrac{M}{m} V_{M}^{2}$
Now for the car, $\therefore V_{m}^{2}=2 \cdot a_{m} \cdot S_{m}$
$\Rightarrow S_{m}=\dfrac{V_{m}^{2}}{2 a_{m}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{M}{m} V_{m}^{2}}{2 \dfrac{F}{m}}=\dfrac{M V_{m}^{2}}{2 F}$.
For the truck,
$S_{M}=\dfrac{V_{M}^{2}}{2 a_{M}}=\dfrac{V_{M}^{2}}{2 \dfrac{F}{M}}=\dfrac{M V_{M}^{2}}{2 F}=\dfrac{m V_{m}^{2}}{2 F}$
$\therefore S_{m}=S_{M}$.
Initial velocity of the car $\vec{V}_{i}=V \hat{i}$ and final velocity $\vec{V}_{f}=V \hat{j}$. As velocity changes, acceleration of the car cannot be zero. So statement (4) is correct.

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are correct.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.
Both Statement A and Statement B are incorrect.
Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.

question:
Assertion: For two bodies, the sum of the mutual forces exerted between them is zero from Newton's third law

Reason: The sum of work done by the two forces must always cancel.
explanation: For two bodies, the sum of the mutual forces exerted between them is zero, following Newton's third law. However, the sum of the work done by these two forces may not always cancel out. While sometimes true, it may not hold in all cases. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Work done in uniform circular motion is zero.

Reason: Force is always directed along displacement.
explanation:
In uniform circular motion, the work done is indeed zero. However, the reason provided is false. In uniform circular motion, the force is directed towards the center of the circle (centripetal force), but the displacement is perpendicular to the force.
So correct option is (3)

question:

Reason: Force and displacement are perpendicular to each other.
explanation: Since centripetal force acts perpendicular to the displacement of the body, the work done by it is zero. This is because $\vec{v}$ is tangential and $\vec{S}$ shall be taken along $\vec{v}$. Angle between $F$ (centripetal) and $\vec{S}$ is zero $\Rightarrow W=0$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955873/preptracker/questions/q_35294_explanation_1764955873238.png So correct option is (1)

question:
Assertion: Soft steel can be made red hot by continued hammering on it, but hard steel cannot.

Reason: Energy transfer in case of soft iron is larger than in hard steel.
explanation: Hammer can do much more positive work in 'soft steel' as more deformation is possible by 'softness of steel' when compared to 'hardness of steel'. Hence, more energy transfer (by work-energy theorem) and so more rise in temperature in 'soft steel'. So correct option is (1)

explanation: Mountain roads rarely go straight up the slope because the steep slope of mountains increases the chances of a vehicle slipping off the road. The reason correctly explains why mountain roads are typically constructed in a zigzag or 'switch-back pattern'. So correct option is (1)

Reason: Mass and energy are inter-convertible in accordance with Einstein's relation, $E=m c^{2}$.
explanation: Mass is not conserved separately, but mass and energy are conserved as a single entity called mass-energy. This is in accordance with Einstein's famous relation, $E=m c^{2}$, which shows the equivalence between mass and energy. So correct option is (1)

question:
Assertion: A body cannot have energy without having momentum but it can have momentum without having energy.

Reason: Momentum and energy have different dimensions.
explanation: A body can possess potential energy due to its position, such as a compressed or stretched spring, even if it lacks momentum. However, when a body has no energy, its kinetic energy is zero, leading to zero momentum. Additionally, the dimensions of momentum, $\left[M L T^{-1}\right]$, differ from the dimensions of energy, $\left[M L^{2} T^{-2}\right]$, indicating that momentum and energy are distinct physical quantities. So correct option is (4)

When a force retards the motion of a body, work is still done, but it is negative because the force and displacement are in opposite directions. So correct option is (3)

question:
Assertion: Work done is greater than zero, if angle between force and displacement is acute or both are in same direction.

Reason: Work done by friction on a body sliding down an inclined plane is positive.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955858/preptracker/questions/q_35203_explanation_1764955858362.png $W=F S \cos \theta$
We know $\cos \,\,0^\circ = 1$ ( When $F$ and $S$ are in same direction )
$\cos \,\,90^\circ = 0$
$\Rightarrow$ Assertion is correct
The work done by friction on a body sliding down an inclined plane is actually negative, not positive. So option (3) is correct. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955860/preptracker/questions/q_35203_explanation_1764955860293.png $F=m g \sin \phi$
$f=\mu m g \cos \phi$
Here $\theta=\angle \vec{f}, \vec{S}$
$\theta = {\rm{ }}180^\circ {\rm{ }}({\rm{as}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \vec f{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{and}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \vec S{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{are}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{antiparallel}})$
$\Rightarrow$ Work done by $f$ is negative
$\Rightarrow$ Reason is incorrect $\left( {\cos 180^\circ = - 1} \right)$
So correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: In the figure, a block slides along a track from one level to a higher level, by moving through an intermediate valley. The track is frictionless untill the block reaches the higher level. There a frictional force stops the block in a distance $d$. The block's initial speed $v_{0}$ is $6\,m{\rm{/}}s$, the height difference $h$ is $1.1\,m$ and the coefficient of kinetic friction $\mu$ is 0.6 . The value of $d$ is
explanation:
By work energy theorem
$\dfrac{1}{2} m\left(6^{2}\right)-m g(1.1)$ is the $K E$ just at the end of valley.
Hence by work energy theorem
$\frac{1}{2}m\left( {{6^2}} \right) - mg(1.1) = \mu mgd$
$18 - 11 = 0.6 \times 10 \times d$
$d = \frac{7}{6} = 1.17\,m$.

question: A dog of mass $m = 4\,kg$ runs from the left end of a curved ramp with speed ${V_0} = 8\,m{\rm{/}}s$ at height $8\,m$ above the ground. It then slides to the right and comes to rest when it reaches a height $10\,m$ from the ground. The maximum increase in thermal energy of the dog-ramp system because of sliding is $\left( {g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}} \right)$
explanation:
Loss of energy $=E_{i}-E_{f}$
${W_{slide{\rm{ }}}} + {W_{conservative{\rm{ }}}} = 0 - \frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 64$
${W_s} + \left( {{U_1} - {U_2}} \right) = - 128$
${W_s} + 4 \times 10 \times 8 - 4 \times 10 \times 10 = - 128$
${W_s} + 320 - 400 = - 128 \Rightarrow {W_s} - 80 = - 128$
${W_s} = - 128 + 80\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \Rightarrow {W_s} = - 48\,J$
subject: Physics

question:
An athlete $m = 60\,kg$ in the Olympic games
covers a distance of $100\,m$ in $10\,s$. His kinetic energy can be estimated to be in the range:
explanation:
Approximate mass $ = 60\,kg$
Approximate velocity $ = 10\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Approximate kinetic energy
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 60 \times 100 = 3000\,J$
So kinetic energy range $ = 2000\,J$ to $5000\,J$.

question: A loaded trolley and uploaded trolley are both moving with same kinetic energy. The mass of the loaded trolley is three times that of the unloaded trolley. Brakes are applied to both of them, so as to exert an equal retarding force. If $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$ be the time taken by the unloaded and loaded trolleys respectively, before coming to a stop, then:
explanation:
Let mass of the unloaded trolley $=M_{1}$. Mass of loaded trolley $=3 M$.
Then, $\dfrac{\text { Time taken by the unloaded trolley }\left(b_{1}\right)}{\text { Time taken by the unloaded trolley }\left(b_{2}\right)}$ $=\sqrt{\dfrac{\text { mass of loaded trolley }}{\text { mass unloaded trolley }}}$
$\dfrac{t_{1}}{t_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{3 M}{M}}=\sqrt{3}$
$t_{1}=\sqrt{3} t_{2}$

question: When a bullet of mass $10 \mathrm{~g}$ and speed $100 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ penetrates up to distance $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ in a human body in rest. The resistance offered by human body is:
explanation:
Mass of bullet $ = 10\,g\,(\,m)$
Speed of bullet $ = 100\,m{s^{ - 1}}(v)$
Distance penetrated
$ = 10\,cm\,(x)(1\,cm) = {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
$U \quad=R x \rightarrow$ displacement
$\downarrow \quad \downarrow$
energy resistance Force
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=R x$
$R = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{{{10}^{ - 2}} \times {{\left( {{{10}^2}} \right)}^2}}}{{0.01}} = 5000\,N$

question: Force acting on a particle moving in a straight line varies with the velocity of the particle as $F=K v^{-2}$, where $K$ is constant. The work done by this force in time $t$ is:
explanation:
$m \dfrac{d v}{d t}=K v^{-2} \int m\left(v^{2} d v\right)=\int K d t$
$m\left(\dfrac{v^{3}}{3}\right)=K t \quad \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{3}{2} \dfrac{K t}{v}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: A particle moves in a straight line with retardation proportional to its displacement. The loss in kinetic energy of the particle, for any displacement $x$ is proportional to:
explanation:
$a=-k x$ or $\dfrac{v d v}{d x}=-k x, v d v=-k x d x$
Let the velocity change from $v_{0}$ to $v$
$\int_{v_{0}}^{v} v d v=-\int_{0}^{x} k x d x \Rightarrow \dfrac{v^{2}-v_{0}^{2}}{2}=\dfrac{-k x^{2}}{2}$
$m\left(\dfrac{v^{2}-v_{0}^{2}}{2}\right)=\dfrac{-m k x^{2}}{2}$
$\Delta k \propto x^{2} \quad[\Delta k$ is loss in kinetic energy]
subject: Physics

question: A weight lifter jerks $220\,kg$ vertically through 1.5 meters and holds still at that height for two minutes. The works done by him in lifting and in holding it still are respectively
explanation:
Work done in lifting through height $h=m g h$. No work is done in holding the weight at height $'h'$. Hence total work done $=m g h$
$ = 220 \times 9.8 \times 1.5$
$ = 3234\,J$
Hence answers are respectively $3234\,J$, zero.

subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: A body constrained to move in the $y$-direction is subjected to a force $F=2 \hat{i}+15 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k} N$. The work done by this force, in moving the body through a distance of $10\,m$ along $y$-axis is
explanation:
$F=(2 \hat{i}+15 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}) N, s=10 \hat{j} m$
$\therefore$ work done,
$W = \vec F \cdot \vec s = (2\hat i + 15\hat j + 6\hat k) \cdot (10\hat j) = 150\,J$

question: A force acts on a body and displaces it in its direction. The graph shows the relation between the force and displacement, the work done by the force is
explanation:
The work done = Area under $F-s$ graph.
$ \Rightarrow W = \frac{1}{2}(12) \times 60 = 360\,J$

question: A force \(F\) acting on a particle varies with the position \(x\) as shown in the graph. Find the work done by the force in displacing the particle from \[x = - a\,\,{\rm{to}}\,\,x = + 2a\]
explanation:
The area under force-displacement area gives work done.
$\Rightarrow$ The given interval $x=-a$ to $x=+2 a$ can be divided into $x=-a$ to 0 and $x=0$ to $2 b$.
$\Rightarrow W_{1}=-\dfrac{1}{2} a b$ and $W_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2}(2 a)(2 b)=2 a b$
$\Rightarrow$ Total work done $=2 a b-\dfrac{1}{2} a b=\dfrac{3 a b}{2}$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: Starting at rest, a  $10\,kg$  object is acted upon by only one force as indicated in figure. Then the total work done by the force is
explanation:
Using impulse-momentum theorem
$P_{f}=P_{i}+\int F d t$
$P=m v=0+30(2)-10 \times 1$
$P=m v=50$
$\Rightarrow K E$ gained $=\dfrac{P^{2}}{2 m}$
$ = \frac{{2500}}{{20}}$
$ = 125\,J.$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: A block of mass $5\,kg$ slides down a rough inclined surface. The angle of inclination is $45^{\circ}$. The coefficient of sliding friction is 0.20 . When the block slides $10\,m$, the work done on the block by force of friction is:
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951416/preptracker/questions/q_35071_explanation_1764951416001.png $W_{F}=\mu N x$
$W_{F}=\mu m g \cos \theta x$
$=0.2 \times m g \cos \theta \times 10$
$=-0.2 \times 5 \times 10 \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \times 10 \cos 180^{\circ}$
$=50 \sqrt{2} \cos 180^{\circ}$
$W_{F}=-50 \sqrt{2}$.
subject: Physics

explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956868/preptracker/questions/q_35064_explanation_1764956868518.png Work done $=$ area under $F-x$ graph $=$ area of trapezium

question: Applied force versus position graph of an object is given below. Find the work done by the forces on the object.

question: A particle experiences a variable force $\vec{F}=\left(4 x \hat{i}+3 y^{2} \hat{j}\right)$ in a horizontal $x$-$y$ plane. Assume distance in meter and force in newton. If the particle moves from point $(1,2)$ to point $(2,3)$ in the $x-y$ plane, the Kinetic energy changes by
explanation:
$F=4 x \hat{i}+3 y^{2} \hat{j}$
We use work-energy theorem
Work done $=$ change in kinetic energy
$W=\Delta E_{K}$
Work $W=\int \vec{F} \cdot(d x \hat{i}+d y \hat{j})$
$\begin{aligned}& =\int_{1}^{2} 4 x d x+\int_{2}^{3} 3 y^{2} d x \\& \Rightarrow W=\left(2 x^{2}\right)_{1}^{2}+\left(y^{3}\right)_{2}^{3} \\& =(8-2)+(27-8) \\& =6+19=25 J \\& \Rightarrow \Delta E_{K}=25 J\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: A body of mass $m$ starts moving from rest along $x$-axis so that its velocity varies as $v=a \sqrt{s}$ where $a$ is a constant and $\mathrm{s}$ is the distance covered by the body. The total work done by all the forces acting on the body in the first $t$ seconds after the start of the motion is:
explanation:
$v=a \sqrt{s}=\dfrac{d s}{d t}$
$\int_{0}^{s} \dfrac{d s}{\sqrt{s}}=a \int_{0}^{t} d t$
$2 \sqrt{s}=a t$
$v=\dfrac{a^{2} t}{2}$
$W=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{8} m a^{4} t^{2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: A small ball of mass $m$ starts at a point $A$ with speed $\mathrm{v}_{0}$ and moves along a frictionless track $AB$ as shown. The track $BC$ has coefficient of friction $\mu$. The ball comes to stop at $C$ after travelling a distance $L$ which is :-
explanation:
From conservation of energy
$\mu mgL{\text{ }} = mgh + \frac{1}{2}mv_0^2\,L{\text{ }} = \frac{h}{\mu } + \frac{{v_0^2}}{{2\mu g}}$
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: A force \(\vec{F}=(3 x y-5 z) \hat{j}+4 z \hat{k}\) is applied on a particle. The work done by the force when the particle moves from point \((0,0,0)\) to point \((2,4,0)\) as shown in figure.
explanation:
The $z$-component of the force and the $x$ component of displacement are ineffective here.
$d W=F_{x} d x+F_{y} d y+F_{z} d z=F_{y} d y=3 x y . d y$
Where ${F_z} = 0,dz = 0\& {F_x} = 0$
$\begin{gathered}d W=6 x^{4} d x \Rightarrow W=\int_{0}^{2} 6 x^{4} d x \\W=192 / 5 .\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: Given \(\vec{F}=\left(x y^{2}\right) \hat{i}+\left(x^{2} y\right) \hat{j} N\). The work done by \(\vec{F}\) when a particle is taken along the semicircular path \[OAB\] where the coordinates of \[B\] are \((4,0)\) is
explanation:
Given $\vec{F}=\left(x y^{2}\right) \hat{i}+\left(x^{2} y\right) \hat{j}$
$\begin{aligned}W & =\int F_{x} d x+\int F_{y} d y \\W & =\int x y^{2} d x+\int x^{2} y d y \\& =\dfrac{1}{2} \int d\left(x^{2} y^{2}\right)=\left[\dfrac{x^{2} y^{2}}{2}\right]_{(0,0)}^{(4,0)}=0\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: A 10 \[kg\] brick moves along an \[x\]-axis. Its acceleration as a function of its position is shown in figure. What is the net work performed on the brick by the force causing the acceleration as the brick moves from \(x=0\) to \[x = 8.0\,m\] ?
explanation:
According to the graph the acceleration $a$ varies linearly with the coordinate $x$. We may write $a=\alpha x$, where $\alpha$ is the slope of the graph
$\alpha = \frac{{20}}{8} = 2.5\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
The force on the brick is in the positive $x$ direction and according to Newton's second law, its magnitude is given by
$F=\dfrac{a}{m}=\dfrac{\alpha}{m} x$
if $x_{f}$ is the final coordinate, the work done by the force is
$\begin{aligned}W & =\int_{0}^{x_{f}} F d x=\dfrac{\alpha}{m} \int_{0}^{x_{f}} x d x \\& =\dfrac{\alpha}{2 m} x_{f}^{2}=\dfrac{2.5}{2 \times 10} \times(8)^{2}=8 J\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: Consider two observers moving with respect to each other at a speed $v$ along a straight line. They observe a block of mass m moving a distance $l$ on a rough surface. The following quantity will be same as observed by the observers
explanation: Kinetic energy of a body depends upon the reference frame and so does the work done. Since two observers are not accelerating w.r.t. each other so they will observe same force acting on mass and so they will observe same accelerations of block.

options.text: Total work done on the block
Kinetic energy of the block at time $t$

subject: Physics
subtopic: Work Energy Theorem with respect to a Moving Frame

question: A block of mass $M$ is kept on a plane from which it starts accelerating upwards from rest with a constant acceleration $a$. During the time interval the work done by contact force on mass $M$ is
explanation:
${W_N} = NS$
$N = Mg + Ma$
$S = \frac{1}{2}a{T^2} \Rightarrow {W_N} = \frac{1}{2}M(a + g)a{T^2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: A body of mass $m$ $kg$ is ascending on a smooth inclined plane of inclination $\sin \theta \left( { = \frac{1}{x}} \right)$ with constant acceleration of $a$. The final velocity of the body is $v$. The work done by the external force is (Initial velocity of the body $=0$ )
explanation:
$\sin \theta=\dfrac{1}{x}$
From free body diagram of the body https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951315/preptracker/questions/q_34697_explanation_1764951315556.png $\begin{gathered}F-m g \sin \theta=m a \\F=m(g \sin \theta+a)=m\left(\dfrac{g}{x}+a\right)\end{gathered}$
Displacement of the body till its velocity reaches $v$
$\begin{aligned}v^{2} & =0+2 a s \Rightarrow s=\dfrac{v^{2}}{2 a} \\W & =F s \cos 0^{\circ}=\dfrac{m}{x}(g+x a) \times \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 a} \\& =\dfrac{m v^{2}}{2 a x}(g+x a)\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: In the figure shown initially spring is in unstretched state & blocks are at rest. Now 100 $N$ force is applied on block $A$ & $B$ as shown in the figure. After some time velocity of $'A'$ becomes 2 $m/s$ & that '$B$' 4 $m/s$ & block $A$ displaced by amount 10 $cm$. Then work done by spring force on $A$ will be:
explanation:
$W_{\text {spring }}+W_{100 N}=\Delta k($ on $A)$
$\begin{aligned}& W_{\text {spring }}+(100)\left(\dfrac{10}{100}\right)=\dfrac{1}{2}(2)(2)^{2} \\& W_{\text {spring }}=4-10=-6 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: A long block $A$ is at rest on a smooth horizontal surface. A small block $B$ whose mass is half mass of $A$ is placed on $A$ at one end and is given an initial velocity u as shown in the figure. The coefficient of friction between the blocks is $\mu$
explanation:
Let $v$ be the final common velocity. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956864/preptracker/questions/q_34689_explanation_1764956864371.png $(m+2 m) v=m u \Rightarrow v=\dfrac{u}{3}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956865/preptracker/questions/q_34689_explanation_1764956865122.png $a_{1}=\dfrac{\mu m g}{m}=\mu g, a_{2}=\dfrac{\mu m g}{2 m}=\dfrac{\mu g}{2}$
Acceleration of $\mathrm{B}$ relative to $\mathrm{A}$
$a_{1}+a_{2}=\mu g+\dfrac{\mu g}{2}=\dfrac{3}{2} \mu g \text { towards left }$
Linear momentum of the system is conserved so $\quad \dfrac{P_{i}}{P_{f}}=1$

options.text: Magnitude of total work done by friction is equal to the final kinetic energy of the system.

Acceleration of $B$ relative to A initially is
$3 \mu g / 2$ towards left.
Finally both move with a common velocity 2$u$/3
The ratio of initial to final momentum of the system is 2

subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: A force $F = 50\,N$ is applied at one end of string, the other end of which is tied to a block of mass 10 $kg$. The block is free to move on a frictionless horizontal surface. Take initial instant as $\theta=30^{\circ}$ and final instant as $37^{\circ}$. For the time between these two instants, answer the following question Net work done by the force $F$ on the block is
explanation:
${l_1} = \frac{2}{{\sin 30^\circ }} = 4m,{l_2} = \frac{2}{{\sin 37^\circ }} = \frac{{10}}{3}m$
Displacement of point $P: l_{1}-l_{2}=4-\dfrac{10}{3}=\dfrac{2}{3} m$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951314/preptracker/questions/q_34685_explanation_1764951313807.png Work done, $W=F\left(l_{1}-l_{2}\right)=50 \times \dfrac{2}{3}=\dfrac{100}{3} \mathrm{~J}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: The figure shows all the surfaces are frictionless and mass of the block,$m = 1\,kg$. The block and wedge are held initially at rest. Now wedge is given a horizontal acceleartion of $10\,m/{s^2}$ by applying a force on the wedge, so that the block does not slip on the wedge. Then work done by the normal force in ground frame on the block in $\sqrt{3}$ seconds is
explanation: Let $N$ be the normal force on the block  https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956829/preptracker/questions/q_34681_explanation_1764956828830.png    $\begin{aligned}& N \sin \theta=m a \\& N \cos \theta=m g \\\Rightarrow & \tan \theta=\dfrac{a}{g}=1 \Rightarrow \theta=45^{\circ} \\W & =(N \sin \theta)(v t)=150 J\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: A uniform chain of length $L$ and mass $M$ overhangs a horizontal table with its two-third part on a table. The friction coefficient between the table and the chain is $\mu$. The work done by the friction during the period the chain slips off the table is
explanation:
Let $x$ be the length of the chain on the table. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955780/preptracker/questions/q_34677_explanation_1764955779681.png The frictional force on the chain is
$\begin{aligned}& f=\mu(\lambda x) g \quad\left(\mu=\dfrac{M}{L}\right) \\& d W=f d x=\mu \lambda g x d x\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned}& W=\mu \lambda g \int_{2 L / 3}^{0} x d x=\dfrac{\mu M g}{2 L}\left[x^{2}\right]_{2 L / 3}^{0} \\& W=\dfrac{\mu M g}{2 L}\left[-\left(\dfrac{2 L}{3}\right)^{2}\right]=\dfrac{-\mu M g}{2 L} \times \dfrac{4 L^{2}}{9} \\& W=\dfrac{-2 \mu M g L}{9}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question:
The work done on a particle of mass $m$ by a force, $K\left[\dfrac{x}{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i}+\dfrac{y}{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{j}\right]$. being a constant of appropriate dimensions),
when the particle is taken from the point $(a, 0)$ to the point $(0, a)$ along a circular path of radius $a$ about the origin in the $x-y$ plane is
explanation:
Here $\dfrac{\partial F_{x}}{\partial y}=\dfrac{\partial F_{y}}{\partial x}$
That's why force is conservative and particle is moving on circle i.e., $x^{2}+y^{2}=a^{2}$
Here work is independent of path
Hence $W=\int_{a}^{0} F_{x} d x+F_{y} d y$
$=\dfrac{K}{a^{3}} \int_{a}^{0} x d x+\dfrac{K}{a^{3}} \int_{0}^{a} y d y=0$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: Two particles $A$ and $B$, move with constant velocities $\vec{v}_{1}$ and $\vec{v}_{2}$. At the initial moment their position vectors are $\vec{r}_{1}$ and $\vec{r}_{2}$ respectively. The condition for particles $A$ and $B$ for their collision is
explanation:
For two particles to collide, the direction of the relative velocity of one with respect to other should be directed towards the relative position of the other particle
i.e. $\dfrac{\vec{r}_{1}-\vec{r}_{2}}{\left|\vec{r}_{1}-\vec{r}_{2}\right|} \rightarrow$ direction of relative position of 1 w.r.t.2.& $\dfrac{\vec{v}_{1}-\vec{v}_{2}}{\left|\vec{v}_{1}-\vec{v}_{2}\right|} \rightarrow$ direction of velocity of 2 w.r..t.1
so for collision of A & B
$\dfrac{\vec{r}_{1}-\vec{r}_{2}}{\left|\vec{r}_{1}-\vec{r}_{2}\right|}=\dfrac{\vec{v}_{1}-\vec{v}_{2}}{\left|\vec{v}_{1}-\vec{v}_{2}\right|} \text { (Both are unit vectors) }$
subject: Physics
topic: Miscellaneous Problems on Work, Energy and Power
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Work, Energy and Power

question: A mass of  1 $kg$ is acted upon by a single force $F = (4\hat i + 4\widehat j)N$. Under this force it is displaced from $(0.0)$ to $(1\,m,1\,m)$. If initially the speed of the particle was 2 $m/s$, its final speed should be
explanation:
Work done = change in kinetic energy
$\therefore \int_{(0.0)}^{(1.1)} {(4\widehat i + 4\widehat j)} \cdot (dx\widehat i + dy\widehat j) = \frac{1}{2} \times 1 \times \left( {v_f^2 - v_i^2} \right)$
or $8=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(v_{f}^{2}-4\right)$
or ${v_f} = 4.5\,m/s$
subject: Physics

subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: A particle of mass 0.01 $kg$ travels with velocity given by $(4\widehat {\rm{i}} + 16)km{s^{ - 1}}$. After sometime, its velocity becomes $(8\widehat i + 20)\widehat j\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. The work done on particle during this interval of time is

question: A block of mass $M$ is attached to the lower end of a vertical spring. The spring is hung from a ceiling and has force constant value $K$. The mass is released from rest with the spring initially unstretched. The maximum extension produced in the length of the spring will be
explanation:
Let $x$ be the extension in the spring
Applying conservation of energy
$Mgx - \frac{1}{2}k{x^2} = 0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=\dfrac{2 M g}{k}$

question: A ball is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity of 10 $m/s$. It returns to the ground with a velocity of 9 $m/s$. If  $g = 9.8\,m/{s^2}$, then the maximum height attained by the is nearly ( assume air resistance to be uniform)
explanation:
Total loss in friction $ = {K_i} - {K_f}$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\,m(100 - 81)$
Half of the loss will be in upward direction Hence,
$mgh + \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{19}}{2}\,m} \right) = {K_i} = \frac{1}{2}\,m(100) = 50\,m$
Therefore, $h = 4.61\,m$
subject: Physics

question: Given that the displacement of the body in metre is a function of time as follows $x=2 t^{4}+5$. The mass of the body is 2 $kg$. What is the increase in its kinetic energy one second after that start of motion?
explanation:
$v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 8{t^3}$
${v_{0\,s}} = 0,{v_{1\,s}} = 8\,m/s$
$\therefore \Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times (64 - 0)J$
$ = 64\,J$

question: A pendulum of length 2 $m$ left at $A$. When it reaches $B$, it looses $10 \%$ of its total energy due to air resistance. The velocity at $B$ is
explanation:
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = (0.9)(mgh)$
$\therefore v = \sqrt {1.8gh} $
$=\sqrt{1.8 \times 10 \times 2}$
$ = 6\,m/s$

question: A ball is released from the top of a tower. The ratio of work done by force of gravity in first, second and third second of the motion of ball is
explanation:
Ratio of displacement is
$\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g}(1)^{2} \dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g}(2)^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g}(1)^{2} \dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g}(3)^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g}(1)^{2} \dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g}(2)^{2}$
or $1: 3: 5$
Therefore ratio of work done will be also be $1: 3: 5$

question: A mass $M$ is lowered with the help of a string by a distance $x$ at a constant acceleration $\frac{g}{2}$. The magnitude of work done by the string will be
explanation: $Mg - T = \frac{{Mg}}{2}$ or $T = \frac{{Mg}}{2}$  https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955731/preptracker/questions/q_34384_explanation_1764955731510.png    or magnitude of work done$ = T \times x = \frac{{Mgx}}{2}$

question: A force acting on a particle is $(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) N$. Work done by the force is zero, when a particle is moved on the line $3y + kx = 5$. Here value of $k$  is
explanation:
Line and force should be perpendicular, as the work done is given to be zero
${m_1} = \frac{{{F_y}}}{{{F_x}}} = \frac{3}{2} = {\rm{ }}$ slope of force
${m_2} = - \frac{k}{3} = $ slope of line
${m_1}\,{m_2} = - 1$
Therefore,$k = 2$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: A body of mass 1 $kg$  moves from points $A(2\,m,3\,m,4\,m)$ to $B(3\,m,2m,5\,m)$. During motion of a body, a force $F=(2 N) \hat{i}-(4 N) \hat{j}$ acts on it. The work done by the force on the particle during displacement is
explanation:
$r=r_{B}-r_{A}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}) m$
$\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{F} \cdot \mathrm{r}=2+4=6 \mathrm{~J}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: A body, constrained to move in the $y$ - direction , is subjected to a force $F=(-2 \hat{i}+15 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}) N$.What is the work done by this force in moving the body through a distance of $10 \mathrm{~m}$ along the $y$ axis?
explanation:
$W=$ ($y$ component of force) $x$ ( displacement along $y$ - axis)
$=15 \times 10=150 \mathrm{~J}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: $A$ force $(F) =  - 5\hat i - 7\hat j + 3\hat k$ acting on a particle causes a displacement $(s) = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + a\hat k$ in its own direction. If the work done is  14 $J$, then the value of ' $a$ ' is
explanation:
Given, $\vec{F}=-5 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$,
$\vec{s}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+a \hat{k}$
and $\mathrm{W}=14 \mathrm{~J}$
The work done by a force in displacing through a distance is given by
$\mathrm{W}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{s}}$
Substituting above values in eq.(1), we get
$14=(-5 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \cdot(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+a \hat{k})$
$14=-15+14+3 a$
$\Rightarrow a=\dfrac{15}{3}=5$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: Work done by a force $F=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) N$ acting on a particle in displacing it from the point $r_{1}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$ to the point $r_{2}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$ is
explanation:
$W = F.S = F.\left( {{r_2} - {r_1}} \right)$
$ = (\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k) \cdot [(\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k) - (\hat i + \hat j + \hat k)]$
$ = (\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k) \cdot ( - 2\hat j + \hat k){\rm{ }}$
$ = - 4 + 3 = - 1\,J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: A block of mass 1 $kg$ slides down a rough inclined plane of inclination $60^{\circ}$ starting from its top. If coefficient of kinetic friction is 0.5 and length of the plane $d = 1\,m$ then work done aganist friction is
explanation:
Work done aganist friction $ = (\mu mg\cos \theta )d$
$W = 0.5 \times 1 \times 9.8 \times \cos 60^\circ = 2.45J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: Two bodies of masses ${m_1}$ and ${m_2}$ are acted upon by a constant force $F$ for a time $t$. They start from rest and acquire kinetic energies ${E_1}$ and ${E_2}$ respectively. Then $\dfrac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}$ is

question: A block of mass 10 $kg$ is moving in $x$ - direction with a constant speed of 10 $m/s$. It is subjected to a retarding force $F = (0.1x)N$ during its travel from $x = 20\,m$ to $x = 30\,m$. Its final kinetic energy will be
explanation:
${K_f} - {K_i} = W$
$\therefore {K_f} = - {K_i} + W$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times {\left( {10} \right)^2} + \int\limits_{20}^{30} {\left( { - 0.1x} \right)dx} $
$ = 475J$
subject: Physics

subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: A particle moves on a rough horizontal ground with some initial velocity say ${v_0}$. If $\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right)^{\text {th }}$ of its kinetic energy is lost due to friction in time ${t_0}$ then coefficient of friction between the particle and the ground is
explanation:
${\frac{3}{4}^{th}}$ is lost. Hence left is ${\frac{1}{4}^{th}}$ or
${v^2} = \frac{{v_0^2}}{u}$
$\therefore v = \frac{{{v_0}}}{2} = {v_0} - a{t_0} = {v_0} - ug{t_0}$
or $u = \frac{{{v_0}}}{{2g{t_0}}}$
subject: Physics

question: A truck accelerates from speed $v$ to 2 $v$. Work done in during this is
explanation:
Let $m$ be mass of the truck.
According to work-energy theorem
$W = {K_f} - {K_i}$
Work done in accelerating the truck from rest to speed $v$ is
$\begin{aligned}& W_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}-0 \quad(\because u=0) \\& =\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}\end{aligned}$
Work done in accelerating the truck from speed $v$ to 2$v$ is
$W_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m(2 v)^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} m(v)^{2}$
$=\dfrac{3}{2} m v^{2}=3 \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=3 W_{1} \quad(\operatorname{Using}(1))$

options.text: Three times as the work done in accelerating it from rest to $v$
Same as the work done in accelerating it from rest to $v$
Four times as the work done in accelerating it from rest to $v$
Lless than the work done in accelerating it from rest to $v$

subject: Physics

subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: The work done by a force acting on a body is as shown in the graph. The total work done in covering an initial distance of 20 $m$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951207/preptracker/questions/q_34292_explanation_1764951207035.png Work done $W=$ area under $F-S$ graph $=$ area of trapezium $ABCD+$ area of trapezium $CEFD$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\, \times \,\left( {10\, + \,15} \right)\, \times \,10\, + \,\frac{1}{2}\, \times \,\left( {10\, + \,20} \right)\, \times \,5$
$ = 125 + 75 = 200\,J$

subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: A uniform force of $(3\hat i + \hat j)N$ acts on a particle of mass 2 $kg$. Hence, the particle is displaced from position $(2\hat i + \hat k)m$ to position $(4\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k)m$. The work done by the force on the particle is
explanation:
Given, force $F=3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}$
$\begin{aligned}& \vec{r}_{1}=(2 \hat{i}+\hat{k}) m \text { and } \vec{r}_{2}=(4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \\& \therefore \quad s=\vec{r}_{2}-\vec{r}_{1}=(4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k})-(2 \hat{i}+\hat{k}) \\& =(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) m \\& \therefore \quad W=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{s}=(3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) \\& =9 J\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: A block of mass 5 $kg$ slides down a rough inclined surface. The angle of inclination is $45^{\circ}$. The coefficient of sliding friction is 0.20 . When the block slides 10 $cm$ , the work done on the block by force of friction is
explanation: $\begin{aligned}& W_{f}=f \cos 180^{\circ}=(\mu \mathrm{mg} \cos \theta)(-1)(\mathrm{S}) \\& =-0.2 \times 5 \times 10 \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \times 0.1 \\& =-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

question: A force $F = 20 + 10y$ acts on a particle in $y$ direction where$F$ is in newton and $y$ in meter. Work done by this force to move the particle from $y = 0$ to $y = 1$ $m$ is
explanation:
Work done by variable force is
$W=\int_{y_{i}}^{y_{f}} F d y$
Here, $y_{i}=0, y_{f}=1 \mathrm{~m}$
$\therefore \quad W=\int_{0}^{1}(20+10 y) d y=\left[20 y+\dfrac{10 y^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{1}=25 J$

question: A spring of force constant $k$ is cut into lengths of ratio 1:2:3. They are connected in series and the new force constant is  $k'$. Then they are connected in parallel and force constant is $k''$. Then ${k^\prime }:k''$ is
explanation:
Length of the spring segments $=\dfrac{\ell}{6}, \dfrac{\ell}{3}, \dfrac{\ell}{2}$
As we know $K \propto \dfrac{1}{\ell}$
So spring constants for spring segments will be
$K_{1}=6 K, K_{2}=3 K, K_{3}=2 K$
For parallel combination
$K^{\prime \prime}=K_{1}+K_{2}+K_{3}=11 K$
For series combination
$K^{\prime}=K$ (As it will become original spring)
so $K^{\prime}: K^{\prime \prime}=1: 11$

question:
Consider a drop of rain water having mass 1 $g$ falling from a height of 1 $km$. It hits the ground with a speed of 50 $m/s$ . Take ' $g$ ' constant with a value $10\,m/{s^2}$. The work done by the
(i) gravitational force and the
(ii) resistive force of air is
explanation:
Work done by the gravity $\left(W_{g}\right)=m g h$
$=10^{-3} \times 10 \times 10^{3}=10 \mathrm{~J}$
By work-energy theorem $=W_{g}+W_{\text {res }}=\Delta K E$
$\begin{aligned}& 10+W_{\text {res }}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10^{-3} \times(50)^{2} \\& W_{\text {res }}=-8.75 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

question: A particle moves from a point $(-2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j})$ to $(4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})$ when a force of $(4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) N$ is applied. How much work has been done by the force ?
explanation:
$\vec{S}=$ Displacement $=(4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})-(-2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j})$
$=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$
Work $=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{S}=(4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})=5 \mathrm{j}$

explanation:

question: A 10 \[kg\] mass moves along \[x\]-axis. Its acceleration as a function of its position is shown in the figure. What is the total work done on the mass by the force as the mass moves from \[x = 0\] to \[x = 8\,cm\] ?
explanation:

question: The graph between the resistive force \(F\) acting on a body and the distance covered by the body is shown in the figure. The mass of the body is 2.5 \[kg\] and initial velocity is 2 \[m/s\]. When the distance covered by the body is 4 \[m\] , its kinetic energy would be
explanation:
Initial $\mathrm{KE}$ of the body
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 25 \times 4=50 \mathrm{~J}$
Work done against resistive force
$\begin{aligned}& =\text { Area between } F-x \text { graph } \\& =\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 20=40 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$
Final $\mathrm{KE}=$ initial $\mathrm{KE}$ - Work done against resistive force
$=50-40=10 \mathrm{~J}$

question: A block of mass $m$ is lying at rest at point $P$ of a wedge having a smooth semi-circular track of radius $R$. What should be the minimum value of $a_{0}$ so that the mass can just reach point $Q$ ?
explanation:
From work energy theorem w.r.to wedge frame
$W_{F_{s}}=W_{g}=\Delta k=0$
$W_{F_{s}}=m a_{0} R \quad$ (work done by pseudo force)
$W_{g}=-m g R$ (work done by gravitational force)
$\begin{aligned}& m a_{0} R=m g R \\& a_{0}=g\end{aligned}$

question: A block of mass $m$ is suspended by a light thread from an elevator. The elevator is accelerating upward with uniform acceleration $a$. The work done by tension on the block during $\mathrm{t}$ seconds from the ground frame and the lift frame are:
explanation:
From ground frame https://image.cleverb.in/upload/2023/07/05/ES-53_(2).png $T-m g=m a \Rightarrow T=m(g+a)$
and $s=\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2} \Rightarrow W=T s \cos 0^{\circ}$
$=m(g+a) \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$
From lift frame https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951199/preptracker/questions/q_34061_explanation_1764951199533.png As in lift frame block is at rest so $\mathrm{W}=0$

question: The trolley starts accelerating with acceleration $a$. The maximum angle deflected by thread from vertical will be:
explanation:
By work energy theorem w.r.to trolley
$\begin{aligned}& W_{m g}+W_{F_{s}}=\Delta K . E \\& W_{m g}=-m g L(1-\cos \theta) \\& W_{F_{s}}=m a L \sin \theta\end{aligned}$
Where $F_{s}$ is the pseudo force $\Delta K . E=0$
$ \Rightarrow \tan \frac{\theta }{2} = \frac{a}{g}$
$ \Rightarrow \theta = 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{a}{g}} \right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951188/preptracker/questions/q_33970_explanation_1764951188535.png

question: A 1 $kg$ mass is projected down a rough circular track placed in vertical plane as shown. The speed of the mass at point$A$ is 3 $m/s$  and at point $B$, it is 60 $m/s$. How much work is done on the mass between $A$ and $B$ by the force of friction?
explanation:
The $K.E$ at $A$ and $B$ are
$\begin{aligned}& K_{A}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 1 \times 3^{2}=4 \cdot 5 \mathrm{~J} \\& K_{B}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 1 \times 6^{2}=18 \mathrm{~J} \\& K_{B}-K_{A}=13 \cdot 5 \mathrm{~J} \\& \Delta U_{B A}=m g h=1 \times 10 \times 2=20 \\& \left|W_{f}\right|=20-13 \cdot 5=6.5 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The block of mass $m$ is pulling, vertically up with constant speed, by applying force $P$. The free end of the string is pulled by $l$ meter, the increase in potential energy of the block is
explanation:
The constraint relation between point $P$ and block is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951185/preptracker/questions/q_33959_explanation_1764951185522.png The total length of the string is
$l=y_{P}+2 y_{B}+C$
where $\mathrm{C}$ and $l$ are constant
By differentiating the equation
$0=\Delta y_{P}+2 \Delta y_{B}$
As $\left(\Delta y_{P}\right)=l \Rightarrow\left(\Delta y_{B}\right)=\dfrac{l}{2}$
$\Delta U=\dfrac{m g l}{2}$

question: A body of mass 3 $kg$ is under a force which causes a displacement in it and is given by $S=\dfrac{t^{3}}{3}$ (in metres). Find the work done by the force in first 2 seconds.
explanation:
$S=\dfrac{t^{3}}{3} \Rightarrow d S=t^{2} d t$
$a=\dfrac{d^{2} S}{d t^{2}}=\dfrac{d^{2}}{d t^{2}}\left[\dfrac{t^{3}}{3}\right]=2 t m / s^{2}$
Now work done by the force
$\begin{aligned}& W=\int_{0}^{2} \vec{F} \cdot d \vec{S}=\int_{0}^{2} m a \cdot d S \\& \int_{0}^{2} 3 \times 2 t \times t^{2} d t=\int_{0}^{2} 6 t^{3} d t=\dfrac{3}{2}\left[t^{4}\right]_{0}^{2}=24 J\end{aligned}$

question: How much work must be done by a force on 50 $kg$  body in order to accelerate it from rest to 20 $m/s$ in $10 s ?$
explanation:
From equation of motion
$\begin{aligned}& v=u+a t \Rightarrow 20=0+a \times 10 \\& 20=a \times 10 \Rightarrow a=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\end{aligned}$
Now, distance
$s=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2} \Rightarrow s=0+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10 \times 10$
or $\mathrm{s}=100 \mathrm{~m}$
$W=F . s=\text { mas }=50 \times 2 \times 100=10^{4} \mathrm{~J} .$

question: A uniform chain of length 2 $m$ is kept on a table such that a length of 60 $cm$ hangs freely from the edge of the table. The total mass of the chain is 4 $kg$ . What is the work done in pulling the entire chain on the table?
explanation:
The initial position of the chain is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955688/preptracker/questions/q_33909_explanation_1764955687944.png ${W_{\varepsilon xt}} = \Delta U = {U_f} - {U_i}$
by taking the reference line to measure $P$.$E$ on the surface of the table
$\begin{aligned}& U_{i}=m_{1} g(0)+m_{2} g(-0.3) \\& U_{f}=m g(0)=0 \\& m_{1}=\dfrac{4 k g}{2 m} \times 1.6 m, m_{2}=\dfrac{4 k g}{2 m} \times 0.6 \\& W_{\varepsilon x t}=m_{2} g(0.3)=3.6 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

question: A particle of mass 6 $kg$ moves according to the law $x=0.2 t^{2}+0.02 t^{3}$. Find the work done by the force in first 4 $s$.
explanation:
$W=\dfrac{1}{2} m\left(v_{f}^{2}-v_{i}^{2}\right)$
$\begin{aligned}v & =\dfrac{d x}{d t}=0.4 t-0.06 t^{2} \\v & =(0.4) 4-0.06(4)^{2} \\& =0.64 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\& =\dfrac{1}{2} \times 6\left[(0.64)^{2}-0\right]=1.6228 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

question: The displacement $x$ in metre of a particle of mass $m$ $kg$ moving in one dimension under the action of a force is related to the time $t$ in second by the equation $t=\sqrt{x}+3$, the work done by the force (in joule) in first six seconds is
explanation:
Here $t=\sqrt{x}+3$
$\begin{aligned}& x=(t-3)^{2}=t^{2}-6 t+9 \\& v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=2 t-6\end{aligned}$
at $t=0 s, v=2 \times 0-6=-6$
at $t=6 s, v=2 \times 6-6=+6$
Initial and final $\mathrm{KE}$ are same hence no work is done
$W=\dfrac{1}{2} m\left(v_{1}^{2}-v_{2}^{2}\right)=0$
subject: Physics

question: When a spring is stretched by a distance $x$, it exerts a restoring force of magnitude $F=a x^{2}+b x^{3}$ where $a$ and $b$ are constants. The work done in stretching the unstretched rubberb and by $L$ is
explanation:
Work done in stretching the rubber band
$\begin{aligned}W & =\int_{0}^{L}\left(a x^{2}+b x^{3}\right) d x \\& =\dfrac{a L^{3}}{3}+\dfrac{b L^{4}}{4}\end{aligned}$

question: A particle is moved from $(0,0)$ to $(2\,m,2\,m)$ with force $\vec{F}=(x \hat{j}) N$ along path (i) $y = x$ (ii) $y=\sqrt{x}$. The work done about two paths is:
explanation:
$d W=\vec{F} \cdot d \vec{s}=(x \hat{j}) \cdot(d x \hat{i}+d y \hat{j})=x d y$
(i) when $y=x$
$W=\int d W=\int_{0}^{2} y d y=\left|\dfrac{y^{2}}{2}\right|_{0}^{2}=2 J$
(ii) When $y=\sqrt{x}$ or $y^{2}=x$
$W=\int_{0}^{2} y^{2} d y=\left|\dfrac{y^{3}}{3}\right|_{0}^{2}=\dfrac{8}{3} J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: A force $\vec{F}=[y \hat{i}+x \hat{j}]$ act on a particle moving in $x$ - $y$ plane starting from the point $(4,6)$, the particle is taken along straight line to $(7,8)$. The work done by the force is:
explanation:
$W=\int \vec{F} \cdot d \vec{S}=\int[(y \hat{i}+x \hat{j}) \cdot(d x \hat{i}+d y \hat{j})]$
$\begin{aligned}W=\int(y d x+x d y) & =\int_{(4,6)}^{(7,8)} d(x y) \\& =[56-24]=32\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: A force $F=-K(y i+x j)$ (where $K$ is positive constant) acts on a particle moving in the $x y$ plane. Starting from the origin, the particle is taken along the positive $x$-axis to the point $(a, 0)$ and then parallel to the $y$-axis to the point $(a, a)$. The total work done by the force $F$ on the particles is
explanation:
While moving from $(0,0)$ to $(a, 0)$
Along positive $x$-axis, $y=0 \therefore \vec{F}=-k x \hat{j}$
i.e., force in negative y-direction while displacement is in positive $\mathrm{x}$ - direction.
$\therefore W_{1}=0$
Because force is perpendicular to displacement.
Then particle moves from $(a, 0)$ to $(a, a)$ along a line parallel to $\mathrm{y}$-axis $(\mathrm{x}=+a)$ during the
$\vec{F}=-k(y \hat{i}+a \hat{j})$
The first component of force, $-k y \hat{i}$ will not contribute any work because this component is along negative $x$-direction $(-\hat{i})$ while displacement is in positive
$\mathrm{y}$ - direction $(a, 0)$ to $(a, a)$. The second component of force i.e., $k a \hat{j}$ will perform negative work
$\therefore W_{2}=(-k a \hat{j})(a \hat{j})=(-k a)(a)=-k a^{2}$
So net work done on the particle $W=W_{1}+W_{2}$
$=0+\left(-k a^{2}\right)=-k a^{2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: A particle is made to move from the origin in three spells of equal distances, first along the $x$- axis, second parallel to $y$-axis and third parallel to $z$-axis. One of the forces acting on it has constant magnitude of 50 $N$ and always acts along the direction of motion. Work done by this force in the three spells of motion are equal and total work done in all three spells is 300 $J$. The final coordinates of the particle will be:
explanation:
As $W=W_{x}+W_{Y}+W_{Z}$
Given that
$W_{x}=W_{y}=W_{z}=300 / 3=100 \mathrm{~J}$
So $\quad x=W_{x} / F=100 / 50=2 m$
$\begin{aligned}& y=W_{y} / F=100 / 50=2 m \\& z=W_{Z} / F=100 / 50=2 m .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: A small block of mass $m$ is kept on a rough inclined surface of inclination $\theta$ fixed in a lift. The lift moves up with a uniform velocity $v$ and the block does not slide on the incline. The work done by the force of friction on the block in time twill be:
explanation:
As shown in the figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951177/preptracker/questions/q_33880_explanation_1764951176752.png $f=m g \sin \theta$, vertical displacement in time
$t=v t$
Work done $=f v t \sin \theta$
$=m g v t \sin ^{2} \theta$

question: Figure shows a particle sliding on a frictionless track which terminates in a straight horizontal section. If the particle starts slipping from the point $A$, how far away on the horizontal track from the terminating point will the particle hit the ground?
explanation:
From conservation of mechanical energy
$\begin{aligned}& m g(1)=m g(0.5)+\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \\& \Rightarrow \quad v=\sqrt{10} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned}& x=v t=(\sqrt{10}) \sqrt{\dfrac{2 h}{g}} \\& x=\sqrt{10} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 0.5}{10}}=1 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

options.text: 1 $m$ from the end of the track
2 $m$ from the end of the track
3 $m$ from the end of the track

question: A gardener pushes a lawn roller through a distance 20 $m$. If he applies a force of 20 $kg$-$wt$ in a direction inclined at $60^{\circ}$ to the ground, the work done by him is
explanation:
Given
$F=20 \mathrm{~kg}-w \mathrm{t}=20 \times 9.8 \mathrm{~N} \text { and } \mathrm{s}=20 \mathrm{~m}$
$\therefore$ Work done $=F s \cos \theta$
$ = 20 \times 9.8 \times 20 \times \cos 60^\circ = 1960\,J$

question: Two particles having position vectors $8 \hat{j},-8 \hat{i}$ metres are moving with velocities $\vec{v}_{1}=4 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}$ and $\vec{v}_{2}=\alpha \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}$. If they collide after 2 seconds, the value of ' $\alpha$ ' is
explanation:
The displacement vector $\Delta \vec{r}$ between the particles is
$\Delta \,\vec r = {{\vec r}_2} - {{\vec r}_1} = - 8\hat i - 8\hat j$
$\left| {\Delta \,\vec r} \right| = \sqrt {{{( - 8)}^2} + {{( - 8)}^2}} = 8\sqrt 2 \,\,\,\,\,(1)$
The relative velocity is given by
${\overrightarrow v _{rel}} = {\overrightarrow v _2} - {\overrightarrow v _1} = (\alpha - 4)\hat i + 4\hat j$
$\left| {{{\overrightarrow v }_{rel}}} \right| = \sqrt {{{(\alpha - 4)}^2} + 16} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
When they collide in time $t$
$\left| {{{\overrightarrow v }_{rel}}} \right| = \frac{{\left| {\Delta \vec r} \right|}}{t} \Rightarrow \alpha = 8.$
subject: Physics

question:
A block of mass $m$ moving with a velocity $v_{0}$ on a smooth horizontal surface strikes and compresses a spring of stiffiness $k$ till mass comes to rest as shown in the figure. This phenomenon is observed by two observers:
$A$: Standing on the horizontal surface
$B$: Standing on the block W.r.to the observer $A$, the work done by spring force is:
explanation:
Observer $A$ is on the ground
$\begin{aligned}& W_{\text {spring }}=\Delta K \cdot E=0-\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2} \\& W_{\text {spring }}=-\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}\end{aligned}$

options.text: Negative but nothing can be said about its magnitude
Positive but nothing can be said about its magnitude

subtopic: Dependance of Work on Frame

question: The earth circles the sun once a year. How much work would have to be done on the earth to bring it to rest relative to the sun, (ignore the rotation of earth about-its own axis) Given that mass of the earth is $6 \times {10^{24}}\,kg$ and distance between the sun and earth is $1.5 \times {10^{11}}\,m$
explanation:
As $T=(2 \pi / \omega)$,
So $\omega=2 \pi /\left(3.15 \times 10^{7}\right)=1.99 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$
Now $v=r \omega=1.5 \times 10^{11} \times 1.99 \times 10^{-7}$
$ \approx 3 \times {10^4}\,m/s$
Now by work-energy theorem,
$W = {K_f} - {K_i} = 0 - \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$
$ = - \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times {10^{24}}{\left( {3 \times {{10}^4}} \right)^2} = - 2.7 \times {10^{33}}J$
subject: Physics

question: Under the acting of a force, a 2 $kg$  body move such that its position $x$ as a function of time $t$ is given by $x=t^{3} / 3$, where $x$ is in meters and $t$ in seconds. The work done by the force in the first two seconds is:
explanation:
$v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=\dfrac{d}{d t}\left(\dfrac{t^{3}}{3}\right)=t^{2}$
When $\mathrm{t}=0$, then $v=0$. When $\mathrm{t}=2$, then $v = 4\,m/s$
Work done in first two seconds = change in KE
$W=\dfrac{1}{2} m\left[(4)^{2}-(0)^{2}\right]=\dfrac{1}{2} 2 \times 16=16$
subject: Physics

explanation:
By conservation of energy,
$-2 m g x+\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}=0 \Rightarrow x=\dfrac{4 m g}{k}$
