question: A hammer of mass $M$ falls from a height $h$ repeatedly to drive a pile of mass $m$ in to the ground. The hammer makes the pile penetrate in the ground to a distance $d$ in single blow. Opposition to penetration is given by
explanation:
Using law of conservation of momentum, we get
$(M+m) v=M \sqrt{2 g h}$

$\Rightarrow v=\dfrac{M \sqrt{2 g h}}{M+m}$
Let the resistance be $\mathrm{F}$ then
Total work= change in K.E.
i.e., $\quad F=\dfrac{1}{2}(M+m) \dfrac{M^{2} 2 g h}{d(M+m)^{2}}+(M+m) g$
$=\dfrac{M^{2} g h}{(M+m) d}+(M+m) g$
subject: Physics

question: Two cylindrical vessel of equal cross-sectional area contain water upto height $h_{1}$ and $h_{2}$. The vessels are interconnected so that the levels in them become equal. The work done by the forces of gravity during the process is:
explanation:
When level becomes equal then
$h=\dfrac{h_{1}+h_{2}}{2}$
Work done by gravity $=$ change in potential energy of the water column
$\begin{aligned}& =2(A h \rho) g \dfrac{h}{2}-\left[A h_{1} \rho g \dfrac{h_{1}}{2}+A h_{2} \rho \dfrac{g h_{2}}{2}\right] \\& =A \rho g h^{2}-\left[A \rho g \dfrac{h_{1}^{2}}{2}+\dfrac{A \rho g h_{2}^{2}}{2}\right] \\& =A \rho g\left[\left(\dfrac{h_{1}+h_{2}}{2}\right)^{2}-\dfrac{h_{1}^{2}+h_{2}^{2}}{2}\right] \\& =A \rho g\left[\dfrac{h_{1}-h_{2}}{2}\right]^{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A variable force, given by the 2-dimensional vector $\vec{F}=\left(3 x^{2} \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}\right)$, acts on a particle. The force is in newton and $\mathrm{x}$ is in metre. What is the change in the kinetic energy of the particle as it moves from the point with coordinates $(2,3)$ to $(3,0)$ ( the coordinates are in metres)
explanation:
$\vec{F}=\left(3 x^{2} \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}\right), \vec{r}=x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}$
$\therefore d \vec{r}=d x \hat{i}+d y \hat{j}$
Work done,
$\begin{aligned}W & =\int \vec{F} \cdot d \vec{r}=\int_{(2,3)}^{(3,0)}\left(3 x^{2} \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}\right) \cdot(d x \hat{i}+d y \hat{j}) \\& =\int_{(2,3)}^{(3,0)}\left(3 x^{2} d x+4 d y\right)=\left[x^{3}+4 y\right]_{(2,3)}^{(3,0)} \\& =\left(3^{3}+4 \times 0\right)-\left(2^{3}+4 \times 3\right)=+7 J\end{aligned}$
Change in the kinetic energy $=$ Work done,
$\mathrm{W}=+7 \mathrm{~J}$
subject: Physics
topic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force
subtopic: The Work-Energy Theorem for a Constant/Variable Force

question: A particle (mass $m$ ) is projected with an initial velocity $v$ at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. What is the work done by the force of gravity from the instant of throwing to the instant it reaches the highest point?
explanation: $W_{m g}=K_{f}-K_{i}=\dfrac{1}{2} m(v \cos \theta)^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ $=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}\left[\cos ^{2} \theta-1\right]=-\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta$

question: A force of magnitude $F$ acts on the free end of the cord. The weight moves up slowly by a distance $h$. How much work is done on the weight by the rope connecting pulley and weight?
explanation:
We are applying force $F$ therefore tension in string is equal to $F$. Hence force acting on weight is 2 $F$ upward, and displacement is $h$ upward.
Hence $W = 2Fh$ (work done on weight)

question: A body of mass 10 $kg$ initially at rest acquires velocity 10 $m/s$. What is the work done ?
explanation:
By work -energy theorem
$W=\Delta K=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times(10)^{2}-0=500 J$

question: A body starts from rest and acquires a velocity $V$ in time $T$. The work done on the body in time $t$ will be proportional to :
explanation:
Acceleration of the body : $a=V / T$.
Velocity acquired in time $t$
$v=a t \Rightarrow v=\dfrac{V}{T} t$
$\Rightarrow \quad K \cdot E \propto v^{2}$
Work done on the body is equal to gain in the kinetic energy.
So Work done $\alpha \dfrac{V^{2} t^{2}}{T^{2}}$
subject: Physics

question: A body of mass 1 $kg$ is thrown upwards with a velocity 20 $m/s$. It momentarily comes to rest after attaining a height of 18 $m$. How much energy is lost due to air friction $(g = 10\,m/{s^2})$?
explanation:
Initial energy of body
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 1 \times(20)^{2}=200 \mathrm{~J}$.
A part of this energy consumes in doing work against gravitational force and remaining part consumes in doing work against air friction.
$\text { i.e., } \begin{aligned}& K . E=W_{\text {grav }}+W_{\text {airfricion }} \\& \Rightarrow 200=1 \times 10 \times 18+W_{\text {air }} \Rightarrow W_{\text {air }}=20 J .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A block of mass 5 $kg$ is resting on a smooth surface. At what angle a force of 20 $N$ be acted on the body so that it will acquire a kinetic energy of 40 $J$ after moving 4 $m$ ?
explanation:
According to work-energy theorem $W=$ Change in kinetic energy
$F S \cos \theta=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} m u^{2}$
Substituting the given values, we get
$\begin{aligned}& 20 \times 4 \times \cos \theta=40-0 \\& \cos \theta=\dfrac{40}{80}=\dfrac{1}{2} \\& \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)=60^{\circ}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

explanation:

question: A block of mass 1 $kg$ is pulled along the curved path $ACB$ by a tangential force as shown in figure. The work done by the frictional force when the block moves from $A$ to $B$ is
explanation:
Consider a small segment on the curve.
Let $\Delta S$ is the length of the segment https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956860/preptracker/questions/q_33476_explanation_1764956859995.png Small work done by the external force is
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta W=F \Delta S \\& F=m g \sin \theta+\mu m g \cos \theta \\& \Delta W=m g \Delta S \sin \theta+\mu m g \Delta S \cos \theta \\& \Delta W=m g \Delta y+\mu m g \Delta x \\& W=\int_{y_{A}}^{y_{B}} m g \Delta y+\mu m g \int_{x_{A}}^{x_{A}} \Delta x \\& W=m g(0)+\mu m g\left(x_{B}-x_{A}\right) \\& W=\mu m g(10 m) \\& W=0.2 \times 1 \times 10(10)=20 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A uniform chain of length $L$ and mass $M$ is lying on a smooth table and one third of its length is hanging vertically down over the edge of the table. If $g$ is acceleration due to gravity, the work required to pull the hanging part on the table is
explanation:
The weight of hanging part $\left(\dfrac{L}{3}\right)$ of chain is $\left(\dfrac{1}{3} M g\right)$. This weight acts at centre of gravity of the hanging part which is at a depth of $L$/6 from the table.
As Work done $=$ force $\times$ distance
$\therefore W=\dfrac{M g}{3} \times \dfrac{L}{6}=\dfrac{M g L}{18}$
subject: Physics

question: A particle moves under the effect of a force $F=C x$ from $x=0$ to $x=x_{1}$. The work done in the process is
explanation: The work done by the force is $W=\int_{0}^{x_{1}} F \cdot d x=\int_{0}^{x_{1}} C x d x=C\left[\dfrac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{x_{1}}=\dfrac{1}{2} C x_{1}^{2}$.
subject: Physics

question: A force $F=A y^{2}+B y+C$ acts on a body in the $y$-direction. The work done by this force during a displacement from $y=-a$ to $y=a$ is
explanation: $\begin{aligned}& W=\int F_{y} d y=\int_{-a}^{a}\left(A y^{2}+B y+C\right) d y \\& =A\left[\dfrac{y^{3}}{3}\right]_{-a}^{a}+B\left[\dfrac{y^{2}}{2}\right]_{-a}^{a}+C[y]_{-a}^{a} \\& W=\dfrac{2 A a^{3}}{3}+2 C a\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

$\vec{S}=4 \hat{k}$$\Rightarrow W=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{S}=(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \cdot 4 \hat{k}=12 J$

explanation:

question: An object of mass $m$ is tied to a string of length $L$ and a variable horizontal force is applied on it which starts at zero and gradually increases until the string makes an angle $\theta$ with the vertical. Work done by the force $F$ is
explanation:
The forces that act on the mass are $F$, $mg$ and tension ($T$). From work energy theorem
$\begin{aligned}& \sum W=\Delta K \\& W_{T}+W_{F}+W_{g}=0\end{aligned}$
$W_{T}=0$ since tension at a point is always perpendicular to the small displacement of the mass.
$\Rightarrow W_{F}=-W_{g}=\Delta U=m g L(1-\cos \theta)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: A body of mass $m$ is slowly pulled up the hill by a force $F$ which at each point was directed along the tangent of the trajectory as shown in figure. All surfaces are smooth. Find the work performed by this force.
explanation:
$\sum W_{n e t}=\Delta K$
As particle is moving slowly, this means
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta K=0 \\& W_{N}+W_{F}+W_{m g}=\Delta K\end{aligned}$
But $W_{N}=0$ as $\vec{N} \perp \overrightarrow{d r}$
$\begin{aligned}& 0+W_{F}-m g h=0 \\& W_{F}=m g h\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: A uniform rope of linear density $d$ and length $l$ is hanging from the edge of a table. The work done in pulling the rope on the table is
explanation:
Here, mass of rope $m=l \times d$
For pulling the rope on the table,
Distance of centre of gravity moved $=l / 2$
$\therefore$ Work done $=F \times \dfrac{l}{2}=l d g \times \dfrac{l}{2}=\dfrac{d g l^{2}}{2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: A position dependent force $F=7-2 x+3 x^{2}$ newton acts on a small body of mass 2 $kg$ and displace it from $x=0$ to $x=5 m$. The work done in joules is
explanation: $\begin{gathered}W=\int_{0}^{5} F d x=\int_{0}^{5}\left(7-2 x+3 x^{2}\right) d x=\left[7 x-x^{2}+x^{3}\right]_{0}^{5} \\W=135 J\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Variable Force

question: A force $\vec F = (5\hat i + 3\hat j + 2\hat k)N$ is applied over a particle which displaces it from its origin to the point$\vec r = (2\hat i - \hat j)m$. The work done on the particle in joule is
explanation:
$W=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{r}=(5 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j})$
$=10-3=7$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: A cord is used to lower vertically a block of mass $M$ by a distance $d$ with constant downward acceleration $\dfrac{g}{4}$. Work done by the cord on the block is
explanation:
When the block moves vertically downward with acceleration $\dfrac{g}{4}$ then tension in the cord
$T=M\left(g-\dfrac{g}{4}\right)=\dfrac{3}{4} M g$
Work done by the cord $\vec{F} \cdot \vec{S}=F S \cos \theta$
$\begin{aligned}& =T d \cos 180^{\circ} \\& =\left(-\dfrac{3}{4} M g\right) \times d=-3 M g \dfrac{d}{4} .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: The area of the acceleration-displacement curve of a body gives
explanation:
Area of acceleration-displacement curves gives change in $KE$ per unit mass
$\frac{1}{2}m\left( {{\upsilon ^2} - {u^2}} \right) = FS = \frac{{md\upsilon }}{{dt}} \times s$
$\therefore \,\frac{{Change\,in\,KE}}{{Mass}} = \frac{{d\upsilon }}{{dt}} \times s$

options.text: Change in KE per unit mass
Change in momentum per unit mass

subject: Physics
subtopic: Work done by a Constant Force

question: A man is rowing a boat upstream and inspite of that the boat is found to be not moving with respect to the bank. The work done by the man is
explanation: Absolute displacement of the point of application of force applied by the man through bars is zero. Hence work done is zero.

question:
Nature of work done by gravitational force
$A$. may be negative
B. may be positive
$C$. may be zero
$D$. always positive

question: The statements which show negative work are $A$. Work done by force of viscosity which increases with increase in velocity. $B$. Work done by attractive forces when particles are taken away from each other. $C$. Work done when applied force decreases kinetic energy of the body. $D$. Work done by gravity when a porter drops a suitcase from his head.
subject: Physics

question: One end of a spring of force constant $k_{1}$ is attached to the ceiling of an elevator. A block of mass 1.5 $kg$ is attached to the other end. Another spring of force constant $k_{2}$ is attached to the bottom of the mass and to the floor of the elevator as shown in figure. At equilibrium, the deformation in both the springs is equal and is 40 $cm$ . If the elevator moves with constant acceleration upward,the additional deformation in both the spring is 8 $cm$ . Find the elevator's acceleration $(g = 10\,\,m{s^{ - 2}})$.
explanation:
At equilibrium, $\left(k_{1}+k_{2}\right) x=m g$
$ \Rightarrow {k_1} + {k_2} = \frac{{mg}}{x}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\left( {x = 40\,cm,{x_1} = 48\,cm} \right){\rm{ }}$ In case of constant acceleration,$\begin{equation*}\left(k_{1}+k_{2}\right) x_{1}-m g=m a \tag{2}\end{equation*}$From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get $a=2 {~ms}^{-2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Work Energy Theorem with respect to a Moving Frame

question: Consider two observers moving with respect to each other at a speed $v$ along a straight line. They observe a block of mass m moving a distance $l$ on a rough surface. The following quantity will be same as observed by the observers
explanation: Kinetic energy of a body depends upon the reference frame and so does the work done. Since two observers are not accelerating w.r.t. each other so they will observe same force acting on mass and so they will observe same accelerations of block.

options.text: Total work done on the block
Kinetic energy of the block at time $t$

subject: Physics
subtopic: Work Energy Theorem with respect to a Moving Frame

question: A block of mass $m$ is lying at rest at point $P$ of a wedge having a smooth semi-circular track of radius $R$. What should be the minimum value of $a_{0}$ so that the mass can just reach point $Q$ ?
explanation:
From work energy theorem w.r.to wedge frame
$W_{F_{s}}=W_{g}=\Delta k=0$
$W_{F_{s}}=m a_{0} R \quad$ (work done by pseudo force)
$W_{g}=-m g R$ (work done by gravitational force)
$\begin{aligned}& m a_{0} R=m g R \\& a_{0}=g\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Dependance of Work on Frame

question: A block of mass $m$ is suspended by a light thread from an elevator. The elevator is accelerating upward with uniform acceleration $a$. The work done by tension on the block during $\mathrm{t}$ seconds from the ground frame and the lift frame are:
explanation:
From ground frame https://image.cleverb.in/upload/2023/07/05/ES-53_(2).png $T-m g=m a \Rightarrow T=m(g+a)$
and $s=\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2} \Rightarrow W=T s \cos 0^{\circ}$
$=m(g+a) \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$
From lift frame https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951199/preptracker/questions/q_34061_explanation_1764951199533.png As in lift frame block is at rest so $\mathrm{W}=0$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Dependance of Work on Frame

question:
A block of mass $m$ moving with a velocity $v_{0}$ on a smooth horizontal surface strikes and compresses a spring of stiffiness $k$ till mass comes to rest as shown in the figure. This phenomenon is observed by two observers:
$A$: Standing on the horizontal surface
$B$: Standing on the block W.r.to the observer $A$, the work done by spring force is:
explanation:
Observer $A$ is on the ground
$\begin{aligned}& W_{\text {spring }}=\Delta K \cdot E=0-\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2} \\& W_{\text {spring }}=-\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}\end{aligned}$

options.text: Negative but nothing can be said about its magnitude
Positive but nothing can be said about its magnitude

subject: Physics
subtopic: Dependance of Work on Frame
